The generic numbering mechanism allows you to:</p><ul><li>Automatically assign a unique number to the elements in the model when they are created, moved, or your model is refactored.</li><li>Easily identify and find an element in the model when communicating with colleagues or stakeholders.</li><li>Show element's place in a hierarchy.</li></ul><ac:structured-macro ac:name="info" ac:schema-version="1" ac:macro-id="7a60c08f-6ebe-4283-a166-55e4cf8ae3de"><ac:rich-text-body><p>For more information about the generic numbering mechanism and how to use it, see the <ac:link><ri:page ri:content-title="(2026x Refresh1) Generic numbering mechanism" /></ac:link> documentation.</p></ac:rich-text-body></ac:structured-macro><p>The default <ac:inline-comment-marker ac:ref="b837aa41-4b6a-43fa-8052-ea7293365d18">numbering</ac:inline-comment-marker> schemas can have two different numbering styles:</p><ul><li><strong>conse</strong><ac:inline-comment-marker ac:ref="bef9ad5d-ed4a-4f76-8438-cd73baa3324d"><strong>cutive</strong> - this style is to <span style="color:var(--ds-text,#172b4d);">number elements in one numbering level. Specify<span> </span></span><span style="color:var(--ds-text,#172b4d);">only one number part.</span></ac:inline-comment-marker></li><li><strong><ac:inline-comment-marker ac:ref="bef9ad5d-ed4a-4f76-8438-cd73baa3324d">multi-le</ac:inline-comment-marker>vel</strong> - this style is <span style="color:var(--ds-text,#172b4d);">to number elements in several numbering levels. Specify at least one number part for each numbering level. </span><span style="color:var(--ds-text,#172b4d);">Top level elements will be numbered using the first number part. Elements in subsequent levels (directly or indirectly contained in the top level element) will be numbered using successive number parts.</span></li></ul><ac:structured-macro ac:name="info" ac:schema-version="1" ac:macro-id="148ffb90-68bf-4b29-be24-74712b4da5db"><ac:rich-text-body><p>To learn more about the numbering styles, see <ac:link><ri:page ri:space-key="UPDG" ri:content-title="Numbering Scheme property values" /></ac:link> documentation.</p></ac:rich-text-body></ac:structured-macro><p><span style="letter-spacing: 0.0px;">UAF numbering uses <strong>multi-level</strong> numbering style schema property.</span></p><h2>UAF hierarchical numbering</h2><p>Numbering of UAF motivational elements and information <ac:inline-comment-marker ac:ref="8482ff7a-d0f3-4175-a938-7cd8dbbde8ef">elements is hierarchical. </ac:inline-comment-marker></p><p><ac:inline-comment-marker ac:ref="8482ff7a-d0f3-4175-a938-7cd8dbbde8ef">A hierarchical structure can only contain elements of the same type. Different element types cannot be mixed within the same hierarchy.</ac:inline-comment-marker></p><p><span style="color:var(--ds-text,#172b4d);">Motivational Elements:</span></p><ul style="text-align: left;"><li>Driver (prefix is D)</li><li>Opportunity (prefix is O)</li><li>Challenge (prefix is C)</li><li>Effect (prefix is E)</li></ul><p><span style="color:var(--ds-text,#172b4d);">Information Elements: </span></p><ul style="text-align: left;"><li>Operational Information (prefix is OI)</li><li>Resource Information (prefix is RI)</li><li>Strategic Information (prefix is SI)</li></ul><h2>UAF hierarchical numbering with dynamic prefixes</h2><p>Numbering of UAF <ac:inline-comment-marker ac:ref="78a84ff1-a965-4790-b198-6a7c92402db0">elements based on SysML v1 requirement</ac:inline-comment-marker>s is hierarchical or dynamic (when prefix differs, numbering adapts. numbers will change, but prefixes will be kept according to elements).</p><p>A hierarchical structure can contain elements of different type. For example, Enterprise Goal and Enterprise Objective can both be in the same hierarchical structure. The same is with the Security elements.</p><p><span style="color:var(--ds-text,#172b4d);">Strategic Elements:</span></p><ul style="text-align: left;"><li>Enterprise Goal (prefix is EG)</li><li>Enterprise Objective (prefix is EO)</li></ul><p><span style="color:var(--ds-text,#172b4d);">Security Elements:</span></p><ul style="text-align: left;"><li>Security Control (prefix is SC)</li><li>Enhanced Security Control (prefix is ESC)</li><li>Security Control Family (prefix is SCF)</li></ul><p><ac:image ac:align="center"><ri:attachment ri:filename="hierarchical_element_numbering.png"><ri:page ri:content-title="(2026x Refresh1) UAF element numbering" /></ri:attachment></ac:image></p></ac:layout-cell></ac:layout-section><ac:layout-section ac:type="single"><ac:layout-cell><p><br /></p></ac:layout-cell></ac:layout-section><ac:layout-section ac:type="single"><ac:layout-cell><p><strong>Related pages</strong></p><ul><li><ac:link><ri:page ri:content-title="(2026x Refresh1) Generic numbering mechanism" /></ac:link></li></ul></ac:layout-cell></ac:layout-section></ac:layout>

This manual aims to help you understand the differences between UAF specifications and guide you along the data mapping process.

The following chapters describe how migration works.

## PAGE 01960: (2026x Refresh1) UAF specific elements

- page_id: `304000752`
- space_key: `MT`
- source_url: https://docs.nomagic.com/spaces/MT/pages/304000752/2026x+Refresh1+UAF+specific+elements
- version_number: 1
- version_date: `2026-04-28T10:32:38.983+02:00`
- ancestors: Versions of Modeling Tools Documentation > (2026x Refresh1) Modeling Tools Documentation > (2026x Refresh1) UAF 1.3 modeling
- labels: ['msosa']

### NORMALIZED CONTENT

| Metaclass | Name | Description |
| --- | --- | --- |
| Association | Operational Association | A relationship summarizing Operational Exchanges between connected Operational Performers. |
| Resource Association | A relationship summarizing Resource Exchanges between connected Resources. |  |
| Call Behavior Action | Operational Action | A Call Behavior Action for which the default type will not be created. However if you need to create or set a type for this element, you can select or create only an Operational Activity. |
| Resource Action | A Call Behavior Action for which the default type will not be created. However if you need to create or set a type for this element, you can select or create only a Function. |  |
| Service Action | A Call Behavior Action for which the default type will not be created. However if you need to create or set a type for this element, you can select or create only a Service Function. |  |
| Class | Time Period | An element which contains Date elements. |
| Dependency | Arbitrary Relationship Dashed Left | A relationship which you can used in a Free Form diagram to show dependencies. |
| Arbitrary Relationship Dashed Right |  |  |
| Arbitrary Relationship Directed Left |  |  |
| Arbitrary Relationship Directed Right |  |  |
| Arbitrary Relationship Solid |  |  |
| Property | Agreement Concept Role | A Concept Role which type is Agreement. |
| Capability Configuration Concept Role | A Concept Role which type is Capability Configuration. |  |
| Condition Concept Role | A Concept Role which type is Condition. |  |
| Environment Concept Role | A Concept Role which type is Environment. |  |
| Location Concept Role | A Concept Role which type is Location. |  |
| Natural Resource Concept Role | A Concept Role which type is Natural Resource. |  |
| Operational Concept Role | A Concept Role which type is Operational Performer. |  |
| Organization Concept Role | A Concept Role which type is Organization. |  |
| Person Concept Role | A Concept Role which type is Person. |  |
| Post Concept Role | A Concept Role which type is Post. |  |
| Resource Artifact Concept Role | A Concept Role which type is Resource Artifact. |  |
| Software Concept Role | A Concept Role which type is Software. |  |
| Technology Concept Role | A Concept Role which type is Technology. |  |

## PAGE 01961: (2026x Refresh1) UAF validation rules

- page_id: `304000762`
- space_key: `MT`
- source_url: https://docs.nomagic.com/spaces/MT/pages/304000762/2026x+Refresh1+UAF+validation+rules

<!--NOMAGIC_PAGE id=303999759 space=MT version=1 -->
## PAGE 01962: (2026x Refresh1) UML 2.5.1 support

- page_id: `303999759`
- space_key: `MT`
- source_url: https://docs.nomagic.com/spaces/MT/pages/303999759/2026x+Refresh1+UML+2.5.1+support
- version_number: 1
- version_date: `2026-04-28T10:31:14.763+02:00`
- ancestors: Versions of Modeling Tools Documentation > (2026x Refresh1) Modeling Tools Documentation > (2026x Refresh1) General modeling features
- labels: ['msosa', 'mcse', 'msa']

### NORMALIZED CONTENT

771348400

771348405

771348403

Our modeling tools always support the latest versions of UML standards. Version 19.0 supports changes in the UML specification from version 2.5 to 2.5.1.

UML 2.5.1 provides several minor enhancements:

- Vertexes are redefinable in State Machines. A vertex is either a State, or Pseudo State, or Connection Point Reference. In earlier standard versions, only the State was redefinable.
- The semantic of Context and Constrained Element properties of the Constraint are clarified in the standard specification.
- The Behavior of an Opaque Expression is now allowed to have input parameters.

The latter two items do not affect the modeling tool functionality.

771348389

**Related pages**

- [CONFLUENCE_PAGE title='(2026x Refresh1) Profiling changes' space='']

### EXACT CONFLUENCE STORAGE SOURCE

````html

Version 19.0 supports changes in the UML specification from version 2.5 to 2.5.1.</p><p>UML 2.5.1 provides several minor enhancements:</p><ul><li>Vertexes are redefinable in State Machines. A vertex is either a State, or Pseudo State, or Connection Point Reference. In earlier standard versions, only the State was redefinable. </li><li>The semantic of Context and Constrained Element properties of the Constraint are clarified in the standard specification.</li><li>The Behavior of an Opaque Expression is now allowed to have input parameters.</li></ul><p>The latter two items do not affect the modeling tool functionality. </p></ac:rich-text-body></ac:structured-macro><ac:structured-macro ac:name="content-block" ac:schema-version="1" ac:macro-id="0ec2eed8-72b4-461b-9616-b9fb1b57c8f2"><ac:parameter ac:name="id">771348389</ac:parameter><ac:rich-text-body><p><strong>Related pages</strong></p><ul><li><ac:link><ri:page ri:content-title="(2026x Refresh1) Profiling changes" /></ac:link></li></ul></ac:rich-text-body></ac:structured-macro></ac:rich-text-body></ac:structured-macro></ac:rich-text-body></ac:structured-macro>
````

<!--NOMAGIC_PAGE id=303990027 space=MT version=1 -->
## PAGE 01963: (2026x Refresh1) UML diagrams

- page_id: `303990027`
- space_key: `MT`
- source_url: https://docs.nomagic.com/spaces/MT/pages/303990027/2026x+Refresh1+UML+diagrams
- version_number: 1
- version_date: `2026-04-28T10:19:02.503+02:00`
- ancestors: Versions of Modeling Tools Documentation > (2026x Refresh1) Modeling Tools Documentation > (2026x Refresh1) General modeling features > (2026x Refresh1) Diagrams
- labels: ['msosa', 'mcse', 'msa']

### NORMALIZED CONTENT

In software development, the diagram is the equivalent of a blueprint. To meet varying user requirements, we often need several different "blueprints" of the same system. Every system is described by different aspects, such as:

- Structural
- Behavioral

Structure and Behavior diagrams are comprised of different types of the UML diagrams.

###### [IMAGE alt='' src='']Different types of the UML diagrams.

The modeling tool supports the following diagrams defined in UML 2:

|  |  |  |  |
| --- | --- | --- | --- |
|  |  |  |  |
|  |  |  |  |
|  |  |  |  |

## PAGE 01965: (2026x Refresh1) UML to SQL transformation

- page_id: `303995891`
- space_key: `MT`
- source_url: https://docs.nomagic.com/spaces/MT/pages/303995891/2026x+Refresh1+UML+to+SQL+transformation
- version_number: 1
- version_date: `2026-04-28T10:26:12.265+02:00`
- ancestors: Versions of Modeling Tools Documentation > (2026x Refresh1) Modeling Tools Documentation > (2026x Refresh1) Data-related modeling > (2026x Refresh1) Transformations
- labels: ['msosa']

### NORMALIZED CONTENT

237093793

237093795

237093792

true

237093794

There are two very similar UML to DDL transformations:

1. UML to SQL (Generic)
2. UML to SQL (Oracle)

These transformations convert the selected part of a UML model with class diagrams into Generic or Oracle SQL models with Oracle SQL diagrams respectively.

### EXACT CONFLUENCE STORAGE SOURCE


<!--NOMAGIC_PAGE id=303995881 space=MT version=1 -->
## PAGE 01966: (2026x Refresh1) UML to XML schema transformation

- page_id: `303995881`
- space_key: `MT`
- source_url: https://docs.nomagic.com/spaces/MT/pages/303995881/2026x+Refresh1+UML+to+XML+schema+transformation
- version_number: 1
- version_date: `2026-04-28T10:26:11.448+02:00`
- ancestors: Versions of Modeling Tools Documentation > (2026x Refresh1) Modeling Tools Documentation > (2026x Refresh1) Data-related modeling > (2026x Refresh1) Transformations
- labels: ['msosa']

### NORMALIZED CONTENT

34012851

34012854

34012852

The UML to XML Schema transformation helps create an equivalent XML schema model from a given UML model. Basically, this transformation creates a copy of a source UML model, then applies the necessary stereotypes according to the XML schema modeling rules.

##### **Type mapping**

This type map stores mapping between primitive UML data types and primitive XML Schema data types.

[IMAGE alt='' src='']

###### UML to XML schema type map.

###### [IMAGE alt='' src='']UML to XML schema type map (Continued).

##### **Transformation results**

After the UML models have been transformed to XML schema models, you will see the following results.

- For each class in the transformation destination set, the «XSDcomplexType» stereotype is applied, unless this class is derived from simple XML type (one of the basic types, or type, stereotyped with XSDsimpleType). In that case, an «XSDsimpleType» stereotype is applied. There are conditions when the «XSDcomplexType» and «XSDsimpleType» stereotypes are used, listed in the table below:

| Condition | Result |
| --- | --- |
| The class is derived from another class, stereotyped as «XSDcomplexType». | The «XSDcomplexContent» stereotype is applied on this class with «XSDextension» on the corresponding generalization relationship. |
| The class is derived from another class, stereotyped as «XSDsimpleType». | The «XSDrestriction» stereotype is applied on the corresponding generalization relationship. |
| The class is not derived from anything, and has attributes with the XSDelement tag. | The «XSDcomplexContent» stereotype is applied on this class. |
| The class is not derived from anything, and has no attributes with the XSDelement tag. | No «XXXXContent» stereotype is applied on this class - the class has an empty content. |
| The UML datatypes in the transformation source set are transformed into the classes with the «XSDsimpleType» stereotype (unless after the type map this class appears to be derived from a class with the «XSDcomplexType» stereotype). | The «XSDcomplexType» stereotype is used. |
| Each attribute of the class is not of the simple XML type (that is, one of the basic types, or type, stereotyped with the «XSDsimpleType») or has a multiplicity more than 1. | The «XSDelement» stereotype is applied. |

- For each composition association, two linking classes are stereotyped as XML schema types. The stereotype on the association end is applied the same as the rules for attributes.
- Enumerations in the UML model are transformed into the enumerations in the XML Schema model. Classes with the «XSDsimpleType» stereotype are derived by restriction from the XML string type, where all the elements of the original enumeration are converted into the attributes with an «XSDenumeration» stereotype.
- For each package in the transformation set, the «XSDnamespace» stereotype is applied.
- In each package, one additional class for the XML schema is created. The name of the schema class is constructed by taking the name of the package and then appending the .xsd to it (e.g. if the package in the source model set is named "user", name the schema class "user.xsd" in the destination package).
- The targetNamespace value is added to the schema class with the name of its parent (e.g. if the schema is placed in the "http://magicdraw.com/User" package, the targetNamespace=" http://magicdraw.com/User" is set on the schema class).
- Schema class and the namespaces http://www.w3c.org/2001/XMLSchema [XML Schema profile] and its target namespace are linked using the xmlns relationships. The names of these links are: the same as the target namespace for the link to the target namespace; and "xs" for the XML Schema namespace.
- Class diagrams are transformed into XML Schema diagrams.
- The model elements that have no meaning in the XML schemas are discarded. This includes (without limitation) behavioral features of classes, interfaces, actors, use cases, states, activities, objects, messages, stereotypes and tag definitions.
- There are additional properties to choose for UML to XML Schema transformation in the Model Transformation Wizard. For more information about the wizard, see [CONFLUENCE_PAGE title='(2026x Refresh1) Model Transformation Wizard' space=''] in MagicDraw User Guide.

###### [IMAGE alt='' src='']Model transformation wizard for UML to XML schema transformation with Specify transformation details.

| Option name | Type | Description |
| --- | --- | --- |
| Default Compositor | Combo box | Possible choices: XSDall, XSDchoice, XSDsequenceDetermines element grouping in complex types of XML Schema.Default: XSDall |
| Default Attribute Kind | Combo box | Determines which attribute kind (XSDelement or XSDattribute UML) the attribute will be mapped to.Default: XSDelement |

919614709

**Related page**

- [CONFLUENCE_PAGE title='(2026x Refresh1) Model Transformation Wizard' space='']

In that case, an «XSDsimpleType» stereotype is applied. There are conditions when the «XSDcomplexType» and «XSDsimpleType» stereotypes are used, listed in the table below:</li></ul><table class="relative-table wrapped" style="width: 99.9451%;"><colgroup><col style="width: 52.3595%;" /><col style="width: 47.6405%;" /></colgroup><tbody><tr><th>Condition</th><th>Result</th></tr><tr><td>The class is derived from another class, stereotyped as «XSDcomplexType».</td><td>The «XSDcomplexContent» stereotype is applied on this class with «XSDextension» on the corresponding generalization relationship.</td></tr><tr><td>The class is derived from another class, stereotyped as «XSDsimpleType».</td><td>The «XSDrestriction» stereotype is applied on the corresponding generalization relationship.</td></tr><tr><td>The class is not derived from anything, and has attributes with the <strong>XSDelement </strong>tag.</td><td>The «XSDcomplexContent» stereotype is applied on this class.</td></tr><tr><td>The class is not derived from anything, and has no attributes with the <strong>XSDelement </strong>tag.</td><td>No «XXXXContent» stereotype is applied on this class - the class has an empty content.</td></tr><tr><td>The UML datatypes in the transformation source set are transformed into the classes with the «XSDsimpleType» stereotype (unless after the type map this class appears to be derived from a class with the «XSDcomplexType» stereotype).</td><td>The «XSDcomplexType» stereotype is used.</td></tr><tr><td>Each attribute of the class is not of the simple XML type (that is, one of the basic types, or type, stereotyped with the «XSDsimpleType») or has a multiplicity more than 1.</td><td>The «XSDelement» stereotype is applied.</td></tr></tbody></table><ul><li>For each composition association, two linking classes are stereotyped as XML schema types. The stereotype on the association end is applied the same as the rules for attributes.</li><li>Enumerations in the UML model are transformed into the enumerations in the XML Schema model. Classes with the «XSDsimpleType» stereotype are derived by restriction from the XML string type, where all the elements of the original enumeration are converted into the attributes with an «XSDenumeration» stereotype.</li><li>For each package in the transformation set, the «XSDnamespace» stereotype is applied.</li><li>In each package, one additional class for the XML schema is created. The name of the schema class is constructed by taking the name of the package and then appending the .xsd to it (e.g. if the package in the source model set is named &quot;user&quot;, name the schema class &quot;user.xsd&quot; in the destination package).</li><li>The targetNamespace value is added to the schema class with the name of its parent (e.g. if the schema is placed in the <a href="http://magicdraw.com/User">&quot;http://magicdraw.com/User&quot; </a>package, the targetNamespace=&quot; <a href="http://magicdraw.com/User">http://magicdraw.com/User&quot; </a>is set on the schema class).</li><li>Schema class and the namespaces <a href="http://www.w3c.org/2001/XMLSchema">http://www.w3c.org/2001/XMLSchema </a>[XML Schema profile] and its target namespace are linked using the xmlns relationships. The names of these links are: the same as the target namespace for the link to the target namespace; and &quot;xs&quot; for the XML Schema namespace.</li><li>Class diagrams are transformed into XML Schema diagrams.</li><li>The model elements that have no meaning in the XML schemas are discarded. This includes (without limitation) behavioral features of classes, interfaces, actors, use cases, states, activities, objects, messages, stereotypes and tag definitions.</li><li>There are additional properties to choose for UML to XML Schema transformation in the <strong>Model Transformation Wizard. </strong>For more information about the wizard, see <ac:link><ri:page ri:content-title="(2026x Refresh1) Model Transformation Wizard" /></ac:link> in MagicDraw User Guide.</li></ul><p><br /></p><h6 style="text-align: center;"><ac:image ac:align="center"><ri:attachment ri:filename="Figure 45 Model Transformation Wizard for UML to XML Schema Transformation Specify Transformation Details.png"><ri:page ri:content-title="(2026x Refresh1) UML to XML schema transformation" /></ri:attachment></ac:image>Model transformation wizard for UML to XML schema transformation with Specify transformation details.</h6><p><br /></p><table class="relative-table wrapped" style="width: 99.0675%;"><colgroup><col style="width: 13.4017%;" /><col style="width: 12.903%;" /><col style="width: 73.6953%;" /></colgroup><tbody><tr><th>Option name</th><th>Type</th><th>Description</th></tr><tr><td><p><strong>Default Compositor</strong></p></td><td><strong>Combo box</strong></td><td><p>Possible choices: XSDall, XSDchoice, XSDsequence</p><p>Determines element grouping in complex types of XML Schema.</p><p>Default: XSDall</p></td></tr><tr><td><p><strong>Default Attribute Kind</strong></p></td><td><strong>Combo box</strong></td><td><p>Determines which attribute kind (XSDelement or XSDattribute UML) the attribute will be mapped to.</p><p>Default: XSDelement</p></td></tr></tbody></table></ac:rich-text-body></ac:structured-macro><ac:structured-macro ac:name="content-block" ac:schema-version="1" ac:macro-id="b195925f-3658-4516-bba8-89172281d634"><ac:parameter ac:name="id">919614709</ac:parameter><ac:rich-text-body><p><strong>Related page</strong></p><ul><li><ac:link><ri:page ri:content-title="(2026x Refresh1) Model Transformation Wizard" /></ac:link></li></ul></ac:rich-text-body></ac:structured-macro></ac:rich-text-body></ac:structured-macro></ac:rich-text-body></ac:structured-macro>

<!--NOMAGIC_PAGE id=303997051 space=MT version=1 -->
## PAGE 01968: (2026x Refresh1) Understanding cell content

- page_id: `303997051`
- space_key: `MT`
- source_url: https://docs.nomagic.com/spaces/MT/pages/303997051/2026x+Refresh1+Understanding+cell+content
- version_number: 1
- version_date: `2026-04-28T10:27:47.129+02:00`
- ancestors: Versions of Modeling Tools Documentation > (2026x Refresh1) Modeling Tools Documentation > (2026x Refresh1) General modeling features > (2026x Refresh1) Diagrams > (2026x Refresh1) Analysis diagrams > (2026x Refresh1) Dependency Matrix > (2026x Refresh1) Using Dependency Matrix
- labels: ['msosa', 'mcse', 'msa']

### NORMALIZED CONTENT

This page explains the content of the cell in the Dependency matrix. The following sections describe the Dependency matrix areas and cell content:

43

##### Rows, columns, and cells in the Dependency matrix

The Dependency matrix contains the following areas:

- Rows display elements specified in the Row Element Type box and Row Scope box of the [CONFLUENCE_PAGE title='(2026x Refresh1) Dependency Matrix Criteria area' space=''] .
- Columns display elements specified in the Column Element Type box and Column Scope box of the [CONFLUENCE_PAGE title='(2026x Refresh1) Dependency Matrix Criteria area' space=''] .
- *Cells* display dependencies between elements specified in the rows and columns. The content of the cells depends on selected criteria in the **Dependency Criteria** box and the **Direction** box of the [CONFLUENCE_PAGE title='(2026x Refresh1) Dependency Matrix Criteria area' space='']. Tip[CONFLUENCE_PAGE title='(2026x Refresh1) Selecting criteria' space='']]]> [IMAGE alt='' src='']

###### The main areas of the Dependency matrix. The illustration displays concepts from the SysML v1 Plugin.

##### Representation of the dependencies in a cell

The default representation of the dependencies in the cells includes the following:

- Arrowed diagonal . The direction of the arrow is from the client element to the supplier element.

#### NOTE: Arrowed diagonal representation

Arrowed diagonal representation

The representation of the arrowed diagonal is different when the same elements are connected through:

- One dependency , e.g., [ATTACHMENT filename='one_way_arrow.png'] .
- Two dependencies, e.g., [ATTACHMENT filename='two_arrows.png'] and [ATTACHMENT filename='two_arrows_different_direction.png'] .
- More than two dependencies, e.g., [ATTACHMENT filename='one_way_arrow_with_number.png'] , [ATTACHMENT filename='two_way_arrow_with_number.png'] .

- Diagonal . Elements depend on each other.

#### TIP: Tip

Tip

You can specify the colors and line styles used to represent the relationships in your dependency matrices. [CONFLUENCE_PAGE title='(2026x Refresh1) Specifying relationship line styles' space='']]]>

##### Numbers in the Dependency matrix

There are three types of numbers in the matrix (see the following figure):

1. The number of dependencies in the row.
2. The number of dependencies in the column.
3. The number of criteria in the cell.

[IMAGE alt='' src='']

##### The details of a cell

Move your mouse over a cell to view details of the dependency (relationship) represented in the cell. A tooltip displays the information about the relationships.

[IMAGE alt='' src='']

###### The ToolTip displays information about existing relations between Over Pressure Valve and Anto Black-flow Valve Blocks. The illustration displays concepts from SysML v1 Plugin.

**Related pages**

- [CONFLUENCE_PAGE title='(2026x Refresh1) Creating Dependency Matrix' space='']
- [CONFLUENCE_PAGE title='(2026x Refresh1) Using Dependency Matrix' space='']
- [CONFLUENCE_PAGE title='(2026x Refresh1) Dependency Matrix environment' space='']

**Sample model**

The model used in the figures of this page is the**dependencyMatrixSample**model. Download[ATTACHMENT filename='dependencyMatrixSample.mdzip'].

<!--NOMAGIC_PAGE id=303997411 space=MT version=1 -->
## PAGE 01969: (2026x Refresh1) Understanding change concept

- page_id: `303997411`
- space_key: `MT`
- source_url: https://docs.nomagic.com/spaces/MT/pages/303997411/2026x+Refresh1+Understanding+change+concept
- version_number: 1
- version_date: `2026-04-28T10:28:08.601+02:00`
- ancestors: Versions of Modeling Tools Documentation > (2026x Refresh1) Modeling Tools Documentation > (2026x Refresh1) General modeling features > (2026x Refresh1) Tools > (2026x Refresh1) Model Merge
- labels: ['change-concept', 'change-type', 'addition-change', 'deletion-change', 'modification-change', 'movement-change', 'order-change', 'change-states', 'dependent-changes', 'conflicting-changes', 'equivalent-changes', 'msosa', 'mcse', 'msa']

### NORMALIZED CONTENT

Change is a difference, found between the ancestor and a contributor. Every change can be accepted or rejected, as well as have dependent changes. Changes can conflict with each other or be equivalent. Merging begins with building a composite change tree, which consists of a model, diagramming, and non-model changes.

Read the following section to get familiar with different change types.

##### **#additionchange**Addition change

If an element has been added to a contributor, an addition change occurs.

##### **#deletionchange**Deletion change

If an element has been removed from a contributor, a deletion change occurs.

##### **#modificationchange**Modification change

If an element property in a contributor has been modified, a modification change occurs.

If the **Is****Abstract** property value of a class in the ancestor had the default value *false* and the same property value in a contributor has been changed to *true*, a modification change occurs.

There are three types of modification changes:

- Addition modification change that occurs when a value is added to a property.
- Deletion modification change that occurs when a value is removed from a property.
- Replacement modification change that occurs when one value is replaced with another. This type of modification change occurs only for properties that have multiplicity less or equal to 1.

##### **#movementchange**Movement change

If an element owner has been changed in a contributor, a movement change occurs.

Let's say package A contains some class in the ancestor and package B contains the same class in a contributor. This means that the class has been moved from package A to package B in the contributor. This case is recognized as a movement change.

Another case of the movement change is when an attribute or an operation that has been owned by class A in the ancestor, becomes the attribute or an operation of class B in a contributor.

##### **#orderchange**Order change

If the order of elements has been changed in a contributor, an order change occurs. Order changes can occur on elements such as attributes, operations, and other ordered elements. Even if a single element in a collection has changed its place, the order change is applied to the entire collection. 
 
Since an element can have several ordered collections, several order changes can occur on a single element.

Let's say class A has attributed a, b, and c in the ancestor. The attribute c has been moved up and placed above attribute a in a contributor. This means that the order of attribute collection in class A has changed in the contributor. This is a case of the order change.

Order changes can be skipped while merging. For this, you need to specify the names of the properties whose order changes should not be detected in the**Do Not Detect Order Changes for**environment option. For more information, see[CONFLUENCE_PAGE title='(2026x Refresh1) Preparing for merge' space=''].

##### **#changestates**Change states

Every change, whether it is addition, modification, deletion, movement, or order change, can be either accepted or rejected. It is a change state.

All accepted changes will be incorporated into the target. Alternatively, they can be rejected and excluded from the target.

##### **#dependentchanges**Dependent changes

In some cases, other changes have to be accepted or rejected before accepting or rejecting a selected change. In other words, a selected change sometimes depends on other changes and is called a dependent change.

For a better understanding of the concept of dependent changes, study the following examples.

Let's say a class attribute type has been changed to a type that had been created by another change. In consequence, the attribute type change depends on the change that has created the type. This means that type creation change must be accepted before accepting type modification change.

Let's suppose there is an attribute type change in a contributor. An old type has been deleted and a new type has been added to the contributor. In this case, three changes occur:

- deletion change (for the old type)
- addition change (for the new type)
- modification change (for the property type)

These are also ownership changes, but they are accepted together with deletion and addition changes. 
The modification change depends on the addition change, and the deletion change depends on the modification change.Thus accepting the deletion change means also accepting the addition change, the modification change and the deletion change itself.

##### Conflicting changes

Conflict occurs when two changes are incompatible, i.e., the changes cannot be accepted together. Conflicts can only occur when using 3-way merge.

The following situations will result in a conflict:

- Each contributor has changed a class name or any other element property value.
- One contributor has added an operation to a class and the other contributor deleted the class.
- One contributor has moved a class into one package while the other contributor moved it to another package.

##### **#equivalentchanges**Equivalent changes

Equivalent change is a pair of identical changes that are detected in both source and target when performing a 3-way merge.

Let's say package A contains some class in the ancestor and both contributors contain the same class in package B. This means that the class has been moved from package A to package B in both source and target. This is a case of the equivalent change.

Another case of equivalent changes is when the same element A becomes renamed to B in both contributors.

**Related pages**

- [CONFLUENCE_PAGE title='(2026x Refresh1) Understanding merge types' space='']
- [CONFLUENCE_PAGE title='(2026x Refresh1) Preparing for merge' space='']
- [CONFLUENCE_PAGE title='(2026x Refresh1) Starting Model Merge' space='']
- [CONFLUENCE_PAGE title='(2026x Refresh1) Analyzing and managing merge results' space='']
- [CONFLUENCE_PAGE title='(2026x Refresh1) Finishing and canceling merge' space='']

### EXACT CONFLUENCE STORAGE SOURCE

````html

Every change can be accepted or rejected, as well as have dependent changes. Changes can conflict with each other or be equivalent. <span style="letter-spacing: 0.0px;">Merging begins with building a composite change tree, which consists of a model, diagramming, and non-model changes.</span></p><p>Read the following section to get familiar with different change types.<span style="color: rgb(129,137,156);"> </span></p><h3><span style="color: ac:name="">#additionchange</ac:parameter></ac:structured-macro></strong></span><span style="letter-spacing: -0.006em;">Addition change</span></h3><p>If an element has been added to a contributor, an addition change occurs.<span style="color: rgb(129,137,156);"> <br /></span></p><h3><span style="color: rgb(129,137,156);"><strong><ac:structured-macro ac:name="anchor" ac:schema-version="1" ac:macro-id="5eb13209-1e68-445b-9dd5-11f1bcda8973"><ac:parameter ac:name="">#deletionchange</ac:parameter></ac:structured-macro></strong></span><span style="letter-spacing: -0.006em;">Deletion change</span></h3><p>If an element has been removed from a contributor, a deletion change occurs.  <span style="color: rgb(129,137,156);"><br /></span></p><h3><span style="color: rgb(129,137,156);"><strong><ac:structured-macro ac:name="anchor" ac:schema-version="1" ac:macro-id="0ac23d21-cc7f-4937-88b3-42e018befea7"><ac:parameter been modified, a modification change occurs.</p><ac:structured-macro ac:name="tip" ac:schema-version="1" ac:macro-id="fe88152e-95da-4b3d-ae50-91886444d127"><ac:rich-text-body><p>If the <strong>Is</strong><strong>Abstract</strong> property value of a class in the ancestor had the default value <em>false</em> and the same property value in a contributor has been changed to <em>true</em>, a modification change occurs.</p></ac:rich-text-body></ac:structured-macro><p>There are three types of modification changes:</p><ul><li><strong>Addition modification change</strong> that occurs when a value is added to a property.</li><li><strong>Deletion modification change</strong> that occurs when a value is removed from a property.</li><li><strong>Replacement modification change</strong> that occurs when one value is replaced with another. This type of modification change occurs only for properties that have multiplicity less or equal to 1.<span style="color: rgb(129,137,156);"> </span></li></ul><h3><span style="color: rgb(129,137,156);"><strong><ac:structured-macro ac:name="anchor" ac:schema-version="1" ac:macro-id="07b2e240-b7fc-4685-baf7-25e88052aa5b"><ac:parameter ac:name="">#movementchange</ac:parameter></ac:structured-macro></strong></span><span style="letter-spacing: -0.006em;">Movement change</span></h3><p>If an element owner has been changed in a contributor, a movement change occurs.</p><ac:structured-macro ac:name="tip" ac:schema-version="1" ac:macro-id="89c94be7-2628-430f-a0ee-5caf9c4bb81f"><ac:rich-text-body><p>Let's say package A contains some class in the ancestor and package B contains the same class in a contributor. This means that the class has been moved from package A to package B in the contributor. This case is recognized as a movement change.</p></ac:rich-text-body></ac:structured-macro><ac:structured-macro ac:name="tip" ac:schema-version="1" ac:macro-id="f15971ff-0eb5-41f1-ab0b-140458a12568"><ac:rich-text-body><p>Another case of the movement change is when an attribute or an operation that has been owned by class A in the ancestor, becomes the attribute or an operation of class B in a contributor.<span style="color: rgb(129,137,156);"> </span></p></ac:rich-text-body></ac:structured-macro><h3><span style="color: rgb(129,137,156);"><strong><ac:structured-macro ac:name="anchor" ac:schema-version="1" ac:macro-id="09c18b65-c5d0-4e7d-8e62-a769a7a93f79"><ac:parameter ac:name="">#orderchange</ac:parameter></ac:structured-macro></strong></span><span style="letter-spacing: -0.006em;">Order change</span></h3><p>If the order of elements has been changed in a contributor, an order change occurs. Order changes can occur on elements such as attributes, operations, and other ordered elements. Even if a single element in a collection has changed its place, the order change is applied to the entire collection.<br /><br />Since an element can have several ordered collections, several order changes can occur on a single element.</p><ac:structured-macro ac:name="tip" ac:schema-version="1" ac:macro-id="fb4a789a-a329-4927-aed5-dd1866d04476"><ac:rich-text-body><p>Let's say class A has attributed a, b, and c in the ancestor. The attribute c has been moved up and placed above attribute a in a contributor. This means that the order of attribute collection in class A has changed in the contributor. This is a case of the order change.</p></ac:rich-text-body></ac:structured-macro><p class="auto-cursor-target"><span style="letter-spacing: 0.0px;">Order changes can be skipped while merging. For this, you need to specify the names of the properties whose order changes should not be detected in the </span><strong style="letter-spacing: 0.0px;">Do Not Detect Order Changes for</strong><span style="letter-spacing: 0.0px;"> environment option. For more information, see </span><ac:link><ri:page ri:content-title="(2026x Refresh1) Preparing for merge" /></ac:link><span style="letter-spacing: 0.0px;">.</span></p><h3><strong><ac:structured-macro ac:name="anchor" ac:schema-version="1" ac:macro-id="5cd9ebef-efce-404e-89be-4c75737fc847"><ac:parameter ac:name="">#changestates</ac:parameter></ac:structured-macro></strong>Change states</h3><p>Every change, whether it is addition, modification, deletion, movement, or order change, can be either accepted or rejected. It is a change state.</p><p>All accepted changes will be incorporated into the target. Alternatively, they can be rejected and excluded from the target.</p><h3><strong><ac:structured-macro ac:name="anchor" ac:schema-version="1" ac:macro-id="aa850e29-3bd2-4449-8a71-f33e8fafb206"><ac:parameter ac:name="">#dependentchanges</ac:parameter></ac:structured-macro></strong>Dependent changes</h3><p>In some cases, other changes have to be accepted or rejected before accepting or rejecting a selected change. In other words, a selected change sometimes depends on other changes and is called a dependent change.</p><p>For a better understanding of the concept of dependent changes, study the following examples.</p><ac:structured-macro ac:name="tip" ac:schema-version="1" ac:macro-id="2e231999-6ec7-43d1-aa0a-7bbbb69b45ae"><ac:rich-text-body><p>Let's say a class attribute type has been changed to a type that had been created by another change. In consequence, the attribute type change depends on the change that has created the type. This means that type creation change must be accepted before accepting type modification change.</p></ac:rich-text-body></ac:structured-macro><ac:structured-macro ac:name="tip" ac:schema-version="1" ac:macro-id="022cfc69-c633-42b1-be98-179ea40707d4"><ac:rich-text-body><p>Let's suppose there is an attribute type change in a contributor. An old type has been deleted and a new type has been added to the contributor. In this case, three changes occur:</p><ul><li>deletion change (for the old type)</li><li>addition change (for the new type)</li><li>modification change (for the property type)</li></ul><p>These are also ownership changes, but they are accepted together with deletion and addition changes.<br />The modification change depends on the addition change, and the deletion change depends on the modification change.Thus accepting the deletion change means also accepting the addition change, the modification change and the deletion change itself.</p></ac:rich-text-body></ac:structured-macro><h3>Conflicting changes</h3><p>Conflict occurs when two changes are incompatible, i.e., the changes cannot be accepted together. Conflicts can only occur when using 3-way merge.</p><ac:structured-macro ac:name="tip" ac:schema-version="1" ac:macro-id="de1e0e95-13ee-45c3-8c87-f1c412e9bfaf"><ac:rich-text-body><p>The following situations will result in a conflict:</p><ul><li>Each contributor has changed a class name or any other element property value.</li><li>One contributor has added an operation to a class and the other contributor deleted the class.</li><li>One contributor has moved a class into one package while the other contributor moved it to another package.</li></ul></ac:rich-text-body></ac:structured-macro><h3><strong><ac:structured-macro ac:name="anchor" ac:schema-version="1" ac:macro-id="22c0bb61-77bd-4811-a52d-71f398b03479"><ac:parameter ac:name="">#equivalentchanges</ac:parameter></ac:structured-macro></strong>Equivalent changes</h3><p>Equivalent change is a pair of identical changes that are detected in both source and target when performing a 3-way merge.</p><ac:structured-macro ac:name="tip" ac:schema-version="1" ac:macro-id="5d46f7e7-ee62-4949-9153-a75ff54171b5"><ac:rich-text-body><p>Let's say package A contains some class in the ancestor and both contributors contain the same class in package B. This means that the class has been moved from package A to package B in both source and target. This is a case of the equivalent change.</p></ac:rich-text-body></ac:structured-macro><ac:structured-macro ac:name="tip" ac:schema-version="1" ac:macro-id="e60837bb-231f-40fe-afd6-272ff1def8c4"><ac:rich-text-body><p>Another case of equivalent changes is when the same element A becomes renamed to B in both contributors.</p></ac:rich-text-body></ac:structured-macro></ac:layout-cell></ac:layout-section><ac:layout-section ac:type="single"><ac:layout-cell><p><br /></p></ac:layout-cell></ac:layout-section><ac:layout-section ac:type="single"><ac:layout-cell><p class="auto-cursor-target"><strong>Related pages</strong></p><ul class="childpages-macro conf-macro output-block"><li><ac:link><ri:page ri:content-title="(2026x Refresh1) Understanding merge types" /></ac:link></li><li><ac:link><ri:page ri:content-title="(2026x Refresh1) Preparing for merge" /></ac:link></li><li><ac:link><ri:page ri:content-title="(2026x Refresh1) Starting Model Merge" /></ac:link></li><li><ac:link><ri:page ri:content-title="(2026x Refresh1) Analyzing and managing merge results" /></ac:link></li><li><ac:link><ri:page ri:content-title="(2026x Refresh1) Finishing and canceling merge" /></ac:link></li></ul></ac:layout-cell></ac:layout-section></ac:layout>
````

<!--NOMAGIC_PAGE id=303995840 space=MT version=1 -->
## PAGE 01970: (2026x Refresh1) Understanding change types

- page_id: `303995840`
- space_key: `MT`
- source_url: https://docs.nomagic.com/spaces/MT/pages/303995840/2026x+Refresh1+Understanding+change+types
- version_number: 1
- version_date: `2026-04-28T10:26:08.965+02:00`
- ancestors: Versions of Modeling Tools Documentation > (2026x Refresh1) Modeling Tools Documentation > (2026x Refresh1) General modeling features > (2026x Refresh1) Model analysis > (2026x Refresh1) Comparing projects
- labels: ['change-concept', 'addition-change', 'deletion-change', 'modification-change', 'movement-change', 'order-change', 'dependent-changes', 'change-types', 'msosa', 'mcse', 'msa']

### NORMALIZED CONTENT

Change is a difference, found between the base and compared project versions.

Read the following definitions to get familiar with different change types.

##### Addition change

If an element has been added to a compared project version, an addition change occurs.

##### Deletion change

If an element has been removed from a compared project version, a deletion change occurs.

##### Modification change

If an element property in a compared project version has been modified, a modification change occurs.

If the **Is****Abstract** property value of a class in the base had the default value *false* and the same property value in a compared project version has been changed to *true*, a modification change occurs.

There are three types of modification changes:

- **Addition modification change** that occurs when a value is added to a property.
- **Deletion modification change** that occurs when a value is removed from a property.
- **Replacement modification change** that occurs when one value is replaced with another. This type of modification change occurs only for properties that have multiplicity less or equal to 1.

##### Movement change

If an element owner has been changed in a compared project version, a movement change occurs.

Let's say package A contains some class in the base and package B contains the same class in a compared project version. This means that the class has been moved from package A to package B in the compared project version. This case is recognized as a movement change.

Another case of the movement change is when an attribute or an operation that has been owned by class A in the base, becomes the attribute or an operation of class B in a compared project version.

##### Order change

If the order of elements has been changed in a compared project version, an order change occurs. Order changes can occur on elements such as attributes, operations, and other ordered elements. Even if a single element in a collection has changed its place, the order change is applied to the entire collection. 
Since an element can have several ordered collections, several order changes can occur on a single element.

Let's say class A has attributes a, b, and c in the base. The attribute c has been moved up and placed above attribute a in a compared project version. This means that the order of attribute collection in class A has changed in the compared project version. This is a case of the order change.

Order changes can be skipped while comparing projects. For this, you need to specify the names of properties whose order changes should not be detected in the **Do Not Detect Order Changes for** environment option. For more information, see [CONFLUENCE_PAGE title='(2026x Refresh1) Preparing for project comparison' space=''].

##### Dependent change

In some cases, changes depend on other changes and are called dependent changes.For better understanding of the concept of dependent changes, study the following examples.

Let's say a class attribute type has been changed to a type that had been created by another change. In consequence, the attribute type change depends on the change that has created the type.

Let's suppose there is an attribute type change in a compared project version. An old type has been deleted and a new type has been added to the compared project version. In this case, three changes occur:

- deletion change (for the old type)
- addition change (for the new type)
- modification change (for the property type)

The modification change depends on the addition change, and the deletion change depends on the modification change.

### EXACT CONFLUENCE STORAGE SOURCE

<!--NOMAGIC_PAGE id=303992071 space=MT version=1 -->
## PAGE 01971: (2026x Refresh1) Understanding content of the Differences tree

- page_id: `303992071`
- space_key: `MT`
- source_url: https://docs.nomagic.com/spaces/MT/pages/303992071/2026x+Refresh1+Understanding+content+of+the+Differences+tree
- version_number: 1
- version_date: `2026-04-28T10:21:28.764+02:00`
- ancestors: Versions of Modeling Tools Documentation > (2026x Refresh1) Modeling Tools Documentation > (2026x Refresh1) General modeling features > (2026x Refresh1) Model analysis > (2026x Refresh1) Comparing projects > (2026x Refresh1) Analyzing comparison results
- labels: ['element-decorations', 'recognizing-changes', 'comparison-result', 'msosa', 'mcse', 'msa']

### NORMALIZED CONTENT

Elements with all change types are displayed in the **Differences**tree that is located at the top left of the **Difference Viewer** window.

**Used project representation**

This node indicates that the compared project contains modified used projects or used projects with modified usages.

[IMAGE alt='' src='']

**Recognizing addition changes**

For the addition change description, refer to [CONFLUENCE_PAGE title='(2026x Refresh1) Understanding change types' space=''].

Added elements are highlighted in green background. 
 
[IMAGE alt='' src='']

**Recognizing deletion changes**

For the deletion change description, refer to [CONFLUENCE_PAGE title='(2026x Refresh1) Understanding change types' space=''].

Deleted elements are highlighted in red background. 
 
[IMAGE alt='' src='']

**Recognizing modification changes**

For the modification change description, refer to [CONFLUENCE_PAGE title='(2026x Refresh1) Understanding change types' space=''].

Elements whose properties have been changed are highlighted in blue. 
 
[IMAGE alt='' src=''] 
 
To see what properties have been modified, click the element and see changes in the **Specification** panel.

For more information about using the Specification panel, see [CONFLUENCE_PAGE title='(2026x Refresh1) Inspecting differences in the Change details panel' space=''].

**Recognizing movement changes**

For the movement change description, refer to[CONFLUENCE_PAGE title='(2026x Refresh1) Understanding change types' space=''].

Elements with movement changes are highlighted in the same blue background that is used to highlight modification changes, since movement changes are some kind of modification changes. Icon of an element with movement changes is represented with arrow symbol on the left.

The following figure illustrates a movement change, when element X has been moved from package A to package B.

[IMAGE alt='' src='']

You can navigate from the element's original location to the new one (and vice versa) using the commands on the element shortcut menu.

[IMAGE alt='' src='']

[IMAGE alt='' src=''] 
 
**Recognizing order changes**

For the order change description, refer to [CONFLUENCE_PAGE title='(2026x Refresh1) Understanding change types' space=''].

Elements with order changes are highlighted in the same blue background that is used to highlight modification changes, since order changes are some kind of modification changes. 
 
[IMAGE alt='' src='']

**Recognizing elements with changed owned elements** 
Elements whose owned elements (in any nesting level) have changes are highlighted using red diagonals.

[IMAGE alt='' src=''] 
 
 Modified elements with changed owned elements are highlighted in blue with red diagonals.

[IMAGE alt='' src='']

### EXACT CONFLUENCE STORAGE SOURCE

details panel" /></ac:link>.</p><p><br class="atl-forced-newline" /><span style="color: rgb(51,51,51);"><strong><ac:inline-comment-marker ac:ref="a9d8377d-0613-483d-ae5f-ca06c0bc8272">Recognizing movement changes</ac:inline-comment-marker></strong></span></p><p><ac:inline-comment-marker ac:ref="a9d8377d-0613-483d-ae5f-ca06c0bc8272">For the movement change description, refer to </ac:inline-comment-marker><ac:link ac:anchor="movementchange"><ri:page ri:content-title="(2026x Refresh1) Understanding change types" /><ac:plain-text-link-body><![CDATA[Movement change]]></ac:plain-text-link-body></ac:link><ac:inline-comment-marker ac:ref="1344219e-85f4-4ce0-aa0f-5cb6a51585d4"><ac:inline-comment-marker ac:ref="a9d8377d-0613-483d-ae5f-ca06c0bc8272">.</ac:inline-comment-marker></ac:inline-comment-marker></p><p><ac:inline-comment-marker ac:ref="a9d8377d-0613-483d-ae5f-ca06c0bc8272">Elements with movement changes are highlighted in the same blue background that is used to highlight modification changes, since movement changes are some kind of modification changes. Icon of an element with movement changes is represented with arrow symbol on the left.</ac:inline-comment-marker></p><p><ac:inline-comment-marker ac:ref="a9d8377d-0613-483d-ae5f-ca06c0bc8272">The following figure illustrates a movement change, when element X has been moved from package A to package B.<br /></ac:inline-comment-marker></p><p><ac:image ac:title="Example of a movement change" ac:alt="Example of a movement change"><ri:attachment ri:filename="movement_change.png"><ri:page ri:content-title="(2026x Refresh1) Understanding content of the Differences tree" /></ri:attachment></ac:image></p><p><span>You can navigate from the element's original location to the new one (and vice versa) using the commands on the element shortcut menu.</span></p><p><ac:image ac:title="Navigating to new element location" ac:alt="Navigating to new element location"><ri:attachment ri:filename="nav_new_element_location.png"><ri:page ri:content-title="(2026x Refresh1) Understanding content of the Differences tree" /></ri:attachment></ac:image></p><p><ac:image ac:title="Navigating to original element location" ac:alt="Navigating to original element location"><ri:attachment ri:filename="nav_original_element_location.png"><ri:page ri:content-title="(2026x Refresh1) Understanding content of the Differences tree" /></ri:attachment></ac:image><br class="atl-forced-newline" /><br class="atl-forced-newline" /><span style="color: rgb(51,51,51);"><strong>Recognizing order changes</strong></span></p><p>For the order change description, refer to <ac:link ac:anchor="orderchange"><ri:page ri:content-title="(2026x Refresh1) Understanding change types" /><ac:plain-text-link-body><![CDATA[Order change]]></ac:plain-text-link-body></ac:link>.</p><p>Elements with order changes are highlighted in the same blue background that is used to highlight modification changes, since order changes are some kind of modification changes. <br class="atl-forced-newline" /><br class="atl-forced-newline" /><ac:image ac:title="Example of accepted order change" ac:alt="Example of accepted order change"><ri:attachment ri:filename="example_order_change.png"><ri:page ri:content-title="(2026x Refresh1) Understanding content of the Differences tree" /></ri:attachment></ac:image></p><p><br class="atl-forced-newline" /><span style="color: rgb(51,51,51);"><strong>Re<ac:inline-comment-marker ac:ref="782f0a10-bbb6-4f1d-869c-5f86bb8cf0ee">cognizing elements with changed owned elements</ac:inline-comment-marker></strong></span><br class="atl-forced-newline" /><ac:inline-comment-marker ac:ref="782f0a10-bbb6-4f1d-869c-5f86bb8cf0ee"> Elements whose owned elements (in any nesting level) have changes are highlig</ac:inline-comment-marker>hted using red diagonals.</p><p><ac:image ac:title="Element with owned elements added" ac:alt="Element with owned elements added"><ri:attachment ri:filename="owned elements added.png"><ri:page ri:content-title="(2026x Refresh1) Understanding content of the Differences tree" /></ri:attachment></ac:image><br class="atl-forced-newline" /><br class="atl-forced-newline" /> Modified elements with changed owned elements are highlighted in blue with red diagonals.</p><p><ac:image ac:title="Element with owned elements added and modification change" ac:alt="Element with owned elements added and modification change"><ri:attachment ri:filename="owned elements added&amp;modification change.png"><ri:page ri:content-title="(2026x Refresh1) Understanding content of the Differences tree" /></ri:attachment></ac:image></p><p> </p>

<!--NOMAGIC_PAGE id=303996684 space=MT version=1 -->
## PAGE 01972: (2026x Refresh1) Understanding content of the Merged Result panel

- page_id: `303996684`
- space_key: `MT`
- source_url: https://docs.nomagic.com/spaces/MT/pages/303996684/2026x+Refresh1+Understanding+content+of+the+Merged+Result+panel
- version_number: 1
- version_date: `2026-04-28T10:27:15.019+02:00`
- ancestors: Versions of Modeling Tools Documentation > (2026x Refresh1) Modeling Tools Documentation > (2026x Refresh1) General modeling features > (2026x Refresh1) Tools > (2026x Refresh1) Model Merge > (2026x Refresh1) Analyzing and managing merge results
- labels: ['merged-result', 'element-decorations', 'change-states', 'recognizing-changes', 'msosa', 'mcse', 'msa']

### NORMALIZED CONTENT

177724953

INLINE

177724956

INLINE

177724954

INLINE

Elements with all change types are displayed in the **Merged Result** tree/list that is located at the top left of the **Merge** window.

You can filter the elements displayed in the **Merged Results** tree/list by type of changes. Use the **Changes filter** drop-down list to filter out a specific type of changes.

[IMAGE alt='' src='']

By default, the changes from the Target and equivalent changes (changes made both in Target and Source) are automatically hidden from the **Merge** **Result** tree/list, meaning that only the Source changes are shown. This allows reducingthe number of changes needed to review for the user. If the filter is applied, the following notification appears:

[IMAGE alt='' src='']

**Understanding element marking**

When a change occurs on a single element, the ticks and crossesbefore the element icon indicate the state of the change which can be either accepted or rejected.

| Marking | Change state | Example |
| --- | --- | --- |
| (green tick before the element icon) | Accepted | A new element had been created in one of the contributors and its addition was accepted. |
| (red cross before the element icon) | Rejected | A new element had been created in one of the contributors and its addition was rejected. |

To understand the concept of change states, refer to [CONFLUENCE_PAGE title='(2026x Refresh1) Understanding change concept' space='']Change states.

Elements with resolved conflicting changes are marked as shown in the following table. Owners of these elements are also marked.

| Marking | Change applied by | Example |
| --- | --- | --- |
| (red diamond on the lower-left corner of the element icon) | System | The element had a conflicting modification change which was resolved automatically. |
| (yellow diamond on the lower-left corner of the element icon) | User | The element had a conflicting modification change which was resolved by the user. |

**Recognizing addition changes**

For the addition change description, refer to [CONFLUENCE_PAGE title='(2026x Refresh1) Understanding change concept' space=''].

Added elements are highlighted in green background. 
 
 [IMAGE alt='' src='']

**Recognizing deletion changes**

For the deletion change description, refer to [CONFLUENCE_PAGE title='(2026x Refresh1) Understanding change concept' space=''].

Deleted elements are highlighted in red background.

[IMAGE alt='' src='']

****

**Recognizing modification changes**

For the modification change description, refer to [CONFLUENCE_PAGE title='(2026x Refresh1) Understanding change concept' space=''].

Elements whose properties have been changed are highlighted in blue. 
 [IMAGE alt='' src=''] 
 
To see what properties have been modified, click the element and see changes in the **Specification** panel.

For more information about using the Specification panel, see [CONFLUENCE_PAGE title='(2026x Refresh1) Inspecting changes in the Change details panel' space=''].

**Recognizing movement changes**

For the movement change description, refer to [CONFLUENCE_PAGE title='(2026x Refresh1) Understanding change concept' space=''].

Elements with movement changes are highlighted in the same blue background that is used to highlight modification changes. Since movement changes are some kind of modification changes. Icon of an element with movement changes is represented with an arrow symbol on the left.

[IMAGE alt='' src='']

You can navigate from the element original location to the new one (and vice versa) using the commands on the element shortcut menu.

**Recognizing order changes**

For the order change description, refer to [CONFLUENCE_PAGE title='(2026x Refresh1) Understanding change concept' space=''].

Elements with order changes are highlighted in the same blue background that is used to highlight modification changes, since order changes are some kind of modification changes. 
After accepting the change, all the elements in the collection are reordered so that it would be the same as in one of the contributors.

[IMAGE alt='' src='']

You can turn off the order changes detection. For more about it, see [CONFLUENCE_PAGE title='(2026x Refresh1) Finishing and canceling merge' space='']

**Recognizing elements with changed owned elements** 
Elements whose owned elements (in any nesting level) have changes are highlighted using red diagonals.

[IMAGE alt='' src='']

177724951

INLINE

**Related pages**

- [CONFLUENCE_PAGE title='(2026x Refresh1) Getting merge summary information and changes legend' space='']
- [CONFLUENCE_PAGE title='(2026x Refresh1) Inspecting element property changes in the Specification panel' space='']
- [CONFLUENCE_PAGE title='(2026x Refresh1) Inspecting changes in the Change details panel' space='']
- [CONFLUENCE_PAGE title='(2026x Refresh1) Displaying and navigating through changes' space='']
- [CONFLUENCE_PAGE title='(2026x Refresh1) Quickly navigating through conflicting changes' space='']
- [CONFLUENCE_PAGE title='(2026x Refresh1) Accepting and rejecting changes using shortcut menu' space='']
- [CONFLUENCE_PAGE title='(2026x Refresh1) Viewing and analyzing changes of modified diagrams' space='']
- [CONFLUENCE_PAGE title='(2026x Refresh1) Generating a differences report' space='']

### EXACT CONFLUENCE STORAGE SOURCE

<!--NOMAGIC_PAGE id=303998309 space=MT version=1 -->
## PAGE 01973: (2026x Refresh1) Understanding Diagram Toolbar

- page_id: `303998309`
- space_key: `MT`
- source_url: https://docs.nomagic.com/spaces/MT/pages/303998309/2026x+Refresh1+Understanding+Diagram+Toolbar
- version_number: 1
- version_date: `2026-04-28T10:29:27.306+02:00`
- ancestors: Versions of Modeling Tools Documentation > (2026x Refresh1) Modeling Tools Documentation > (2026x Refresh1) General modeling features > (2026x Refresh1) Diagramming
- labels: []

### NORMALIZED CONTENT

The diagram toolbar provides quick access to common diagram editing and navigation commands. These tools help you arrange elements, adjust connector routing, apply styles, and manage the diagram's appearance. The table below lists commands, descriptions, and their icons to help you identify them in the toolbar.

| Icon | Command | Description |
| --- | --- | --- |
|  | Back to Index | Returns to the previously viewed diagram or diagram location. |
|  | Forward | Returns to the next viewed diagram or diagram location. |
|  | Select in Containment Tree | Selects the active element in the Containment tree. |
|  | Quick Diagram Layout | Automatically arranges the visible diagram elements using the default layout algorithm. To learn more, refer to the Layout page. |
|  | Align | Aligns the selected symbols based on the chosen alignment options described below:IconCommandDescriptionMake Same SizeResizes all selected shapes to match the size of the largest shape.Make Same WidthResizes all selected shapes to match the width of the widest shape.Make Same HeightResizes all selected shapes to match the height of the tallest shape.Center HorizontallyAligns the selected shapes to a common horizontal center.Center VerticallyAligns the selected shapes to a common vertical center.Space Evenly HorizontallyEvenly spaces the selected shapes horizontally.Space Evenly VerticallyEvenly spaces the selected shapes vertically.Align RightAligns the selected shapes to the right edge of the rightmost shape.Align LeftAligns the selected shapes to the left edge of the leftmost shape.Align TopAligns the selected shapes to the top edge of the topmost shape.Align BottomAligns the selected shapes to the bottom edge of the lowest shape. |
| Icon | Command | Description |
|  | Make Same Size | Resizes all selected shapes to match the size of the largest shape. |
|  | Make Same Width | Resizes all selected shapes to match the width of the widest shape. |
|  | Make Same Height | Resizes all selected shapes to match the height of the tallest shape. |
|  | Center Horizontally | Aligns the selected shapes to a common horizontal center. |
|  | Center Vertically | Aligns the selected shapes to a common vertical center. |
|  | Space Evenly Horizontally | Evenly spaces the selected shapes horizontally. |
|  | Space Evenly Vertically | Evenly spaces the selected shapes vertically. |
|  | Align Right | Aligns the selected shapes to the right edge of the rightmost shape. |
|  | Align Left | Aligns the selected shapes to the left edge of the leftmost shape. |
|  | Align Top | Aligns the selected shapes to the top edge of the topmost shape. |
|  | Align Bottom | Aligns the selected shapes to the bottom edge of the lowest shape. |
|  | Remove Break Points | Removes all user-defined breakpoints from the selected connectors. |
|  | Rectilinear | Changes the selected connector to a rectilinear routing style. |
|  | Reset Label Position | Returns the selected label to its default position. |
|  | Rotate Label | Rotates the selected label to the next available orientation. |
|  | Copy | Copies the selected diagram elements or symbols to the clipboard. |
|  | Paste | Pastes the copied elements or symbols into the current diagram. |
|  | Delete | Deletes the selected element from both the diagram and the model. |
|  | Remove from Diagram | Removes the selected symbol from the diagram without deleting the underlying model element. |
|  | Fill Color | Applies a fill color to the selected symbol. |
|  | Apply Default Symbol Style | Restores the selected symbol to the default style defined for its element type. |
|  | Paste Style | Applies the copied style properties to the selected symbol. |
|  | Suppress All | Hides all compartments and visual details in the selected symbol. |
|  | Show as Box | Displays the selected element as a box symbol. |
|  | Show as Image | Displays the selected element using its assigned image instead of the default box symbol. |
|  | Make Preferred Size | Resizes the selected symbol to its preferred size. |
|  | Validate Diagram | Runs validation on the active diagram and displays any detected issues. |
|  | Options | Opens the diagram’s display and presentation options. |
|  | Legend | Opens the diagram legend for managing style categories and legend items. To learn more, refer to the page. |
|  | Find in Diagram | Locates and highlights the selected element in the active diagram. |
|  | Run | Runs the Simulation. |

### EXACT CONFLUENCE STORAGE SOURCE

<!--NOMAGIC_PAGE id=303997388 space=MT version=1 -->
## PAGE 01974: (2026x Refresh1) Understanding merge types

- page_id: `303997388`
- space_key: `MT`
- source_url: https://docs.nomagic.com/spaces/MT/pages/303997388/2026x+Refresh1+Understanding+merge+types
- version_number: 1
- version_date: `2026-04-28T10:28:07.345+02:00`
- ancestors: Versions of Modeling Tools Documentation > (2026x Refresh1) Modeling Tools Documentation > (2026x Refresh1) General modeling features > (2026x Refresh1) Tools > (2026x Refresh1) Model Merge
- labels: ['merge-type', '2-way-merge', '3-way-merge', 'msosa', 'mcse', 'msa']

### NORMALIZED CONTENT

1993946884

INLINE

1993946886

INLINE

1993946885

INLINE

There are two types of model merge that can be used to merge two selected project versions (Source and Target):

- 3-way merge
- 2-way merge

##### 3-way merge

The 3-way merge allows merging both local and server projects. To merge two selected project versions, the common ancestor (a common project version for both target and source) is used. The common ancestor is calculated automatically when working with Teamwork Cloud projects; however, you have to select a correct common ancestor manually for local projects. This is the recommended way to merge project versions because the 3-way merge is able to identify the changes made in the source and target project versions separately by comparing changes between the ancestor and both contributors. This allows merging the selected project versions more smoothly.

[IMAGE alt='' src='']

###### Visual representation of the 3-way merge

##### 2-way merge

The 2-way merge compares two local project versions selected by the user. Typically, the target version of the project is considered to be the common ancestor (a common project version for both target and source). As a result, only the differences between the source and the target project versions are identified.

[IMAGE alt='' src='']

###### Visual representation of the 2-way merge

1993946883

INLINE

To merge two selected project versions, the common ancestor (a common project version for both target and source) is used. The common ancestor is calculated automatically when working with Teamwork Cloud projects; however, you have to select a correct common ancestor manually for local projects. This is the recommended way to merge project versions because the 3-way merge is able to identify the changes made in the source and target project versions separately by comparing changes between the ancestor and both contributors. This allows merging the selected project versions more smoothly. </p><p><ac:image ac:align="center"><ri:attachment ri:filename="3-way_merge_explanation.png"><ri:page ri:content-title="(2026x Refresh1) Understanding merge types" /></ri:attachment></ac:image></p><h6 style="text-align: center;">Visual representation of the 3-way merge</h6><h3>2-way merge</h3><p>The 2-way merge compares two local project versions selected by the user. Typically, the target version of the project is considered to be the common ancestor (a common project version for both target and source). As a result, only the differences between the source and the target project versions are identified. </p><p><ac:image ac:align="center"><ri:attachment ri:filename="2-way_merge_explanation.png"><ri:page ri:content-title="(2026x Refresh1) Understanding merge types" /></ri:attachment></ac:image></p><h6 style="text-align: center;">Visual representation of the 2-way merge</h6></ac:rich-text-body></ac:structured-macro><p class="auto-cursor-target"><br /></p><ac:structured-macro ac:name="content-block" ac:schema-version="1" ac:macro-id="9a15a32b-4b65-4453-b599-d525df849345"><ac:parameter ac:name="id">1993946883</ac:parameter><ac:parameter ac:name="atlassian-macro-output-type">INLINE</ac:parameter><ac:rich-text-body><p class="auto-cursor-target"><strong>Related pages</strong></p><ul class="childpages-macro conf-macro output-block"><li><ac:link><ri:page ri:content-title="(2026x Refresh1) Understanding change concept" /></ac:link></li><li><ac:link><ri:page ri:content-title="(2026x Refresh1) Preparing for merge" /></ac:link></li><li><ac:link><ri:page ri:content-title="(2026x Refresh1) Starting Model Merge" /></ac:link></li><li><ac:link><ri:page ri:content-title="(2026x Refresh1) Analyzing and managing merge results" /></ac:link></li><li><ac:link><ri:page ri:content-title="(2026x Refresh1) Finishing and canceling merge" /></ac:link></li></ul></ac:rich-text-body></ac:structured-macro><p class="auto-cursor-target"><br /></p></ac:rich-text-body></ac:structured-macro><p class="auto-cursor-target"><br /></p></ac:rich-text-body></ac:structured-macro><p class="auto-cursor-target"><br /></p>

<!--NOMAGIC_PAGE id=303989914 space=MT version=1 -->
## PAGE 01976: (2026x Refresh1) Union

- page_id: `303989914`
- space_key: `MT`
- source_url: https://docs.nomagic.com/spaces/MT/pages/303989914/2026x+Refresh1+Union
- version_number: 1
- version_date: `2026-04-28T10:18:36.721+02:00`
- ancestors: Versions of Modeling Tools Documentation > (2026x Refresh1) Modeling Tools Documentation > (2026x Refresh1) Data-related modeling > (2026x Refresh1) XML schemas > (2026x Refresh1) XML schema mapping to UML elements
- labels: ['msosa']

### NORMALIZED CONTENT

- The UML Class must have the additional stereotype XSDunion.
- “memberTypes” and inner simpleTypes maps to several UML Generalizations between the simple type and members types.
- In order to have an inner simpleType element, the parent of this Generalization must be an inner Class of an outer UML Class.

###### [IMAGE alt='' src='']

###### Union model example.

#### PANEL: Union model and XML code samples

Union model and XML code samples

```text
<xs:schema xmlns:nm = "http://nomagic.com" xmlns:xs = "http://www.w3.org/2001/XMLSchema" targetNamespace = "http://nomagic.com" >	<xs:simpleType name = "my_simple_union" >
```

```text
<xs:union id = "unionID" memberTypes = "xs:string xs:number" />	</xs:simpleType>
```

```text
<xs:simpleType name = "my_simple_union2" >
```

```text
<xs:annotation >
```

```text
<xs:documentation >very important documentation</xs:documentation>
```

```text
</xs:annotation>
```

```text
<xs:union id = "unionID" memberTypes = "xs:number" >  			<xs:simpleType > 				<xs:restriction base = "xs:number" />
```

```text
</xs:simpleType> 		</xs:union>
```

```text
</xs:simpleType>
```

```text
</xs:schema>
```

### EXACT CONFLUENCE STORAGE SOURCE

````html
<ul><li>The UML Class must have the additional stereotype XSDunion.</li><li>“memberTypes” and inner simpleTypes maps to several UML Generalizations between the simple type and members types.</li><li>In order to have an inner simpleType element, the parent of this Generalization must be an inner Class of an outer UML Class.</li></ul><h6><ac:image ac:align="center"><ri:attachment ri:filename="Figure 73 Union Example Model and XML Code2.png"><ri:page ri:content-title="(2026x Refresh1) Union" /></ri:attachment></ac:image></h6><h6 style="text-align: center;">Union model example.</h6><ac:structured-macro ac:name="panel" ac:schema-version="1" ac:macro-id="52b0db29-d7c1-4a90-bc63-cf186f5700d7"><ac:parameter ac:name="title">Union model and XML code samples</ac:parameter><ac:rich-text-body><pre>&lt;xs:schema xmlns:nm = &quot;<a href="http://nomagic.com">http://nomagic.com</a>&quot; xmlns:xs = &quot;<a href="http://www.w3.org/2001/XMLSchema">http://www.w3.org/2001/XMLSchema</a>&quot; targetNamespace = &quot;<a href="http://nomagic.com">http://nomagic.com</a>&quot; &gt;<br />	&lt;xs:simpleType name = &quot;my_simple_union&quot; &gt;</pre><pre>		&lt;xs:union id = &quot;unionID&quot; memberTypes = &quot;xs:string xs:number&quot; /&gt;<br />	&lt;/xs:simpleType&gt;</pre><pre>	&lt;xs:simpleType name = &quot;my_simple_union2&quot; &gt;</pre><pre>		&lt;xs:annotation &gt;</pre><pre>			&lt;xs:documentation &gt;very important documentation&lt;/xs:documentation&gt;</pre><pre>		&lt;/xs:annotation&gt;</pre><pre>		&lt;xs:union id = &quot;unionID&quot; memberTypes = &quot;xs:number&quot; &gt; <br /> 			&lt;xs:simpleType &gt; <br />				&lt;xs:restriction base = &quot;xs:number&quot; /&gt;</pre><pre>			&lt;/xs:simpleType&gt; <br />		&lt;/xs:union&gt;</pre><pre>	&lt;/xs:simpleType&gt;</pre><pre>&lt;/xs:schema&gt;</pre></ac:rich-text-body></ac:structured-macro><p class="auto-cursor-target"><br /></p>
````
