<!--NOMAGIC_PAGE id=303993808 space=MT version=1 -->
## PAGE 02024: (2026x Refresh1) Using Eclipse to develop Groovy scripts

- page_id: `303993808`
- space_key: `MT`
- source_url: https://docs.nomagic.com/spaces/MT/pages/303993808/2026x+Refresh1+Using+Eclipse+to+develop+Groovy+scripts
- version_number: 1
- version_date: `2026-04-28T10:23:39.480+02:00`
- ancestors: Versions of Modeling Tools Documentation > (2026x Refresh1) Modeling Tools Documentation > (2026x Refresh1) General modeling features > (2026x Refresh1) Macro engine > (2026x Refresh1) Supported scripting languages
- labels: ['msosa', 'mcse', 'msa']

### NORMALIZED CONTENT

Use the following three simple steps to develop and run a Groovy script in NetBeans:

1. Install Groovy-Eclipse Plugin.
2. Develop a Groovy script.

To install Groovy-Eclipse Plugin

1. Go to [https://github.com/groovy/groovy-eclipse](https://github.com/groovy/groovy-eclipse) .
2. Follow the instructions for installing Groovy-Eclipse Plugin.

To develop a Groovy Script

1. Create a Groovy project.
2. Right-click the project in Package Explorer and click Properties .
3. Click Java Build Path in the tree on the left-hand side. Click the Libraries tab on the right pane.
4. Click Add External JARs to add all the JAR files in <modeling tool name>/lib and in <modeling tool name>/plugins/com.nomagic.magicdraw.automaton/engines/groovy-2.0.1/embeddable/groovy-all-2.0.1.jar .
5. Click OK to close the dialog.
6. Create a new Groovy class in the project. Remove a class declaration, and then put the script there.

### EXACT CONFLUENCE STORAGE SOURCE

````html
<ac:layout><ac:layout-section ac:type="two_right_sidebar"><ac:layout-cell><p>Use the following three simple steps to develop and run a Groovy script in NetBeans:</p><ol><li>Install Groovy-Eclipse Plugin.</li><li>Develop a Groovy script.</li></ol><p>To install Groovy-Eclipse Plugin</p><hr /><ol><li>Go to <em><a href="https://github.com/groovy/groovy-eclipse">https://github.com/groovy/groovy-eclipse</a></em>.</li><li>Follow the instructions for installing Groovy-Eclipse Plugin.</li></ol><p>To develop a Groovy Script</p><hr /><ol><li>Create a Groovy project.</li><li>Right-click the project in Package Explorer and click <strong>Properties</strong>.</li><li>Click Java Build Path in the tree on the left-hand side. Click the <strong>Libraries</strong> tab on the right pane.</li><li>Click <strong>Add External JARs</strong> to add all the JAR files in <em>&lt;modeling tool name&gt;/lib</em> and in <em>&lt;modeling tool name&gt;/plugins/com.nomagic.magicdraw.automaton/engines/groovy-2.0.1/embeddable/groovy-all-2.0.1.jar</em>.</li><li>Click <strong>OK</strong> to close the dialog.</li><li>Create a new Groovy class in the project. Remove a class declaration, and then put the script there.</li></ol></ac:layout-cell><ac:layout-cell><p><br /></p></ac:layout-cell></ac:layout-section></ac:layout>
````

<!--NOMAGIC_PAGE id=303997708 space=MT version=2 -->
## PAGE 02025: (2026x Refresh1) Using Find operation

- page_id: `303997708`
- space_key: `MT`
- source_url: https://docs.nomagic.com/spaces/MT/pages/303997708/2026x+Refresh1+Using+Find+operation
- version_number: 2
- version_date: `2026-04-28T10:48:50.174+02:00`
- ancestors: Versions of Modeling Tools Documentation > (2026x Refresh1) Modeling Tools Documentation > (2026x Refresh1) General modeling features > (2026x Refresh1) Model analysis > (2026x Refresh1) Specifying criteria for querying model
- labels: ['msosa', 'mcse', 'msa']

### NORMALIZED CONTENT

#### CONTENT-LAYER: Useful information

68780004

#### CONTENT-COLUMN: Useful information

68780016

68780003

**On this page**

****

#### CONTENT-BLOCK: Useful information

68780006

The modeling tool utilizes a Find operation that allows you to automatically search for model elements, represent relations between these elements, or gather their [CONFLUENCE_PAGE title='(2026x Refresh1) Custom traceability rules' space=''] values each time you update your model. The Find operation is customizable, so you can change the default values of all the properties for the selected operation at any any time. The Find operation can be accessed and used:

- In a [CONFLUENCE_PAGE title='(2026x Refresh1) Relation Map' space=''] to represent relations between elements.
- In a [CONFLUENCE_PAGE title='(2026x Refresh1) Dependency Matrix' space=''] to associate elements in rows and columns of a matrix.
- From the [CONFLUENCE_PAGE title='(2026x Refresh1) Specification window' space=''] of a [CONFLUENCE_PAGE title='Smart Package' space='MED'] to query a model and gather specific elements.
- From the Specification window of the selected element to calculate/gather the values of derived properties.
- In a [CONFLUENCE_PAGE title='(2026x Refresh1) Generic table' space=''] to calculate/gather the values of derived properties.
- From the Specification window of an [CONFLUENCE_PAGE title='(2026x Refresh1) Creating executable opaque behaviors' space=''] to specify the body of a StructuredExpression.

#### TIP: Tip

Tip

If you would like to simply find the desired model element, and don't need to automatically query your model every time you update it, use the [CONFLUENCE_PAGE title='(2026x Refresh1) Find dialog' space='']**Find** dialog instead.

To use a Find operation, you need to specify it as criteria. The next section describes how to complete this task.

##### Specifying a Find operation as criteria

Specifying a Find operation as criteria allows you to automatically query your model in order to find and render specific model elements, relations between these elements, or gather their derived properties depending on where the operation is used (as mentioned above).

To specify a Find operation as criteria

1. Open the dialog for [CONFLUENCE_PAGE title='(2026x Refresh1) Getting started with specifying criteria' space=''] .
2. Select Find on the left side of the open dialog. [ATTACHMENT filename='Find_criterion.png']
3. Change the **Operation Name**, if needed.
4. Select the **Style** to represent the specified Find criteria. Note that the style selection is available only in the **Relation Criteria** and **Dependency Criteria** dialogs.
5. Specify the [CONFLUENCE_PAGE title='(2026x Refresh1) Find dialog' space=''] , and click OK .

#### TIP: Useful information

Useful information

Selecting the**Search in all texts** check box allows you to search for text in Documentation and TODO properties.

##### expertAdvanced Manipulations

###### Specifying the Find operation properties

To specify the Find operation properties

1. [CONFLUENCE_PAGE title='(2026x Refresh1) Getting started with specifying criteria' space=''] .
2. On the left side of the dialog, expand the Find operation.
3. Select the property you want to specify, and, in the property specification panel on the right side of the dialog, specify the value of the selected property. [ATTACHMENT filename='find_relation_criteria_dialog.png']
4. Click OK . The selected Find operation is now customized to query a model according to your specified properties. You can use a specific Find operation as an argument of another operation. In other words, you can create a new Find, Filter, Implied Relation, Exclude, Script, Union or Collection operation, and use the results of a specific Find operation as its property.

###### **find as property**To use the expression of a Find operation as a property of another operation

To use the expression of a Find operation as a property of another operation

1. [CONFLUENCE_PAGE title='(2026x Refresh1) Getting started with specifying criteria' space=''] .
2. Select Find on the left side of the dialog.
3. In the criteria specification panel on the right side of the dialog, click the **Use as** button, and select the desired command that corresponds to the operation you want to create as shown below. A new operation is created. 
[IMAGE alt='' src='']
4. Specify other parameters of the created operation, and click OK . You now have a new operation that has been created with the selected Find operation as one of its properties.

68794211

**Related pages**

- Specifying criteria for querying model
  - Getting started with specifying criteria
  - Using Simple Navigation
  - Using Metachain Navigation
  - Using Implied Relations
  - Creating new operations
    - Calling operations from the model
    - Creating script operations

### EXACT CONFLUENCE STORAGE SOURCE

````html
<ac:structured-macro ac:name="content-layer" ac:schema-version="1" ac:macro-id="231785dc-5bcb-4ad3-bcaa-bc987f91a085"><ac:parameter ac:name="id">68780004</ac:parameter><ac:rich-text-body><ac:structured-macro ac:name="content-column" ac:schema-version="1" ac:macro-id="9ad9c228-6cc2-4c56-b835-de1065ff6026"><ac:parameter ac:name="id">68780016</ac:parameter><ac:rich-text-body><ac:structured-macro ac:name="content-block" ac:schema-version="1" ac:macro-id="23823620-6e72-473a-a4eb-1649e7e581c3"><ac:parameter ac:name="id">68780003</ac:parameter><ac:rich-text-body><p><strong>On this page</strong></p><p><strong><ac:structured-macro ac:name="toc" ac:schema-version="1" ac:macro-id="9d9bd883-f80d-4224-8670-309ea8e2cad5" /></strong></p></ac:rich-text-body></ac:structured-macro><ac:structured-macro ac:name="content-block" ac:schema-version="1" ac:macro-id="b8e54c0c-9bfa-4a2d-9767-daa76ae79a33"><ac:parameter ac:name="id">68780006</ac:parameter><ac:rich-text-body><p>The modeling tool utilizes a Find operation that allows you to automatically search for model elements, represent relations between these elements, or gather their <ac:link><ri:page ri:content-title="(2026x Refresh1) Custom traceability rules" /><ac:plain-text-link-body><![CDATA[derived property]]></ac:plain-text-link-body></ac:link> values each time you update your model. The Find operation is customizable, so you can change the default values of all the properties for the selected operation at any any time. The Find operation can be accessed and used:</p><ul><li>In a <ac:link><ri:page ri:content-title="(2026x Refresh1) Relation Map" /></ac:link> to represent relations between elements.</li><li>In a <ac:link><ri:page ri:content-title="(2026x Refresh1) Dependency Matrix" /></ac:link> to associate elements in rows and columns of a matrix.</li><li>From the <ac:link><ri:page ri:content-title="(2026x Refresh1) Specification window" /></ac:link> of a <ac:link><ri:page ri:space-key="MED" ri:content-title="Smart Package" /></ac:link> to query a model and gather specific elements.</li><li>From the Specification window of the selected element to calculate/gather the values of derived properties.</li><li>In a <ac:link><ri:page ri:content-title="(2026x Refresh1) Generic table" /><ac:plain-text-link-body><![CDATA[Generic Table]]></ac:plain-text-link-body></ac:link> to calculate/gather the values of derived properties.</li><li>From the Specification window of an <ac:link><ri:page ri:content-title="(2026x Refresh1) Creating executable opaque behaviors" /><ac:plain-text-link-body><![CDATA[opaque behavior]]></ac:plain-text-link-body></ac:link> to specify the body of a StructuredExpression.<br /> </li></ul><ac:structured-macro ac:name="tip" ac:schema-version="1" ac:macro-id="abe846db-615f-4e59-b8d6-be5f9b6795c5"><ac:parameter ac:name="title">Tip</ac:parameter><ac:rich-text-body><p>If you would like to simply find the desired model element, and don't need to automatically query your model every time you update it, use the <ac:link><ri:page ri:content-title="(2026x Refresh1) Find dialog" /><ac:link-body><strong>Find</strong> dialog</ac:link-body></ac:link> instead.</p></ac:rich-text-body></ac:structured-macro><p>To use a Find operation, you need to specify it as criteria. The next section describes how to complete this task.</p><h3>Specifying a Find operation as criteria</h3><p>Specifying a Find operation as criteria allows you to automatically query your model in order to find and render specific model elements, relations between these elements, or gather their derived properties depending on where the operation is used (as mentioned above).</p><p><br /></p><p>To specify a Find operation as criteria</p><hr /><ol><li>Open the dialog for <ac:link><ri:page ri:content-title="(2026x Refresh1) Getting started with specifying criteria" /><ac:plain-text-link-body><![CDATA[specifying criteria]]></ac:plain-text-link-body></ac:link>.</li><li>Select <strong>Find</strong> on the left side of the open dialog.<br /><ac:image><ri:attachment ri:filename="Find_criterion.png"><ri:page ri:content-title="(2026x Refresh1) Using Find operation" /></ri:attachment></ac:image></li><li><p>Change the <strong>Operation Name</strong>, if needed.</p></li><li><p>Select the <strong>Style</strong> to represent the specified Find criteria. Note that the style selection is available only in the <strong>Relation Criteria</strong> and <strong>Dependency Criteria</strong> dialogs.</p></li><li>Specify the <ac:link ac:anchor="Find dialog options"><ri:page ri:content-title="(2026x Refresh1) Find dialog" /><ac:plain-text-link-body><![CDATA[Find operation properties]]></ac:plain-text-link-body></ac:link>, and click <strong>OK</strong>.<br /> </li></ol><ac:structured-macro ac:name="tip" ac:schema-version="1" ac:macro-id="d1e6a977-d642-493c-a2e9-f3cf1e6cdd46"><ac:parameter ac:name="title">Useful information</ac:parameter><ac:rich-text-body><p>Selecting the<strong> Search in all texts</strong> check box allows you to search for text in Documentation and TODO properties.</p></ac:rich-text-body></ac:structured-macro><h3><ac:structured-macro ac:name="anchor" ac:schema-version="1" ac:macro-id="0d1a126e-a626-422b-9c1f-995128cfe8a4"><ac:parameter ac:name="">expert</ac:parameter></ac:structured-macro>Advanced Manipulations</h3><h4>Specifying the Find operation properties </h4><p>To specify the Find operation properties </p><hr /><ol><li><ac:link><ri:page ri:content-title="(2026x Refresh1) Getting started with specifying criteria" /><ac:plain-text-link-body><![CDATA[Open the dialog for specifying criteria]]></ac:plain-text-link-body></ac:link>.</li><li>On the left side of the dialog, expand the Find operation.</li><li>Select the property you want to specify, and, in the property specification panel on the right side of the dialog, specify the value of the selected property.<br /><ac:image><ri:attachment ri:filename="find_relation_criteria_dialog.png"><ri:page ri:content-title="(2026x Refresh1) Using Find operation" /></ri:attachment></ac:image></li><li>Click <strong>OK</strong>.<br />The selected Find operation is now customized to query a model according to your specified properties. You can use a specific Find operation as an argument of another operation. In other words, you can create a new Find, Filter, Implied Relation, Exclude, Script, Union or Collection operation, and use the results of a specific Find operation as its property. </li></ol><h4><strong><ac:structured-macro ac:name="anchor" ac:schema-version="1" ac:macro-id="1dee633f-88ad-4f66-bb82-5e8dbb066561"><ac:parameter ac:name="">find as property</ac:parameter></ac:structured-macro></strong>To use the expression of a Find operation as a property of another operation</h4><p>To use the expression of a Find operation as a property of another operation</p><hr /><ol><li><ac:link><ri:page ri:content-title="(2026x Refresh1) Getting started with specifying criteria" /><ac:plain-text-link-body><![CDATA[Open the dialog for specifying criteria]]></ac:plain-text-link-body></ac:link>.</li><li>Select <strong>Find</strong> on the left side of the dialog.</li><li><p>In the criteria specification panel on the right side of the dialog, click the <strong>Use as</strong> button, and select the desired command that corresponds to the operation you want to create as shown below. A new operation is created.<br /><ac:image><ri:attachment ri:filename="Find_use_as.png"><ri:page ri:content-title="(2026x Refresh1) Using Find operation" /></ri:attachment></ac:image></p></li><li>Specify other parameters of the created operation, and click <strong>OK</strong>.<br />You now have a new operation that has been created with the selected Find operation as one of its properties.</li></ol></ac:rich-text-body></ac:structured-macro><ac:structured-macro ac:name="content-block" ac:schema-version="1" ac:macro-id="bfb704e1-7e83-4c8d-bc46-19c1abd44a38"><ac:parameter ac:name="id">68794211</ac:parameter><ac:rich-text-body><p><strong>Related pages</strong></p><ul><li class="ancestor-link parent-link"><a href="https://docs.nomagic.com/display/MT/(2026x Refresh1) Specifying+criteria+for+querying+model">Specifying criteria for querying model</a><ul><li><a href="https://docs.nomagic.com/display/MT/(2026x Refresh1) Getting+started+with+specifying+criteria">Getting started with specifying criteria</a></li><li><a href="https://docs.nomagic.com/display/MT/(2026x Refresh1) Using+Simple+Navigation">Using Simple Navigation</a></li><li><a href="https://docs.nomagic.com/display/MT/(2026x Refresh1) Using+Metachain+Navigation">Using Metachain Navigation</a></li><li><a href="https://docs.nomagic.com/display/MT/(2026x Refresh1) Using+Implied+Relations">Using Implied Relations</a></li><li><a href="https://docs.nomagic.com/display/MT/(2026x Refresh1) Creating+new+operations">Creating new operations</a><ul><li><a href="https://docs.nomagic.com/display/MT/(2026x Refresh1) Calling+operations+from+the+model">Calling operations from the model</a></li><li><a href="https://docs.nomagic.com/display/MT/(2026x Refresh1) Creating+script+operations">Creating script operations</a></li></ul></li></ul></li></ul><p><br /></p></ac:rich-text-body></ac:structured-macro></ac:rich-text-body></ac:structured-macro></ac:rich-text-body></ac:structured-macro>
````

<!--NOMAGIC_PAGE id=303997689 space=MT version=2 -->
## PAGE 02026: (2026x Refresh1) Using Implied Relations

- page_id: `303997689`
- space_key: `MT`
- source_url: https://docs.nomagic.com/spaces/MT/pages/303997689/2026x+Refresh1+Using+Implied+Relations
- version_number: 2
- version_date: `2026-04-28T10:48:49.365+02:00`
- ancestors: Versions of Modeling Tools Documentation > (2026x Refresh1) Modeling Tools Documentation > (2026x Refresh1) General modeling features > (2026x Refresh1) Model analysis > (2026x Refresh1) Specifying criteria for querying model
- labels: ['msosa', 'mcse', 'msa']

### NORMALIZED CONTENT

**On this page**

Use the implied relation operation type if you need to specify indirect relations between the elements when going through a definable hierarchy. Implied relation operation collects indirectly associated elements according to the specified **Source**, **Criteria** and **Target** parameters.

[IMAGE alt='' src='']

The figure above displays composition and inheritance structure between blocks. If a composite block depends on a particular element, the whole block also depends on this element. The same situation exists with inheritance between blocks. Inherited elements indirectly depend on the elements to which the parent element has relations. These indirect relations are referred to as implied relations, and can be displayed in a dependency matrix, as shown in the figure below.

[IMAGE alt='' src='']

As shown in the figure, the *High-voltage Battery* block satisfies the *Plug-in charge* requirement. According to the composition and generalization, the *Power SubSystem* and *High-voltage Battery Eco* blocks should satisfy this requirement as well. In the matrix, these relationships are shown as implied, and marked with red, dashed arrows.

Implied relations can be created in:

- Matrices
- Relation maps
- Tables
- Derived properties
- Smart packages

##### Specifying an Implied Relation operation as a criterion

To specify an Implied Relation operation as a criterion

1. Open the [CONFLUENCE_PAGE title='(2026x Refresh1) Getting started with specifying criteria' space=''] .
2. In the criteria list on the left side of the dialog, select the Implied Relation operation. The Implied Relation criteria specification panel opens. [ATTACHMENT filename='implied_relation.png']
3. In the Criteria drop-down list, select the desired relation. [ATTACHMENT filename='implied_relation_.png']
4. In the Direction drop-down list, select the direction of the implied relation:
  - The Source To Target direction means that only the outgoing relations, which are pointing from the source element to the target element, are treated as the result of this criterion.
  - The Target To Source direction means that only the incoming relations, which are pointing from the target element to the source element, are treated as the result of this criterion.
  - If the **Both** direction is chosen, both cases described above are treated as a valid result. A source is a [CONFLUENCE_PAGE title='(2026x Refresh1) Specifying criteria for querying model' space=''], and a target is the result of the calculation.
5. If you want direct relations to be displayed together with (and the same way as) implied relations, select the Include Direct Relations check box. However, be aware that after selecting this check box, there is no way to visually distinguish direct relations from implied relations.
6. If the implied relation is created in a matrix or relation map, optionally modify the style of the relation by clicking [IMAGE alt='' src=''] next to the **Style** box to open the **Style** dialog, then select preferred style properties and click **OK**.
7. Click **OK**.

Criteria can be other relations than those listed in the **Criteria** drop-down list. Through advanced manipulations, any operation or even a combination of operations can be set as a criterion, e.g., metachain or find operation. By default, an implied relation operation goes through a predefined hierarchy (a composition and generalization) to collect indirectly associated elements, whereas through advanced manipulations, you can define your own hierarchy.

##### Advanced Manipulations

You can change the **Criteria**, **Source,** and **Target** parameters of an implied relation operation. Specifying the **Source** and **Target** parameters lets you define your own hierarchy. You also have more flexibility in setting criteria with the ability to choose from a larger scope of operations, or even combine a few of them.

Implied relation operation goes through the **Source** parameter value to recursively collect elements, and uses the **Criteria** parameter value to navigate from the **Source** parameter results to implied elements. In addition, the **Target** parameter value recursively collects implied elements starting from the results of the **Criteria** parameter. The results of this operation are the results of the **Criteria** and **Target** parameters.

The parameters that can be specified:

- **Source**: specifies the operation that recursively collects elements starting from the Contextual element. [IMAGE alt='' src=''] In the example above, the value of the **Source** parameter is a generalization (direction – **Source To Target**). This means that starting from the *High-voltage Battery Eco*block, all generic classifiers are collected. In this case, the *High-voltage Battery* block is the result of the **Source** parameter. By default, the values of the **Source** parameter are simple navigation operations: a generalization (direction – **Source To Target**) and part (direction - **Source To Target**). Essentially, when a generalization is selected as the value of the **Source** parameter (direction - **Source To Target**), all general classifiers are recursively collected. And, when the value of the **Source** parameter is a part (direction - **Source To Target**), all composed part types (blocks) are recursively collected.
- **Criteria**: specifies the operation that navigates to the elements from the results of the **Source** parameter. In other words, the value of the **Criteria** parameter is the relation for which implied elements are calculated. In the example above, the value of the **Criteria**parameter is a satisfy (direction – **Source To Target**). It means that starting from the result of the **Source** parameter - the *High-voltage Battery*block, the operation navigates through satisfy relation to collect implied elements. In this case, an implied element is the *Plug-In charge* requirement. Note that, by default, the direction of the criteria is **Source To Target**, and that sub-types are also included.
- **Target**: specifies the operation that recursively collects elements starting from the result of the **Criteria** parameter. [IMAGE alt='' src=''] 
In the example above, the value of the **Target**parameter is a generalization (direction – **Target To Source**). This means that starting from the *AC Unit*block, all composed blocks are collected. In this case, the *Cooler* block is the result of the **Target** parameter. By default, the values of the **Target** parameter are simple navigation operations - a generalization (direction - **Target To Source**) and part (direction - **Target To Source**).

To use an[CONFLUENCE_PAGE title='Opaque Behavior' space='MED']as input, you can place it under the source of the Implied Relation to ensure correct evaluation.

###### Specifying implied relation parameters

To specify implied relation parameters

1. In the criteria list on the left side of the dialog, expand the implied relation operation, and select the parameter you want to specify.
2. In the open pane for specifying the parameter, select the value you want to use. [ATTACHMENT filename='implied_relation_criteria.png']
3. When you finish specifying required parameters, click **OK**. To set the value of the selected parameter as **null**, click the Reset button.

**Related pages**

- Specifying criteria for querying model
  - Getting started with specifying criteria
  - Using Simple Navigation
  - Using Metachain Navigation
  - Using Find operation
  - Creating new operations
    - Calling operations from the model
    - Creating script operations

### EXACT CONFLUENCE STORAGE SOURCE

<!--NOMAGIC_PAGE id=303991051 space=MT version=2 -->
## PAGE 02027: (2026x Refresh1) Using layout templates with diagram aspects

- page_id: `303991051`
- space_key: `MT`
- source_url: https://docs.nomagic.com/spaces/MT/pages/303991051/2026x+Refresh1+Using+layout+templates+with+diagram+aspects
- version_number: 2
- version_date: `2026-04-28T10:48:56.987+02:00`
- ancestors: Versions of Modeling Tools Documentation > (2026x Refresh1) Modeling Tools Documentation > (2026x Refresh1) General modeling features > (2026x Refresh1) Diagramming > (2026x Refresh1) Layout templates
- labels: ['msosa', 'mcse', 'msa']

### NORMALIZED CONTENT

58117776

58117778

58117777

The Class Diagram that is used as a layout template can have a specific[diagram aspect](https://docs.nomagic.com/display/MT/(2026x Refresh1) Diagram+aspects)defined. In other words, the layout template can only be used in another diagram with the same aspect. In the example below, the*Control System*Block Definition Diagram is a layout template with the*electrical*aspect defined, so the*Control System*layout template can only be used in the*electrical*view of the system. In this case, the*Control System*layout template is used in the*Climate Hardware*Internal Block Diagram that is defined as electrical. This layout template can't be applied in the diagram with a different aspect defined, e.g., Communication.

[IMAGE alt='' src='']

###### Usage of a layout template with aspect. The illustration displays concepts from SysML v1 Plugin.

58117774

**Sample model**

The sample model used in the figure of this page is **Diagram Aspects** which you can find in modeling tool *<modeling tool installation directory>\samples\SysML v1\diagram aspects.mdzip.*

### EXACT CONFLUENCE STORAGE SOURCE

````html
<ac:structured-macro ac:name="content-layer" ac:schema-version="1" ac:macro-id="dc39c676-0229-4fd1-a3bb-2fb1f6478dc9"><ac:parameter ac:name="id">58117776</ac:parameter><ac:rich-text-body><ac:structured-macro ac:name="content-column" ac:schema-version="1" ac:macro-id="c11d2b6c-9800-4e1e-8bbd-5a0a065c1ff2"><ac:parameter ac:name="id">58117778</ac:parameter><ac:rich-text-body><ac:structured-macro ac:name="content-block" ac:schema-version="1" ac:macro-id="b6a08214-706a-496f-bba3-f369bd8a6b0d"><ac:parameter ac:name="id">58117777</ac:parameter><ac:rich-text-body><p><span style="color:var(--ds-text,#333333);">The Class Diagram that is used as a layout template can have a specific</span><a href="https://docs.nomagic.com/display/MT/(2026x Refresh1) Diagram+aspects"> diagram aspect</a><span style="color:var(--ds-text,#333333);"> defined. In other words, the layout template can only be used in another diagram with the same aspect. In the example below, the </span><em>Control System</em><span style="color:var(--ds-text,#333333);"> Block Definition Diagram is a layout template with the </span><em>electrical</em><span style="color:var(--ds-text,#333333);"> aspect defined, so the </span><em>Control System</em><span style="color:var(--ds-text,#333333);"> layout template can only be used in the </span><em>electrical</em><span style="color:var(--ds-text,#333333);"> view of the system. In this case, the </span><em>Control System</em><span style="color:var(--ds-text,#333333);"> layout template is used in the </span><em>Climate Hardware </em><span style="color:var(--ds-text,#333333);">Internal Block Diagram that is defined as electrical. This layout template can't be applied in the diagram with a different aspect defined, e.g., Communication.</span></p><p><ac:image ac:align="center"><ri:attachment ri:filename="layout_template_with_aspect.png"><ri:page ri:content-title="(2026x Refresh1) Using layout templates with diagram aspects" /></ri:attachment></ac:image></p><h6 style="text-align: center;">Usage of a layout template with aspect. The illustration displays concepts from SysML v1 Plugin.</h6></ac:rich-text-body></ac:structured-macro><ac:structured-macro ac:name="content-block" ac:schema-version="1" ac:macro-id="4ec35787-f2ec-4906-9040-76f491c5cfd4"><ac:parameter ac:name="id">58117774</ac:parameter><ac:rich-text-body><p class="auto-cursor-target"><strong>Sample model</strong></p><p>The sample model used in the figure of this page is <strong>Diagram Aspects</strong> which you can <span class="confluence-link">find in modeling tool <em>&lt;modeling tool installation directory&gt;\samples\SysML v1\diagram aspects.mdzip.</em></span></p></ac:rich-text-body></ac:structured-macro></ac:rich-text-body></ac:structured-macro></ac:rich-text-body></ac:structured-macro>
````

<!--NOMAGIC_PAGE id=304000378 space=MT version=1 -->
## PAGE 02028: (2026x Refresh1) Using libraries

- page_id: `304000378`
- space_key: `MT`
- source_url: https://docs.nomagic.com/spaces/MT/pages/304000378/2026x+Refresh1+Using+libraries
- version_number: 1
- version_date: `2026-04-28T10:32:05.282+02:00`
- ancestors: Versions of Modeling Tools Documentation > (2026x Refresh1) Modeling Tools Documentation > (2026x Refresh1) UAF 1.3 modeling > (2026x Refresh1) Procedures
- labels: ['libraries', 'universal-joint-task-list', 'ujtl', 'dod-it-standards-registry', 'disr', 'joint-conditions-library', 'add-standard-policy-from-disr-library', 'msosa']

### NORMALIZED CONTENT

715818604

715818606

715818603

**On this page**

715818605

UAF plugin supports three element libraries:

1. The Universal Joint Task List (UJTL).
2. DoD Information Technology Standards and Profile Registry (DISR).
3. Joint Conditions.

The **Universal Joint Task List (UJTL)**, when augmented with the Service task lists, is a comprehensive integrated menu of functional tasks, conditions, measures, and criteria. It supports all levels of the Department of Defense in executing the National Military Strategy. In UAF UJTL library consists of a list of Operational Activities representing universal joint tasks.

The **DoD IT Standards Registry (DISR)** is an online repository of IT standards formerly captured in the Joint Technical Architecture (JTA), Version 6.0. DISR replaces JTA. In UAF, DISR is the list of standard elements that any of UAF element may conform to. DISR Library usage is the same as UJTL library. Library item indexes are included when searching for elements within the element Selection dialog.

The **Joint Conditions Library** supports physical, military, and civil locations, where joint tasks can be performed.You can use it, when selecting Locations.

##### Library versions

You can choose which UJTL or DISR library version to use for your project. By default, libraries will be loaded, but the versions will not be set (except for the Cameo Enterprise Architecture modeling tool, where the latest library versions will be set).

There are the following ways of working with library versions:

- start a new project and work with the existing version of the libraries.
- start a new project with Cameo Enterprise Architecture and choose different version of the libraries (you can choose one of the existing or download using the [CONFLUENCE_PAGE title='(2026x Refresh1) Resource Manager' space=''] ).
- open the existing project with libraries that are in the current modeling tool's version.

###### Choosing library version

When creating a new project, you can choose which library version you will be using.

- By default, the libraries version is not selected for NAF, NAF4, MODAF, UAF EA, and UAF projects.
- By default, the latest libraries version is selected for the DoDAF projects.

To choose which library version will be used

1. [CONFLUENCE_PAGE title='(2026x Refresh1) Creating a UAF project' space=''] .
2. In the New Project dialog, General options, choose the needed version of the preferred library (see the figure below).
3. Click OK .

[IMAGE alt='' src='']

###### Changing library version

You can change the library version at any time.

To change library version

1. In the Containment tree toolbar, click Options and choose Show Auxiliary Resources . [ATTACHMENT filename='containment_tree_toolbar.png']
2. In the Containment tree, right-click the library which version you want to change.
3. From the library shortcut menu, choose Change Library Version and then click the needed version. [ATTACHMENT filename='change_library_version.png']

If the selected library version does not contain elements of the previously used library version, such elements will be marked DELETED, but still remain in your model.

[IMAGE alt='' src='']

****

**Case Study**

Let us say, we need to add a standard or a policy for a system element using the StdV-1 Standards Profile table. We will select a standard from the DISR library.

To add a standard/policy from the DISR library

1. Create the StdV-1 diagram.
2. Create new or add an existing system element.
3. In the Standard / Policy cell of the created or added row, click the Edit button. The Select Standard dialog opens.
4. Click the DISR Library tab. The list of DISR standards is displayed.
5. Select the standard and do one of the following:
  - Double-click the selected standard to add it to the Selected elements list.
  - Click the + button to add the selected standard to the Selected elements list.
6. Click OK , after you finish selecting the standards.

[IMAGE alt='' src='']

### EXACT CONFLUENCE STORAGE SOURCE


<!--NOMAGIC_PAGE id=303991653 space=MT version=1 -->
## PAGE 02029: (2026x Refresh1) Using Lifelines

- page_id: `303991653`
- space_key: `MT`
- source_url: https://docs.nomagic.com/spaces/MT/pages/303991653/2026x+Refresh1+Using+Lifelines
- version_number: 1
- version_date: `2026-04-28T10:20:57.657+02:00`
- ancestors: Versions of Modeling Tools Documentation > (2026x Refresh1) Modeling Tools Documentation > (2026x Refresh1) SysML v1 modeling > (2026x Refresh1) Functional analysis > (2026x Refresh1) Modeling interactions with Sequence Diagrams
- labels: ['mcse', 'msosa']

### NORMALIZED CONTENT

INLINE

A lifeline represents an individual participant in the Interaction. The lifeline represents only one interacting entity, symbolized by a rectangle.

The following steps show you how to work with a lifeline:

##### Assigning a type to a lifeline

To assign a type (classifier) to a lifeline, do one of the following:

- In the lifeline Specification window, in the Type property value, click the ... button, and in the open dialog, select the type. When you are finished, click OK .
- On the diagram, from the lifeline shortcut menu, select Type . In the open list, select the type.
- On the diagram, click a lifeline, and then, on the smart manipulator toolbar, click [ATTACHMENT filename='type_command.png'] . In the open list, select the type.
- On the diagram, click a lifeline, and then press Ctrl+T. In the open list, select the type.
- On the diagram, select a lifeline, and in the name area, type the “:” and the name of the type. Press Enter or click anywhere on the diagram. The type is created and assigned. Type the “:”, press Ctrl+Space, or Ctrl+Backspace. In the open list, select the type.
- In the Containment tree, select an element and drag it to the lifeline shape.

##### Showing/hiding a base classifier

To hide/show a base classifier

- On the diagram, right-click the lifeline. From the shortcut menu, select the Show Classifier check box.

##### Displaying lifelines

You can display lifelines on the Sequence or Communication diagram in the following ways:

- By manually dragging lifeline elements from the Containment tree.
- By automatically using the Display Lifelines dialog.

There are two situations in which you can use the **Display Lifelines** dialog to automatically display lifelines:

- .
- .

newTo display lifelines when creating a new diagram

1. When creating a new Sequence or Communication diagram for an existing classifier, the Display Lifelines dialog opens automatically. [ATTACHMENT filename='Display Lifelines.png']
2. In the opened Display Lifelines dialog, select the lifelines you want to display, and click OK . Selected lifelines are displayed on the diagram pane.

modifyTo display lifelines in a preexisting diagram

1. Select the [CONFLUENCE_PAGE title='(2026x Refresh1) Understanding the user interface' space=''] .
2. Open the Display Lifelines dialog by performing one of the following steps:
  - From the diagram shortcut menu, select Display > Display Lifelines . [ATTACHMENT filename='Shortcut menu_ Display Lifelines..png']
  - On the [CONFLUENCE_PAGE title='(2026x Refresh1) Understanding the user interface' space=''] , click [IMAGE alt='' src=''] and select Display Lifelines .
3. In the dialog, select the lifelines you want to display, and click OK . Selected lifelines are displayed on the diagram pane. [ATTACHMENT filename='displayed_lifelines_in_sequence_diagram.png']

##### Deeply nested parts

The “dot notation” is available for the lifelines. Now you can send messages directly to the deeply nested parts.

To display a deeply nested part

1. Create a Sequence diagram. The **Display Lifelines** dialog opens. You can open the **Display Lifelines** dialog in the existing diagram. On the diagram pane, click the right mouse button and from the shortcut menu, select **Related Elements** > **Display Lifelines**.
2. Select a nested part you want to display. Make sure that the nesting part is not selected, otherwise it will be displayed instead of the nested part.

[IMAGE alt='' src='']

##### Activation bars on lifelines

By default, when there is a message sent between lifelines in a sequence diagram, the lifelines are displayed with activation bars on them. However, you can hide the activation bars from the diagram. 
 
To hide activation bars from lifelines in a sequence diagram

1. Right-click on the diagram pane. From the shortcut menu, select Diagram properties .
2. The Diagram properties of Sequence Diagram dialog opens. Deselect Show Activations check box, setting the value from true to false . [ATTACHMENT filename='Show Activations_Dialog.png']

**Sample model**

The models used in the figures of this page is the **Communication diagram** and **Sequence diagram** sample models.

To open these samples

- Find in modeling tool: - <modeling tool installation directory>\samples\diagrams\communication diagram.mdzip. 
 - <modeling tool installation directory>\samples\diagrams\sequence diagram.mdzip.

Related pages

- [CONFLUENCE_PAGE title='(2026x Refresh1) Sequence diagram' space='']
- [CONFLUENCE_PAGE title='(2026x Refresh1) Communication diagram' space='']
- [CONFLUENCE_PAGE title='(2026x Refresh1) Creating diagrams' space='']
- [CONFLUENCE_PAGE title='UML elements' space='MED']

### EXACT CONFLUENCE STORAGE SOURCE

````html

The lifeline represents only one interacting entity, symbolized by a rectangle.</p><p>The following steps show you how to work with a lifeline:</p><p><ac:structured-macro ac:name="toc" ac:schema-version="1" ac:macro-id="118a1089-8d61-4662-83ea-0d78de432f91" /></p><h3>Assigning a type to a lifeline</h3><p>To assign a type (classifier) to a lifeline, do one of the following:</p><hr /><ul><li>In the lifeline Specification window, in the Type property value, click the <strong>...</strong> button, and in the open dialog, select the type. lifelines in a preexisting diagram</p><hr /><ol><li>Select the <ac:link><ri:page ri:content-title="(2026x Refresh1) Understanding the user interface" /><ac:plain-text-link-body><![CDATA[diagram pane]]></ac:plain-text-link-body></ac:link>.</li><li>Open the <strong>Display Lifelines</strong> dialog by performing one of the following steps:<br /><ul><li>From the diagram shortcut menu, select <strong>Display</strong> &gt; <strong>Display Lifelines</strong>.<br /><br /><ac:image><ri:attachment ri:filename="Shortcut menu_ Display Lifelines..png" /></ac:image><br /><br /></li><li>On the <ac:link><ri:page ri:content-title="(2026x Refresh1) Understanding the user interface" /><ac:plain-text-link-body><![CDATA[diagram toolbar]]></ac:plain-text-link-body></ac:link>, click <span class="confluence-embedded-file-wrapper"> <ac:image ac:title="Display" ac:alt="Display"><ri:attachment ri:filename="Display.png" /></ac:image> </span> and select <strong>Display Lifelines</strong>.<br /><br /></li></ul></li><li>In the dialog, select the lifelines you want to display, and click <strong>OK</strong>.<br />Selected lifelines are displayed on the diagram pane.<br /><br /><ac:image ac:title="Displayed lifelines on the Order Ticket Sequence diagram pane." ac:alt="Displayed lifelines on the Order Ticket Sequence diagram pane."><ri:attachment ri:filename="displayed_lifelines_in_sequence_diagram.png" /></ac:image></li></ol><h3>Deeply nested parts</h3><p>The “dot notation” is available for the lifelines. Now you can send messages directly to the deeply nested parts.</p><p>To display a deeply nested part</p><hr /><ol><li><p>Create a Sequence diagram. The <strong>Display Lifelines</strong> dialog opens.</p><ac:structured-macro ac:name="tip" ac:schema-version="1" ac:macro-id="98fd5ffa-bb9d-4254-92e5-33de9319cf50"><ac:rich-text-body><p>You can open the <strong>Display Lifelines</strong> dialog in the existing diagram. On the diagram pane, click the right mouse button and from the shortcut menu, select <strong>Related Elements</strong> &gt; <strong>Display Lifelines</strong>.</p></ac:rich-text-body></ac:structured-macro></li><li><p>Select a nested part you want to display.</p><ac:structured-macro ac:name="note" ac:schema-version="1" ac:macro-id="97000b27-6df9-4af6-a592-d6842556549d"><ac:rich-text-body><p>Make sure that the nesting part is not selected, otherwise it will be displayed instead of the nested part.</p></ac:rich-text-body></ac:structured-macro></li></ol><p><ac:image><ri:attachment ri:filename="Dot Notation.png" /></ac:image></p><h3>Activation bars on lifelines</h3><p>By default, when there is a message sent between lifelines in a sequence diagram, the lifelines are displayed with activation bars on them. However, you can hide the activation bars from the diagram.<br /><br />To hide activation bars from lifelines in a sequence diagram</p><hr /><ol><li>Right-click on the diagram pane. From the shortcut menu, select <strong>Diagram properties</strong>.</li><li>The <strong>Diagram properties of Sequence Diagram</strong> dialog opens. Deselect <strong>Show Activations</strong> check box, setting the value from <strong>true</strong> to <strong>false</strong>.<br /><br /><ac:image><ri:attachment ri:filename="Show Activations_Dialog.png" /></ac:image></li></ol></ac:rich-text-body></ac:structured-macro><p><strong style="letter-spacing: 0.0px;">Sample model</strong></p><p>The models used in the figures of this page is the <strong>Communication diagram</strong> and <strong>Sequence diagram</strong> sample models.</p><p>To open these samples</p><hr /><ul><li>Find in modeling tool:<br />   - <em>&lt;modeling tool installation directory&gt;\samples\diagrams\communication diagram.mdzip.<br />   - &lt;modeling tool installation directory&gt;\samples\diagrams\sequence diagram.mdzip.</em></li></ul> <strong style="letter-spacing: 0.0px;">Related pages</strong><ul><li><ac:link><ri:page ri:content-title="(2026x Refresh1) Sequence diagram" /></ac:link></li><li><ac:link><ri:page ri:content-title="(2026x Refresh1) Communication diagram" /></ac:link></li><li><ac:link><ri:page ri:content-title="(2026x Refresh1) Creating diagrams" /></ac:link></li><li><ac:link><ri:page ri:content-title="UML elements" ri:space-key="MED" /><ac:plain-text-link-body><![CDATA[Model Elements]]></ac:plain-text-link-body></ac:link></li></ul>
````

<!--NOMAGIC_PAGE id=303997627 space=MT version=2 -->
## PAGE 02030: (2026x Refresh1) Using Metachain Navigation

- page_id: `303997627`
- space_key: `MT`
- source_url: https://docs.nomagic.com/spaces/MT/pages/303997627/2026x+Refresh1+Using+Metachain+Navigation
- version_number: 2
- version_date: `2026-04-28T10:48:47.759+02:00`
- ancestors: Versions of Modeling Tools Documentation > (2026x Refresh1) Modeling Tools Documentation > (2026x Refresh1) General modeling features > (2026x Refresh1) Model analysis > (2026x Refresh1) Specifying criteria for querying model
- labels: ['msosa', 'mcse', 'msa']

### NORMALIZED CONTENT

#### CONTENT-LAYER: Information

2216620902

INLINE

#### CONTENT-COLUMN: Information

2216620905

INLINE

2145362930

**On this page:**

#### CONTENT-BLOCK: Information

2216620904

INLINE

Metachain operation is one of the operation types used to specify criteria for querying models. Use the **Metachain Navigation** operation type when specifying indirect (multi-level) relations between elements through chains of properties.

- Metachains allow you to search for indirectly related elements, although they do not support loops and recursive relations.
- Please note that the Metachain Navigation operation returns a set.

##### Specifying a Metachain Navigation operation as criteria

To specify a Metachain Navigation operation as criteria

1. Click Metachain Navigation on the left side of the dialog box. [ATTACHMENT filename='metachain_navigation.png']
2. Change the name of the operation, if needed.
3. Click [IMAGE alt='' src=''] to set a style that represents the specified metachain navigation symbol. The Style option is only available in Dependency Matrices and Relation Maps.
4. Click the **Insert** button to create a new step of the metachain. A new row is added to the list in which you need to specify a metaclass or a stereotype as well as a property or an operation. [IMAGE alt='' src='']
5. Repeat the previous step to create as many steps of the metachain as you need for specifying the criteria. To remove a step from the metachain, click the**Remove**button.To create another Metachain operation, click **Create operation** > **Metachain Navigation**.

In the table below you can see the options that can be specified for Metachain Navigation.

| Option | Description |
| --- | --- |
| Metaclass or Stereotype | Either a metaclass or a stereotype of a metachain step (see the image below). The first step, when created, already has a metaclass or a stereotype selected by default. This value is the context element type. The default metaclass or stereotype of the first step can be changed. The second step and all further steps are more specific: the values suggested for selection are limited according to the values selected in the first step since you will not be able to add a new step until the current step is not specified. |
| Property | A property or operation of the selected metaclass or stereotype. |
| Unique | Only one identical element will count as the result if the check box is selected.Note: the option is unavailable if Simple Navigation is nested directly under the root union. In such a case, the root union controls the uniqueness.The option is especially important to return non-unique numeric values to correctly execute mathematical operations (such as Sum). |

##### Advanced Manipulations

###### Changing the Contextual element of a Metachain Navigation criterion

To change the [CONFLUENCE_PAGE title='(2026x Refresh1) Specifying criteria for querying model' space=''] of a Metachain Navigation criterion

1. On the left side of the dialog box, expand the Metachain Navigation criterion and select the Context parameter.
2. Click the Reset button on the Context specification panel. [ATTACHMENT filename='metachain_navigation_context.png'] The value of the Context parameter becomes null.
3. Select a new context from the list of operations and element types. InformationTo restore the original context, expand the **Other** group, and click **Contextual Variable**.[IMAGE alt='' src='']

###### Using the expression of a Metachain Navigation operation as a parameter of another operation

To use the expression of a Metachain Navigation operation as a parameter of another operation

1. On the left side of the dialog box, Click Metachain Navigation .
2. On the criteria specification panel, click the **Use as** button, and select the appropriate command. 
[IMAGE alt='' src='']
3. Specify other parameters of the new operation.

###### Filtering results by type

To filter results by type

1. Next to the Result Filter by Type line , click the button. Select Element Type dialog opens.
2. [CONFLUENCE_PAGE title='(2026x Refresh1) Selecting element types' space=''] .
3. [CONFLUENCE_PAGE title='(2026x Refresh1) Selecting element types' space=''] . 
[IMAGE alt='' src='']

2216620901

INLINE

**Related pages**

- [CONFLUENCE_PAGE title='(2026x Refresh1) Selecting element types' space=''] [CONFLUENCE_PAGE title='(2026x Refresh1) Selecting element types' space='']

<!--NOMAGIC_PAGE id=303997501 space=MT version=1 -->
## PAGE 02031: (2026x Refresh1) Using Model Patch

- page_id: `303997501`
- space_key: `MT`
- source_url: https://docs.nomagic.com/spaces/MT/pages/303997501/2026x+Refresh1+Using+Model+Patch
- version_number: 1
- version_date: `2026-04-28T10:28:16.448+02:00`
- ancestors: Versions of Modeling Tools Documentation > (2026x Refresh1) Modeling Tools Documentation > (2026x Refresh1) General modeling features > (2026x Refresh1) Tools > (2026x Refresh1) Model Patch
- labels: ['msosa', 'mcse', 'msa']

### NORMALIZED CONTENT

**On this page**

4

**NotePlease note before creating a model patch!**

Since model elements are usually related to and depend on other elements, you must include the necessary elements in the scope. The Model Patch mechanism does not automatically check dependencies or include them in the patch data.

To avoid data loss:

- Go to **Related Elements > Depends On** and check if you are bringing all the elements that your elements depend on; or
- Ensure that the target version (the project where you are applying the patch) already has the dependent elements.

For example, you have Part A that has Type B. If you want to bring Part A, you must either:

- Include Type B in the patch as well, or
- Make sure Type B already exists in the target.

Failure to do this will lead to data loss, as Part A will be created without a type.

##### Creating a model patch

To create a model patch

1. Open the source project.
2. Do one of the following:
  - In the main menu, select Tools > Patch > Create Patch .
  - Right-click an element in the Containment tree and select Tools > Create Patch.
3. In the Create a Patch dialog, select the elements you want to include in the patch using the Add , Remove , and Remove All buttons. If you select an element that has child elements, all the child elements are automatically included in the patch. [ATTACHMENT filename='selecting_patch_scope.png']
4. Click Next .
5. In the next step, select the type of patch you want to create:
  - All selected content - This type of patch brings all the selected scope to the target project potentially overriding existing elements.
  - Changes from selected version - This type of patch adds the selected scope to the target project as concurrent changes. If you select this patch type, also select a project version. All changes from the selected version, subsequent versions, and the current project version will be included in the patch. This patch type is only available for Teamwork Cloud projects. [ATTACHMENT filename='patch_types.png']
6. Click Next.
7. Click [ATTACHMENT filename='three_dots_button.png'] and select the location where you want to save the patch file.
8. Click Save.

##### Applying model patch

To apply a model patch

1. Open the target project.
2. In the main menu, select Tools > Patch > Apply Patch .
3. Select the patch file and click Open.
4. In the Apply Patch window, review the changes that will be applied to the project. [ATTACHMENT filename='apply_patch.png']
5. Click the Apply Patch button.
6. Investigate the results:
  - If you receive a message that the patch is applied successfully, it means all changes from the patch were successfully applied.
  - If you receive a warning that the patch has been applied, but some changes could not be applied, click **OK** and review the changes that were not applied in the open **Changes That Are Not Applied** pane. In the **Changes That Are Not Applied** pane, select each change and read the description explaining the reason of the issue. To avoid such issues, remember tobefore creating a model patch. [ATTACHMENT filename='not_applied_changes.png']

### EXACT CONFLUENCE STORAGE SOURCE

````html
<ac:layout><ac:layout-section ac:type="single"><ac:layout-cell><p><strong>On this page</strong></p><p><ac:structured-macro ac:name="toc" ac:schema-version="1" ac:macro-id="86b2ef22-7d1e-422b-bcba-bafa5244528c"><ac:parameter ac:name="maxLevel">4</ac:parameter></ac:structured-macro></p></ac:layout-cell></ac:layout-section><ac:layout-section ac:type="single"><ac:layout-cell><p><br /></p></ac:layout-cell></ac:layout-section><ac:layout-section ac:type="single"><ac:layout-cell><ac:structured-macro ac:name="note" ac:schema-version="1" ac:macro-id="b384de94-7bdf-4ef8-a6fe-a4ee28f7a268"><ac:rich-text-body><p><span style="color: rgb(0,0,0);"><strong><ac:structured-macro ac:name="anchor" ac:schema-version="1" ac:macro-id="2284355a-ae83-4d60-bfb5-34009764b3f4"><ac:parameter ac:name="">Note</ac:parameter></ac:structured-macro>Please note before creating a model patch!</strong></span></p><p><span style="color: rgb(0,0,0);">Since model elements are usually related to and depend on other elements, you must include the necessary elements in the scope. The Model Patch mechanism does not automatically check dependencies or include them in the patch data.</span></p><p><span style="color: rgb(0,0,0);">To avoid data loss:</span></p><ul><li><span style="color: rgb(0,0,0);">Go to <strong>Related Elements &gt; Depends On</strong> and check if you are bringing all the elements that your elements depend on; or</span></li><li><span style="color: rgb(0,0,0);">Ensure that the target version (the project where you are applying the patch) already has the dependent elements.</span></li></ul><p><span style="color: rgb(0,0,0);">For example, you have Part A that has Type B. If you want to bring Part A, you must either:</span></p><ul><li><span style="color: rgb(0,0,0);">Include Type B in the patch as well, or</span></li><li><span style="color: rgb(0,0,0);">Make sure Type B already exists in the target.</span></li></ul><p><span style="color: rgb(0,0,0);">Failure to do this will lead to data loss, as Part A will be created without a type.</span></p></ac:rich-text-body></ac:structured-macro><h3>Creating a model patch</h3><p><br /></p><p>To create a model patch</p><hr /><ol><li>Open the source project.</li><li>Do one of the following:<ul><li>In the main menu, select <strong>Tools &gt; Patch &gt; Create Patch</strong>.</li><li>Right-click an element in the Containment tree and select <strong>Tools &gt; Create Patch.</strong></li></ul></li><li>In the <strong>Create a Patch</strong> dialog, select the elements you want to include in the patch using the <strong>Add</strong>, <strong>Remove</strong>, and <strong>Remove</strong> <strong>All</strong> buttons.<br /><ac:structured-macro ac:name="tip" ac:schema-version="1" ac:macro-id="26c1300c-1cb4-4b69-8a8f-3d98d5d52607"><ac:rich-text-body>If you select an element that has child elements, all the child elements are automatically included in the patch.</ac:rich-text-body></ac:structured-macro><br /><ac:image><ri:attachment ri:filename="selecting_patch_scope.png"><ri:page ri:content-title="(2026x Refresh1) Using Model Patch" /></ri:attachment></ac:image><br /><br /></li><li>Click <strong>Next</strong>.</li><li>In the next step, select the type of patch you want to create:<ul><li><strong>All selected content</strong> - This type of patch brings all the selected scope to the target project potentially overriding existing elements. </li><li><strong>Changes from selected version</strong> - This type of patch adds the selected scope to the target project as concurrent changes. If you select this patch type, also select a project version. All changes from the selected version, subsequent versions, and the current project version will be included in the patch.<br /><ac:structured-macro ac:name="note" ac:schema-version="1" ac:macro-id="bb75f378-42e7-45bb-940c-f58495270cf7"><ac:rich-text-body>This patch type is only available for Teamwork Cloud projects.</ac:rich-text-body></ac:structured-macro><br /><ac:image><ri:attachment ri:filename="patch_types.png"><ri:page ri:content-title="(2026x Refresh1) Using Model Patch" /></ri:attachment></ac:image><br /><br /></li></ul></li><li>Click <strong>Next.</strong></li><li>Click <ac:image ac:thumbnail="true" ac:height="18"><ri:attachment ri:filename="three_dots_button.png"><ri:page ri:content-title="(2026x Refresh1) Using Model Patch" /></ri:attachment></ac:image> and select the location where you want to save the patch file.</li><li>Click <strong>Save.</strong></li></ol><h3>Applying model patch</h3><p><br /></p><p>To apply a model patch</p><hr /><ol><li>Open the target project.</li><li>In the main menu, select <strong>Tools &gt; Patch &gt; Apply Patch</strong>.</li><li>Select the patch file and click <strong>Open.</strong></li><li>In the <strong>Apply Patch</strong> window, review the changes that will be applied to the project.<br /><br /><ac:image><ri:attachment ri:filename="apply_patch.png"><ri:page ri:content-title="(2026x Refresh1) Using Model Patch" /></ri:attachment></ac:image><br /><br /></li><li>Click the <strong>Apply Patch</strong> button.</li><li>Investigate the results:<ul><li><p>If you receive a message that the patch is applied successfully, it means all changes from the patch were successfully applied.</p></li><li><p>If you receive a warning that the patch has been applied, but some changes could not be applied, click <strong>OK</strong> and review the changes that were not applied in the open <span><strong>Changes That Are Not Applied</strong> pane</span>.</p><ac:structured-macro ac:name="tip" ac:schema-version="1" ac:macro-id="e6144784-c49b-43c1-8ae4-d7fef3cf21b4"><ac:rich-text-body><span>In the <strong>Changes That Are Not Applied</strong> pane, select each change and read the description explaining the reason of the issue. To avoid such issues, remember to </span><ac:link ac:anchor="Note"><ac:plain-text-link-body><![CDATA[check element dependencies ]]></ac:plain-text-link-body></ac:link><span>before creating a model patch.</span></ac:rich-text-body></ac:structured-macro><br /><ac:image><ri:attachment ri:filename="not_applied_changes.png"><ri:page ri:content-title="(2026x Refresh1) Using Model Patch" /></ri:attachment></ac:image></li></ul></li></ol></ac:layout-cell></ac:layout-section></ac:layout>
````

<!--NOMAGIC_PAGE id=303993762 space=MT version=1 -->
## PAGE 02032: (2026x Refresh1) Using NetBeans IDE to develop Groovy Scripts

- page_id: `303993762`
- space_key: `MT`
- source_url: https://docs.nomagic.com/spaces/MT/pages/303993762/2026x+Refresh1+Using+NetBeans+IDE+to+develop+Groovy+Scripts
- version_number: 1
- version_date: `2026-04-28T10:23:31.386+02:00`
- ancestors: Versions of Modeling Tools Documentation > (2026x Refresh1) Modeling Tools Documentation > (2026x Refresh1) General modeling features > (2026x Refresh1) Macro engine > (2026x Refresh1) Supported scripting languages
- labels: ['msosa', 'mcse', 'msa']

### NORMALIZED CONTENT

You will need NetBeans IDE 6.7.1 or 6.8 with Groovy Plugin to develop Groovy scripts. The Groovy support comes with NetBeans "Java" and "All" package. If your NetBeans does not have Groovy, download the plugin through NetBeans Plugin Manager. For more information about adding a new plugin, click**IDE Basics** >**Plugins** > **Updating the IDE** on the NetBeans **Help** menu.

Use the following three simple steps to develop and run a Groovy script in NetBeans:

1. Set up a classpath.
2. Develop a Groovy script.

To set up a classpath

1. Click Tools > Libraries on the main menu. The Library Manager dialog will open.
2. Click the New Library button. The New Library dialog will open.
3. Specify a library name, for example, MD16.8. The Library Type must be Class Libraries.
4. Click OK to close the New Library dialog.
5. Select your new library in the Libraries tree.
6. Click the Add JAR/Folder button and add all the JAR files in <modeling tool name>/lib .
7. Repeat steps 2 to 6 to add the Groovy library that is in <modeling tool name>/plugins/com.nomagic.magicdraw.automaton/engines/groovy-2.0.1/embeddable/groovy-all-2.0.1.jar.
8. Click OK to close the Library Manager dialog.

To develop a Groovy script

1. Click File > New Project on the main menu to create a Java application project. The New Project dialog will open.
2. Select Java in the Categories box and Java Application in the Projects box, and then click Next .
3. Choose a project location. Be sure that you do not select Create Main Class .
4. Click Finish .
5. Expand your project node in the Project window. The Libraries node will appear.
6. Right-click the Libraries node and select Add Library from the shortcut menu.
7. Click File > New File on the main menu to add a new Groovy file.
8. Select Groovy in the Categories box and Groovy Script in the File Types box.
9. Follow the instructions until finish.

### EXACT CONFLUENCE STORAGE SOURCE

````html
<ac:layout><ac:layout-section ac:type="two_right_sidebar"><ac:layout-cell><p>You will need NetBeans IDE 6.7.1 or 6.8 with Groovy Plugin to develop Groovy scripts. The Groovy support comes with NetBeans &quot;Java&quot; and &quot;All&quot; package. If your NetBeans does not have Groovy, download the plugin through NetBeans Plugin Manager. For more information about adding a new plugin, click<strong style="line-height: 1.4285715;"> IDE Basics</strong> &gt;<strong style="line-height: 1.4285715;"> Plugins</strong> &gt; <strong style="line-height: 1.4285715;">Updating the IDE</strong> on the NetBeans <strong style="line-height: 1.4285715;">Help</strong> menu.</p><p>Use the following three simple steps to develop and run a Groovy script in NetBeans:</p><ol><li>Set up a classpath.</li><li>Develop a Groovy script.</li></ol><p><br /></p><p>To set up a classpath</p><hr /><ol><li>Click <strong>Tools</strong> &gt; <strong>Libraries</strong> on the main menu. The <strong>Library Manager</strong> dialog will open.</li><li>Click the <strong>New Library</strong> button. The <strong>New Library</strong> dialog will open.</li><li>Specify a library name, for example, MD16.8. The Library Type must be Class Libraries.</li><li>Click <strong>OK</strong> to close the <strong>New Library</strong> dialog.</li><li>Select your new library in the Libraries tree.</li><li>Click the <strong>Add JAR/Folder</strong> button and add all the JAR files in <em>&lt;modeling tool name&gt;/lib</em>.</li><li>Repeat steps 2 to 6 to add the Groovy library that is in <em>&lt;modeling tool name&gt;/plugins/com.nomagic.magicdraw.automaton/engines/groovy-2.0.1/embeddable/groovy-all-2.0.1.jar.</em></li><li>Click <strong>OK</strong> to close the <strong>Library Manager</strong> dialog.</li></ol><p><br /></p><p>To develop a Groovy script</p><hr /><ol><li>Click <strong>File</strong> &gt; <strong>New Project</strong> on the main menu to create a Java application project. The <strong>New Project</strong> dialog will open.</li><li>Select <strong>Java</strong> in the <strong>Categories</strong> box and <strong>Java Application</strong> in the<strong> Projects</strong> box, and then click <strong>Next</strong>.</li><li>Choose a project location. Be sure that you do not select <strong>Create Main Class</strong>.</li><li>Click <strong>Finish</strong>.</li><li>Expand your project node in the <strong>Project</strong> window. The Libraries node will appear.</li><li>Right-click the Libraries node and select <strong>Add Library</strong> from the shortcut menu.</li><li>Click <strong>File</strong> &gt; <strong>New File</strong> on the main menu to add a new Groovy file.</li><li>Select Groovy in the <strong>Categories</strong> box and Groovy Script in the <strong>File Types</strong> box.</li><li>Follow the instructions until finish.</li></ol></ac:layout-cell><ac:layout-cell><p><br /></p></ac:layout-cell></ac:layout-section></ac:layout>
````

<!--NOMAGIC_PAGE id=303998780 space=MT version=2 -->
## PAGE 02033: (2026x Refresh1) Using Organization Structure Diagram

- page_id: `303998780`
- space_key: `MT`
- source_url: https://docs.nomagic.com/spaces/MT/pages/303998780/2026x+Refresh1+Using+Organization+Structure+Diagram
- version_number: 2
- version_date: `2026-04-28T10:47:49.870+02:00`
- ancestors: Versions of Modeling Tools Documentation > (2026x Refresh1) Modeling Tools Documentation > (2026x Refresh1) BPMN modeling > (2026x Refresh1) Using Cameo Business Modeler Plugin
- labels: ['msosa']

### NORMALIZED CONTENT

##### Creating and Using Resources

You can create a Resource inside a package. The Resource is not displayed on a BPMN2 diagram as it is represented by a Pool, Data Object, and Choreography Task.

To create a Resource on a Organization Structure diagram

- On the Organization Structure Diagram pallet, click the Resource button.

To create a Resource in the Containment tree

- Right-click a package in the Containment tree. On the shortcut menu, click Create Element > Resource .

To change a type of Resource

- Right-click the Resource and on the shortcut menu select needed Resource type.

To review traceability information related to a Resource

1. Right-click a created Resource in the Containment tree and click Specification to open the Resource Specification window.
2. In the Resource Specification window, select Traceability .

**Related elements**

- Resource
- Organization Unit
- Role
- Person
- Information system

**Related diagrams**

- BPMN Process Diagram
- BPMN Collaboration Diagram
- BPMN Choreography Diagram

**Related procedures**

- [CONFLUENCE_PAGE title='(2026x Refresh1) Creating diagrams' space='']
- Using BPMN Element Numbers

### EXACT CONFLUENCE STORAGE SOURCE

<!--NOMAGIC_PAGE id=303992748 space=MT version=1 -->
## PAGE 02034: (2026x Refresh1) Using other projects in a project

- page_id: `303992748`
- space_key: `MT`
- source_url: https://docs.nomagic.com/spaces/MT/pages/303992748/2026x+Refresh1+Using+other+projects+in+a+project
- version_number: 1
- version_date: `2026-04-28T10:22:15.845+02:00`
- ancestors: Versions of Modeling Tools Documentation > (2026x Refresh1) Modeling Tools Documentation > (2026x Refresh1) General modeling features > (2026x Refresh1) Working with projects > (2026x Refresh1) Project partitioning > (2026x Refresh1) Composing model
- labels: ['msosa', 'mcse', 'msa']

### NORMALIZED CONTENT

305408088

305408090

305408089

When a project is used in another project, shared elements of the used project become visible and accessiblein the Containment tree of the project that uses them as if they were a part of the project.

##### Using local projects

To start using a project

1. Do one of the following: The Use Project wizard opens.
  - From the File menu, select Use Project > Use Local Project .
  - From the Options menu select Project Usages and click the Use Project button.
2. In the Select project step, select the project you want to use from the file system or from the predefined location:
  - If you want to use your custom project from a custom location, select to use a project From file system and click [ATTACHMENT filename='3dot_button.png'] next to the Project file box to navigate to the desired file.
  - If you want to use the project coming with modeling tool installation, select to use a project**From predefined**location, then select a path from the**Paths to used projects**list and the project to use fromthe list below. You can customize the **Paths to used projects** list and add your own paths by [CONFLUENCE_PAGE title='(2026x Refresh1) Creating path variables' space=''].
3. Click the Next button. [IMAGE alt='' src='']
4. In the Specify usage options step, [CONFLUENCE_PAGE title='Managing project usages' space='CM'] , such as the accessibility mode, load mode, and mounting locations of shared packages.
5. Click the Finish button.

After your project starts using another project, the elements of the used project are still stored separately:

- Used project elements — in the used project file.
- Project elements — in the project file.

##### Modifying project accessibility

The accessibility mode of the used project can be specified in the **Used Projects** dialog. By default, projects are used in the read-only mode.

To change the accessibility mode of a used project

1. From the Options menu, select Project Usages . The Used Projects dialog opens.
2. In the tree on the left side of the open dialog, select the used project and click the Read-only or Read-write option in the Accessibility area.
3. Click OK .

If you need to change the contents of the project used in the read-only mode, you can open it separately from the project that uses it. For this, click the **Open Project** button in the same dialog.

305408087

**Related pages**

- [CONFLUENCE_PAGE title='(2026x Refresh1) Importing models' space='']
- [CONFLUENCE_PAGE title='(2026x Refresh1) Managing used projects' space='']

used project file.</li><li>Project elements — in the project file.</li></ul><h3>Modifying project accessibility</h3><p><ac:inline-comment-marker ac:ref="abd1ad40-ec60-46e8-9e09-a5f70ae64aed">The acces</ac:inline-comment-marker>sibility mode of the used project can be specified in the <strong>Used Projects</strong> dialog. By default, projects are used in the read-only mode.</p><p>To change the accessibility mode of a used project</p><hr /><ol><li>From the <strong>Options</strong> menu, select <strong>Project Usages</strong>. The <strong>Used Projects</strong> dialog opens.</li><li>In the tree on the left side of the open dialog, select the used project and click the <strong>Read-only</strong> or <strong>Read-write</strong> option in the <strong>Accessibility</strong> area.</li><li>Click <strong>OK</strong>.</li></ol><p>If you need to change the contents of the project used in the read-only mode, you can open it separately from the project that uses it. For this, click the <strong>Open Project</strong> button in the same dialog.</p></ac:rich-text-body></ac:structured-macro><ac:structured-macro ac:name="content-block" ac:schema-version="1" ac:macro-id="7e8fa884-63cc-41db-925a-73ab8bee6e1f"><ac:parameter ac:name="id">305408087</ac:parameter><ac:rich-text-body><p><strong>Related pages</strong></p><ul><li><ac:link><ri:page ri:content-title="(2026x Refresh1) Importing models" /></ac:link></li><li><ac:link><ri:page ri:content-title="(2026x Refresh1) Managing used projects" /></ac:link></li></ul></ac:rich-text-body></ac:structured-macro></ac:rich-text-body></ac:structured-macro></ac:rich-text-body></ac:structured-macro>

<!--NOMAGIC_PAGE id=303999855 space=MT version=1 -->
## PAGE 02035: (2026x Refresh1) Using packages

- page_id: `303999855`
- space_key: `MT`
- source_url: https://docs.nomagic.com/spaces/MT/pages/303999855/2026x+Refresh1+Using+packages
- version_number: 1
- version_date: `2026-04-28T10:31:18.308+02:00`
- ancestors: Versions of Modeling Tools Documentation > (2026x Refresh1) Modeling Tools Documentation > (2026x Refresh1) SysML v1 modeling > (2026x Refresh1) Organizing your model
- labels: ['mcse', 'msosa']

### NORMALIZED CONTENT

There are multiple ways to organize your model into [CONFLUENCE_PAGE title='(2026x Refresh1) Package' space=''].The S. Friedenthal in the "Practical Guide to SysML" recommends organizing your model structure in the following ways:

- By system hierarchy (e.g., system level, subsystem level, component level).
- By process life cycle where each model subpackage represents a stage in the process (e.g., requirements analysis, system design).
- By teams that are working on the model (e.g., Requirements Team, Integrated Product Team (IPT) 1, 2).
- By the type of model elements contained in it (e.g., requirements, behavior, structure).
- By model elements that are likely to change together.
- By model elements organized to support reuse (e.g., model libraries).
- By other logical or cohesive groupings of model elements based on defined model-partitioning criteria.
- A combination of the preceding.

You can also organize your model structure according to your internal methodologies. The procedures of managing Packages in the modeling tool are provided in the[CONFLUENCE_PAGE title='Working with packages' space='MED'] and [CONFLUENCE_PAGE title='Package import' space='MED'] pages. 
In the following figure, the Packages are organized according to the MagicGrid approach. You can use this predefined package structure by creating a MagicGridBlank project. You can find the example and guidelines of how to use the MagicGrid approach by creating the MagicGridQuickStart project. [CONFLUENCE_PAGE title='(2026x Refresh1) Creating SysML v1 projects' space='']>]]> 
 
 
[IMAGE alt='' src='']

###### The Package structure of MagicGrid project.

##### Automated Package Structure Creation in MagicGrid Projects

The package structure is automatically created upon the creation of the MagicGrid project. However, you can also create package structures for any additional components or subsystems within the solution domain of your model. You can do that by using predefined package structure configurations. To learn more, see the [CONFLUENCE_PAGE title='(2026x Refresh1) Automated Package Structure Creation' space=''] page.

To create a predefined package structure in a MagicGrid project

1. Right-click a block you want to create a package structure for, and in its shortcut menu, select Tools > Create Package Structure . The Select Package Structure dialog opens.
2. In the dialog, select the class you require:
  1. To create a package structure for a component, select **PackagesForComponent** class.
  2. To create a package structure for a subsystem, select **PackagesForSubsystem**class.
3. Click OK . A package structure is created for the selected element. [ATTACHMENT filename='additional_subsystems.png']

### EXACT CONFLUENCE STORAGE SOURCE

<!--NOMAGIC_PAGE id=303997786 space=MT version=2 -->
## PAGE 02036: (2026x Refresh1) Using Property Test operation

- page_id: `303997786`
- space_key: `MT`
- source_url: https://docs.nomagic.com/spaces/MT/pages/303997786/2026x+Refresh1+Using+Property+Test+operation
- version_number: 2
- version_date: `2026-04-28T10:48:54.704+02:00`
- ancestors: Versions of Modeling Tools Documentation > (2026x Refresh1) Modeling Tools Documentation > (2026x Refresh1) General modeling features > (2026x Refresh1) Model analysis > (2026x Refresh1) Specifying criteria for querying model
- labels: ['msosa', 'mcse', 'msa']

### NORMALIZED CONTENT

#### CONTENT-LAYER: Property information

69029293

#### CONTENT-COLUMN: Property information

69029305

69029292

**On this page**

#### CONTENT-BLOCK: Property information

69029294

A Property Test operation checks if model elements contain the selected properties with the specified values. If the property values of a model element match the specified property values, the operation returns *true*, and if they do not match -*false.*

##### Specifying a Property Test operation

To specify a Property Test operation

1. Open a [CONFLUENCE_PAGE title='(2026x Refresh1) Getting started with specifying criteria' space=''] .
2. From the operation list on the left side of the dialog, select the**Property Test**operation. If Property Test operation node is not displayed, select **Create operation**, and then select **Property Test** in the **Operations** area on the right side of the dialog. The **Property Test** panel appears.
3. From the property list, select the desired properties and specify their values, as shown below. 
[IMAGE alt='' src=''] ImportantIf**Show test criteria available only for context**is set to true, the list of properties available for testing is reduced to properties applicable only to the [CONFLUENCE_PAGE title='(2026x Refresh1) Specifying criteria for querying model' space=''] type.
4. Click OK and close the dialog for querying the model if it is still open.

After specifying a Property Test operation, it checks if model elements contain the property values that you specified in the step 3. For example, if you select the **risk** property and set it to **High**, as highlighted in the figure above, the Property Test operation returns *true* for all model elements with the **risk** property set to **High**. Advanced operation properties can be specified through advanced manipulations.

##### Advanced Manipulations

###### Specifying a Property Test operation through advanced manipulations

To specify a Property Test operation through advanced manipulations

1. Open the [CONFLUENCE_PAGE title='(2026x Refresh1) Getting started with specifying criteria' space=''] .
2. In the operation list on the left side of the dialog, expand the Property Test node.
3. Select the desired property from the list under the Property Test node highlighted in the figure below, then specify its value in the specification panel on the right side of the dialog. [ATTACHMENT filename='specifying_property_test_properties.png']
4. Click OK and close the dialog for querying model if it is still open.

#### NOTE: Property information

Property information

If **AND Operator** is set to *true* (default value), the operation returns *true* only for the elements that contain all of the selected properties with specified values. If **AND Operator** is set to *false*, the operation returns *true* for the elements that contain at least one of the selected properties with the specified value.

69053065

**Related pages**

- Specifying criteria for querying model
  - Getting started with specifying criteria
  - Using Simple Navigation
  - Using Metachain Navigation
  - Using Find operation
  - Using Implied Relations
  - Using Property Test operation
  - Creating new operations
