## PAGE 02060: (2026x Refresh1) Velocity Directives

- page_id: `303998266`
- space_key: `MT`
- source_url: https://docs.nomagic.com/spaces/MT/pages/303998266/2026x+Refresh1+Velocity+Directives
- version_number: 1
- version_date: `2026-04-28T10:29:14.371+02:00`
- ancestors: Versions of Modeling Tools Documentation > (2026x Refresh1) Modeling Tools Documentation > (2026x Refresh1) General modeling features > (2026x Refresh1) Report Wizard > (2026x Refresh1) Template Creation Tutorial > (2026x Refresh1) Velocity templating language
- labels: ['msosa', 'mcse', 'msa']

### NORMALIZED CONTENT

1357045784

1357045785

1357045785

A Velocity directive is a keyword used by Velocity to perform certain tasks such as looping and controlling the output of the document based on values from the modeling tool.

With directives you can make dynamic templates and you can do more than just print out elements and attributes. A Velocity directive begins with **#** and followed by the directive name. Some of the frequently used Velocity directives are as follows:

1. #if , #elseif , and #else : these directives are used to decide whether or not text should be included in the output based on the conditional statement.
2. #set : this directive is used to assign a value to a variable.
3. #foreach : this directive is used to iterate through a list of variables.
4. #macro : this directive is used as a means to create a reusable script. It is especially useful if you need to call a certain line of scripts repeatedly.

1357045783

**Related pages**

A Velocity directive begins with <strong>#</strong> and followed by the directive name. Some of the frequently used Velocity directives are as follows:</p><ol><li><strong>#if</strong>, <strong>#elseif</strong>, and <strong>#else</strong>: these directives are used to decide whether or not text should be included in the output based on the conditional statement.</li><li><strong>#set</strong>: this directive is used to assign a value to a variable.</li><li><strong>#foreach</strong>: this directive is used to iterate through a list of variables.</li><li><strong>#macro</strong>: this directive is used as a means to create a reusable script. It is especially useful if you need to call a certain line of scripts repeatedly.</li></ol></ac:rich-text-body></ac:structured-macro><ac:structured-macro ac:name="content-block" ac:schema-version="1" ac:macro-id="620b5f44-617c-4173-9b95-77f1e5f8d1f5"><ac:parameter ac:name="id">1357045783</ac:parameter><ac:rich-text-body><p><strong>Related pages</strong></p><p><ac:structured-macro ac:name="children" ac:schema-version="2" ac:macro-id="af5875c2-eca2-406a-a317-e6cc4ae713ba" /></p></ac:rich-text-body></ac:structured-macro></ac:rich-text-body></ac:structured-macro></ac:rich-text-body></ac:structured-macro>

<!--NOMAGIC_PAGE id=303997913 space=MT version=1 -->
## PAGE 02061: (2026x Refresh1) Velocity template language

- page_id: `303997913`
- space_key: `MT`
- source_url: https://docs.nomagic.com/spaces/MT/pages/303997913/2026x+Refresh1+Velocity+template+language
- version_number: 1
- version_date: `2026-04-28T10:28:44.294+02:00`
- ancestors: Versions of Modeling Tools Documentation > (2026x Refresh1) Modeling Tools Documentation > (2026x Refresh1) General modeling features > (2026x Refresh1) Report Wizard > (2026x Refresh1) Report Wizard template language
- labels: ['msosa', 'mcse', 'msa']

### NORMALIZED CONTENT

The user guide for Velocity (VTL) can be found at: *[http://velocity.apache.org/engine/devel/user-guide.html](http://velocity.apache.org/engine/devel/user-guide.html).*

#### WARNING: Important

Important

- The formal reference such as ${hello} is not supported in RTF templates.
- A macro cannot be used as macro parameter in any RTF report template. This is a limitation of VTL itself: Velocity treats a macro like copy-and-paste content defined in the macro (between #macro and #end) at the inserted position.
- In an RTF report template, the style and formatting defined on a directive will have no effect on the report output. In addition, the paragraph symbol at the end of a line will also be removed.

### EXACT CONFLUENCE STORAGE SOURCE

````html
<p>The user guide for Velocity (VTL) can be found at: <em><a href="http://velocity.apache.org/engine/devel/user-guide.html">http://velocity.apache.org/engine/devel/user-guide.html</a>.</em></p><ac:structured-macro ac:name="warning" ac:schema-version="1" ac:macro-id="1d6567af-e1af-47d3-8891-94e0c001b235"><ac:parameter ac:name="title">Important</ac:parameter><ac:rich-text-body><ul><li>The formal reference such as ${hello} is not supported in RTF templates.</li><li>A macro cannot be used as macro parameter in any RTF report template. This is a limitation of VTL itself: Velocity treats a macro like copy-and-paste content defined in the macro (between #macro and #end) at the inserted position.</li><li>In an RTF report template, the style and formatting defined on a directive will have no effect on the report output. In addition, the paragraph symbol at the end of a line will also be removed.</li></ul></ac:rich-text-body></ac:structured-macro><p class="auto-cursor-target"><br /></p>
````

<!--NOMAGIC_PAGE id=303990548 space=MT version=1 -->
## PAGE 02062: (2026x Refresh1) Velocity templating language

- page_id: `303990548`
- space_key: `MT`
- source_url: https://docs.nomagic.com/spaces/MT/pages/303990548/2026x+Refresh1+Velocity+templating+language
- version_number: 1
- version_date: `2026-04-28T10:19:44.804+02:00`
- ancestors: Versions of Modeling Tools Documentation > (2026x Refresh1) Modeling Tools Documentation > (2026x Refresh1) General modeling features > (2026x Refresh1) Report Wizard > (2026x Refresh1) Template Creation Tutorial
- labels: ['msosa', 'mcse', 'msa']

### NORMALIZED CONTENT

1356919600

1356919600

1356919600

Velocity Template Language is a scripting language used only by Velocity Engine to determine what the output should look like. This section is divided into three parts:

- Velocity Variable
- Velocity Directive
- Comment and unparsing code

For more information on the Velocity Template Language, visit [https://velocity.apache.org/engine/2.2/user-guide.html](https://velocity.apache.org/engine/2.2/user-guide.html)[.](http://velocity.apache.org/engine/releases/velocity-1.6.2/user-guide.html+)

1356919599

**Related pages**

This section is divided into three parts:</p><ul><li>Velocity Variable</li><li>Velocity Directive</li><li>Comment and unparsing code</li></ul><p>For more information on the Velocity Template Language, visit <a href="https://velocity.apache.org/engine/2.2/user-guide.html" title="Follow link">https://velocity.apache.org/engine/2.2/user-guide.html</a><a href="http://velocity.apache.org/engine/releases/velocity-1.6.2/user-guide.html+" class="external-link" rel="nofollow">.</a></p></ac:rich-text-body></ac:structured-macro><ac:structured-macro ac:name="content-block" ac:schema-version="1" ac:macro-id="4a0124e2-2885-40fa-8b8b-0c9c5a8076e7"><ac:parameter ac:name="id">1356919599</ac:parameter><ac:rich-text-body><p><strong>Related pages</strong></p><p><ac:structured-macro ac:name="children" ac:schema-version="2" ac:macro-id="7b81369c-1839-41e1-8c96-81801c3e1a24" /></p></ac:rich-text-body></ac:structured-macro></ac:rich-text-body></ac:structured-macro></ac:rich-text-body></ac:structured-macro>

<!--NOMAGIC_PAGE id=303998268 space=MT version=1 -->
## PAGE 02063: (2026x Refresh1) Velocity variable

- page_id: `303998268`
- space_key: `MT`
- source_url: https://docs.nomagic.com/spaces/MT/pages/303998268/2026x+Refresh1+Velocity+variable
- version_number: 1
- version_date: `2026-04-28T10:29:15.177+02:00`
- ancestors: Versions of Modeling Tools Documentation > (2026x Refresh1) Modeling Tools Documentation > (2026x Refresh1) General modeling features > (2026x Refresh1) Report Wizard > (2026x Refresh1) Template Creation Tutorial > (2026x Refresh1) Velocity templating language
- labels: ['msosa', 'mcse', 'msa']

### NORMALIZED CONTENT

A Velocity variable can be either a referenced Java object or a declared variable inside a template. A Velocity variable begins with **$** followed by the name of the variable. Depending on what is added to the Velocity Context (the modeling tool automatically adds its element to the Velocity Context) the variable can either be a local variable or a reference to a Java object.

To declare a local variable inside a template, type: **$** followed by a string beginning with a letter. A referenced Java object variable is provided by Report Wizard as shown in the following table (see the [CONFLUENCE_PAGE title='(2026x Refresh1) Linking report templates to the modeling tool' space=''] section for a complete list of variables).

The table shows a partial list of modeling tool-specific variables.

| Variable Name | Return Value |
| --- | --- |
| $Class | Contains a list of class elements. |
| $UseCase | Contains a list of use case elements. |
| $Diagram | Contains a list of diagram elements. |

If a Velocity variable is a reference to a Java object, you can call its methods and value by:

- Directly calling the object method with the Velocity variable, for example, $object.method() .This is useful if you want to use a Java object's method to process data and print it out.
- Calling the object properties, for example, $object.name .

Velocity allows you to call the referenced Java object's method directly like you would in Java. This is very useful as you can create methods in Java that can help you create a more flexible Velocity template. For instance, if you need to perform a complex operation, such as sorting a list according to certain variables, you can create a sorting method in Java, and then call that method from a Java object.

Report Wizard provides a collection of complex statements in standard tool methods. You can find more information on the standard tool in the [CONFLUENCE_PAGE title='(2026x Refresh1) Helper tools' space=''] section, Report Wizard User Guide. You can also create your own custom tool, deploy, and use it inside a template. For more information on the custom tool, see [CONFLUENCE_PAGE title='(2026x Refresh1) Appendix A: report extensions' space=''], Report Wizard User Guide.

#### NOTE: Note

Note

- Report Wizard does not support calling variables in double quotes. 
 
The following calls are not valid: text

- If you want to concatenate string, use the following style: text

### EXACT CONFLUENCE STORAGE SOURCE

````html
<p>A Velocity variable can be either a referenced Java object or a declared variable inside a template. A Velocity variable begins with <strong>$</strong> followed by the name of the variable. Depending on what is added to the Velocity Context (the modeling tool automatically adds its element to the Velocity Context) the variable can either be a local variable or a reference to a Java object.</p><p>To declare a local variable inside a template, type: <strong>$</strong> followed by a string beginning with a letter. A referenced Java object variable is provided by Report Wizard as shown in the following table  (see the <span style="color: rgb(0,0,255);"><ac:link><ri:page ri:content-title="(2026x Refresh1) Linking report templates to the modeling tool" /><ac:plain-text-link-body><![CDATA[Linking Report Wizard template to modeling tool]]></ac:plain-text-link-body></ac:link></span> section for a complete list of variables).</p><p>The table shows a partial list of modeling tool-specific variables.</p><table><colgroup><col /><col /></colgroup><tbody><tr><th><p>Variable Name</p></th><th><p>Return Value</p></th></tr><tr><td><p>$Class</p></td><td><p>Contains a list of class elements.</p></td></tr><tr><td><p>$UseCase</p></td><td><p>Contains a list of use case elements.</p></td></tr><tr><td><p>$Diagram</p></td><td><p>Contains a list of diagram elements.</p></td></tr></tbody></table><p><br /></p><p>If a Velocity variable is a reference to a Java object, you can call its methods and value by:</p><ul><li>Directly calling the object method with the Velocity variable, for example, <strong>$object.method()</strong>.This is useful if you want to use a Java object's method to process data and print it out.</li><li>Calling the object properties, for example, <strong>$object.name</strong>.</li></ul><p>Velocity allows you to call the referenced Java object's method directly like you would in Java. This is very useful as you can create methods in Java that can help you create a more flexible Velocity template. For instance, if you need to perform a complex operation, such as sorting a list according to certain variables, you can create a sorting method in Java, and then call that method from a Java object.</p><p>Report Wizard provides a collection of complex statements in standard tool methods. You can find more information on the standard tool in the <ac:link><ri:page ri:content-title="(2026x Refresh1) Helper tools" /><ac:plain-text-link-body><![CDATA[Helper Modules]]></ac:plain-text-link-body></ac:link> section, Report Wizard User Guide. You can also create your own custom tool, deploy, and use it inside a template. For more information on the custom tool, see <ac:link><ri:page ri:content-title="(2026x Refresh1) Appendix A: report extensions" /><ac:plain-text-link-body><![CDATA[Appendix A: Report Extensions]]></ac:plain-text-link-body></ac:link>, Report Wizard User Guide.</p><ac:structured-macro ac:name="note" ac:schema-version="1" ac:macro-id="24b06755-6994-436e-a7d4-cb26619babdc"><ac:parameter ac:name="title">Note</ac:parameter><ac:rich-text-body><ul><li><p class="auto-cursor-target">Report Wizard does not support calling variables in double quotes.<br /><br />The following calls are not valid:</p><ac:structured-macro ac:name="code" ac:schema-version="1" ac:macro-id="8e895bb7-09d0-4e3a-bc4e-6e04516bdb4c"><ac:parameter ac:name="language">text</ac:parameter><ac:plain-text-body><![CDATA[#set($newVTL = "$vtl")
#set($newVTL = "text $vtl")
#aMacro("text $vtl")
#aMacro("$vtl")
#if("$text.name" == "class")]]></ac:plain-text-body></ac:structured-macro><p class="auto-cursor-target"><br /></p></li></ul><ul><li><p class="auto-cursor-target">If you want to concatenate string, use the following style:</p><ac:structured-macro ac:name="code" ac:schema-version="1" ac:macro-id="9fbba1a9-0709-4b4f-b22f-0be37a3a0891"><ac:parameter ac:name="language">text</ac:parameter><ac:plain-text-body><![CDATA[#set($newVTL = "text " + $vtl)]]></ac:plain-text-body></ac:structured-macro><p class="auto-cursor-target"><br /></p></li></ul></ac:rich-text-body></ac:structured-macro><p class="auto-cursor-target"><br /></p>
````

<!--NOMAGIC_PAGE id=303999247 space=MT version=1 -->
## PAGE 02064: (2026x Refresh1) View mapping

- page_id: `303999247`
- space_key: `MT`
- source_url: https://docs.nomagic.com/spaces/MT/pages/303999247/2026x+Refresh1+View+mapping
- version_number: 1
- version_date: `2026-04-28T10:30:42.324+02:00`
- ancestors: Versions of Modeling Tools Documentation > (2026x Refresh1) Modeling Tools Documentation > (2026x Refresh1) UAF 1.3 modeling > (2026x Refresh1) Migration User Guides > (2026x Refresh1) UPDM projects migration manual > (2026x Refresh1) UPDM 2.1 migration to UAF 1.1 > (2026x Refresh1) Model migration
- labels: ['msosa']

### NORMALIZED CONTENT

The following table describes view mapping between UPDM 2.1 and UAF 1.1.

| UPDM 2.1 | UAF 1.1 | Comments |
| --- | --- | --- |
| Svcv-6 | SV-6 | The SvcV-6 Services Resource Flow Matrix is no longer used. An existing diagram will be loaded as the SV-6 Systems Resource Flow Matrix. Instead of SvcV-6 diagram, the SV-6 diagram is introduced and recommended to use. |
| Svcv-6 Role-based | SV-6 Role-based | The SvcV-6 Role-based Services Resource Flow Matrix is no longer used. An existing diagram will be loaded as the SV-6 Role-based Systems Resource Flow Matrix. Instead of SvcV-6 Role-based diagram, the SV-6 Role-based diagram is introduced and recommended to use. |
| Service Channels Summary Table | Deleted. | The Service Channels Summary Table is no longer used for service channels analysis, creation, and modification. The Service Channel element is replaced to Resource Connector or Operational Connector. |
| Service Channels Summary Matrix | Deleted. | The Service Channels Summary Matrix is no longer used for service channels analysis and creation. The Service Channel element is replaced to Resource Connector or Operational Connector. |

### EXACT CONFLUENCE STORAGE SOURCE

An existing diagram will be loaded as the SV-6 Systems Resource Flow Matrix. Instead of SvcV-6 diagram, the SV-6 diagram is introduced and recommended to use.</td></tr><tr><td>Svcv-6 Role-based</td><td>SV-6 Role-based</td><td>The SvcV-6 Role-based Services Resource Flow Matrix is no longer used. An existing diagram will be loaded as the SV-6 Role-based Systems Resource Flow Matrix. Instead of SvcV-6 Role-based diagram, the SV-6 Role-based diagram is introduced and recommended to use.</td></tr><tr><td>Service Channels Summary Table</td><td>Deleted.</td><td>The Service Channels Summary Table is no longer used for service channels analysis, creation, and modification. The Service Channel element is replaced to Resource Connector or Operational Connector.</td></tr><tr><td>Service Channels Summary Matrix</td><td>Deleted.</td><td>The Service Channels Summary Matrix is no longer used for service channels analysis and creation. The Service Channel element is replaced to Resource Connector or Operational Connector.</td></tr></tbody></table><p class="auto-cursor-target"><br /></p>

<!--NOMAGIC_PAGE id=303996840 space=MT version=1 -->
## PAGE 02065: (2026x Refresh1) View Mappings

- page_id: `303996840`
- space_key: `MT`
- source_url: https://docs.nomagic.com/spaces/MT/pages/303996840/2026x+Refresh1+View+Mappings
- version_number: 1
- version_date: `2026-04-28T10:27:28.401+02:00`
- ancestors: Versions of Modeling Tools Documentation > (2026x Refresh1) Modeling Tools Documentation > (2026x Refresh1) UAF 1.3 modeling > (2026x Refresh1) Migration User Guides > (2026x Refresh1) UPDM projects migration manual > (2026x Refresh1) UPDM 1.x Migration to UPDM 2.0
- labels: ['msosa']

### NORMALIZED CONTENT

The following table describes view mapping between UPDM 1.x and UPDM 2.0.

| UPDM 1.x | UPDM 2.0 | Comments |
| --- | --- | --- |
| CV-3 | CV-2 | CV-3 Capability Phasing diagram is no longer used. An existing diagram will be loaded as the CV-2 Capability Taxonomy diagram. Instead of the diagram, the CV-3 Capability Phasing chart is introduced and recommended to use. |
| CV-5 | CV-2 | CV-5 Capability to Organizational Development Mapping diagram is no longer used. An existing diagram will be loaded as the CV-2 Capability Taxonomy diagram. The CV-5 tabular form of the representation is introduced and recommended to use. |
| StV-3 | StV-2 | StV-3 Capability Phasing diagram is no longer used. An existing diagram will be loaded as the StV-2 Capability Taxonomy diagram. Instead of the diagram, the StV-3 Capability Taxonomy chart is introduced and recommended to use. |
| StV-5 | StV-2 | StV-5 Organization Deployment Mapping diagram is no longer used. An existing diagram will be loaded as the StV-2 Capability Taxonomy diagram. The StV-5 tabular form of representation is introduced and recommended to use. |
| PV-2 | PV-1 | PV-2 Project Timelines diagram is no longer used. An existing diagram will be loaded as the PV-1 Project Portfolio Relationships diagram. Instead of the diagram, the PV-2 Project Timelines chart is introduced and recommended to use. |
| AcV-2 | AcV-1 | AcV-2 Programme Timelines diagram is no longer used. An existing diagram will be loaded as the AcV-1 Acquisition Clusters diagram. Instead of the diagram, the AcV-2 Acquisition Clusters chart is introduced and recommended to use. |
| SV-8 | SV-1 | SV-8 Capability Configuration Management diagram is refactored to meet UPDM 2.0 requirements. An existing diagram will be loaded as the SV-1 Resource Interaction Specification diagram. New form of the SV-8 diagram is introduced and recommended to use. |
| SvcV-1 | SOV-1 |  |
| SvcV-2 | SOV-2 |  |
| SvcV-3a | Service Channels Summary Matrix | Service Channels Summary Matrix is a new type of supportive tool, dedicated for service channels analysis and creation. |
| SvcV-3b | SV-12 |  |
| SvcV-4a | SOV-5 structural |  |
| SvcV-4b | SOV-5 behavioral |  |
| SvcV-5 | Dependency Matrix |  |
| SvcV-6 | Service Channels Summary Table | Service Channels Summary Table is a new type of supportive tool, dedicated for service channels analysis, creation, and modification. |
| SvcV-7 | Generic Table |  |
| SvcV-10a | SOV-4a |  |
| SvcV-10b | SOV-4b |  |
| SvcV-10c | SOV-4c |  |

### EXACT CONFLUENCE STORAGE SOURCE

Instead of the diagram, the AcV-2 Acquisition Clusters chart is introduced and recommended to use.</p></td></tr><tr><td colspan="1">SV-8 </td><td colspan="1">SV-1 </td><td colspan="1"><p>SV-8 Capability Configuration Management diagram is refactored to meet UPDM 2.0 requirements. An existing diagram will be loaded as the SV-1 Resource Interaction Specification diagram. New form of the SV-8 diagram is introduced and recommended to use.</p></td></tr><tr><td colspan="1">SvcV-1 </td><td colspan="1"><p>SOV-1</p></td><td colspan="1"><br /></td></tr><tr><td colspan="1">SvcV-2 </td><td colspan="1"><p>SOV-2</p></td><td colspan="1"><br /></td></tr><tr><td colspan="1">SvcV-3a </td><td colspan="1"><p>Service Channels Summary Matrix</p></td><td colspan="1"><p>Service Channels Summary Matrix is a new type of supportive tool, dedicated for service channels analysis and creation.</p></td></tr><tr><td colspan="1">SvcV-3b </td><td colspan="1">SV-12</td><td colspan="1"><br /></td></tr><tr><td colspan="1">SvcV-4a </td><td colspan="1"><p>SOV-5 structural</p></td><td colspan="1"><br /></td></tr><tr><td colspan="1">SvcV-4b </td><td colspan="1">SOV-5 behavioral</td><td colspan="1"><br /></td></tr><tr><td colspan="1">SvcV-5 </td><td colspan="1">Dependency Matrix</td><td colspan="1"><br /></td></tr><tr><td colspan="1">SvcV-6</td><td colspan="1"><p>Service Channels Summary Table</p></td><td colspan="1"><p>Service Channels Summary Table is a new type of supportive tool, dedicated for service channels analysis, creation, and modification.</p></td></tr><tr><td colspan="1">SvcV-7 </td><td colspan="1"><p>Generic Table</p></td><td colspan="1"><br /></td></tr><tr><td colspan="1"><p>SvcV-10a</p></td><td colspan="1"><p>SOV-4a</p></td><td colspan="1"><br /></td></tr><tr><td colspan="1"><span>SvcV-10b </span></td><td colspan="1"><span>SOV-4b</span></td><td colspan="1"><br /></td></tr><tr><td colspan="1"><span>SvcV-10c</span></td><td colspan="1"><span> SOV-4c</span></td><td colspan="1"><br /></td></tr></tbody></table><p class="auto-cursor-target"><br /></p></ac:layout-cell><ac:layout-cell><p><br /></p></ac:layout-cell></ac:layout-section></ac:layout>

<!--NOMAGIC_PAGE id=303996723 space=MT version=1 -->
## PAGE 02066: (2026x Refresh1) Viewing and analyzing changes of modified diagrams

- page_id: `303996723`
- space_key: `MT`
- source_url: https://docs.nomagic.com/spaces/MT/pages/303996723/2026x+Refresh1+Viewing+and+analyzing+changes+of+modified+diagrams
- version_number: 1
- version_date: `2026-04-28T10:27:16.721+02:00`
- ancestors: Versions of Modeling Tools Documentation > (2026x Refresh1) Modeling Tools Documentation > (2026x Refresh1) General modeling features > (2026x Refresh1) Tools > (2026x Refresh1) Model Merge > (2026x Refresh1) Analyzing and managing merge results
- labels: ['merged-result', 'analyzing-changes', 'difference-viewer', 'msosa', 'mcse', 'msa']

### NORMALIZED CONTENT

199264098

INLINE

199264100

INLINE

199264099

INLINE

The different diagram versions are displayed in separate **Difference Viewer** tabsin the **Merge** window next to the **Merged Result** tree (see the following figure). 
 
To open diagram difference viewers of a modified diagram

- Double-click the modified diagram in the Merged Result tree.

One, two or three new tabsopen in the **Merge** window. In 3-way merge, the ancestor's tab is opened by default, the source's tab is opened, if changes have been detected in the source, and the target's tab is opened, if changes have been detected in the target. In 2-way merge, the target tab is opened by default. If a diagram is only available in source, only the source tab opens in the difference viewer .

All the diagram difference viewers are used to display the following changes:

- One or more symbols were moved or modified on the diagram.
- A new symbol was added to the diagram.
- A symbol was deleted from the diagram.
- The number of inner model elements has changed .
- A model element has been modified.

Be advised that symbols with property value changes that actually have no effect on their appearance are highlighted in a diagram difference viewer as well. For example, changing the **Show Stereotypes** property value in the **Symbol Properties** dialog of a class makes the class symbol highlighted as changed in a diagram difference viewer.

As you can see in the following figure, all these changes are marked by color coded lines and flags allowing the quick distinguishing between different types of changes. Numbers on flags indicate the number of changes made to that element. Clicking a flag switches to the **Merged Result** tab and selects the flagged element in the **Merged Result** tree.

The colors of lines and flags correspond to the color system explained in the difference legend:

- Blue flags indicate the number of modification/movement changes made to the element, blue lines represent modified/moved relations, and blue borders around symbols mark modified/moved elements in the diagram.
- Green flags indicate the number of addition changes made to the element, green lines represent added relations, and green borders around symbols mark added elements in the diagram.
- Red flags indicate the number of deletion changes made to the element, red lines represent deleted relations, and red borders around symbols mark deleted elements in the diagram.

[IMAGE alt='' src='']

You can customize the types of changes you want to be displayed in the difference viewer by using filtering options available in the legend. Simply select or clear selection from the appropriate check boxes and view the differences that are relevant to you. 
 [IMAGE alt='' src='']

If you would like to see the diagram version from other project version without switching between tabs, use the slider available at the bottom of the **Difference Viewer**. Moving the slider makes the current diagram symbols to fade, and the symbols from another diagram version start appearing. When the slider is set to the middle position, different symbols from both diagram.

[IMAGE alt='' src='']

You can manage the content of a diagram difference viewer using the toolbar that is located above it. 
 [IMAGE alt='' src='']

See the button descriptions in the following table.

| Button | Tooltip text | Description |
| --- | --- | --- |
|  | Return to Merged Result | Click to open the Merged Result tree with the appropriate diagram selected within. |
|  | Synchronize Zooming | Click to turn on or off the zooming synchronization in all difference viewers of the same diagram. |
|  | Synchronize Scrolling | Click to turn on or off scrolling synchronization in all difference viewers of the same diagram. |
|  | Print Diagram | Click the button to print a diagram. The diagram will be printed with highlighted areas on it, if the highlighting of the diagram changes is turned on. |
|  | Zooming | Use the buttons to control the diagram size. |

199264096

INLINE

<!--NOMAGIC_PAGE id=303992094 space=MT version=1 -->
## PAGE 02067: (2026x Refresh1) Viewing and analyzing differences of modified diagrams

- page_id: `303992094`
- space_key: `MT`
- source_url: https://docs.nomagic.com/spaces/MT/pages/303992094/2026x+Refresh1+Viewing+and+analyzing+differences+of+modified+diagrams
- version_number: 1
- version_date: `2026-04-28T10:21:31.337+02:00`
- ancestors: Versions of Modeling Tools Documentation > (2026x Refresh1) Modeling Tools Documentation > (2026x Refresh1) General modeling features > (2026x Refresh1) Model analysis > (2026x Refresh1) Comparing projects > (2026x Refresh1) Analyzing comparison results
- labels: ['analyzing-changes', 'difference-viewer', 'comparison-result', 'msosa', 'mcse', 'msa']

### NORMALIZED CONTENT

The different diagram versions are displayed in separate **Difference Viewer** tabs in the **Difference Viewer** window next to the **Differences** tree (see the following figure).

To open diagram difference viewers of a modified diagram

- Double-click the modified diagram in the Differences tree.

One, two or three new tabs open in the **Difference Viewer** window. The base tab is opened by default. If a diagram is only available in compared, only this tab is opened in the difference viewer.

All the diagram difference viewers are used to display the following changes:

- One or more symbols were moved or modified on the diagram.
- A new symbol was added to the diagram.
- A symbol was deleted from the diagram.
- The number of inner model elements has changed.
- A model element has been modified.

Be advised that symbols with property value changes that actually have no effect on their appearance are highlighted in a diagram difference viewer as well. For example, changing the **Show Stereotypes** property value in the **Symbol Properties** dialog of a class makes the class symbol highlighted as changed in a diagram difference viewer.

Asyou can see in the following figure, all these changes are marked by color coded lines and flags allowing the quick distinguishing between different types of changes. Numbers on flags indicate the number of changes made to that element. Clicking a flag switches to the **Differences**tab and selects the flagged element in the **Differences** tree.

The colors of lines and flags correspond to the color system explained in the difference legend:

- Blue flags indicate the number of modification/movement changes made to the element, blue lines represent modified/moved relations, and blue borders around symbols mark modified/moved elements in the diagram.
- Green flags indicate the number of addition changes made to the element, green lines represent added relations, and green borders around symbols mark added elements in the diagram.
- Red flags indicate the number of deletion changes made to the element, red lines represent deleted relations, and red borders around symbols mark deleted elements in the diagram.

[IMAGE alt='' src='']

You can customize the types of changes you want to be displayed in the difference viewer by using filtering options available in the legend. Simply select or clear selection from the appropriate check-boxes and view the differences that are relevant to you. 
 [IMAGE alt='' src='']

If you would like to see the diagram version from other project version without switching between tabs, use the slider available at the bottom of the **Difference Viewer**. Moving the slider makes the current diagram symbols to fade, and the symbols from another diagram version start appearing. When the slider is set to the middle position, different symbols from both diagram.

[IMAGE alt='' src='']

You can manage the content of a diagram difference viewer using the toolbar that is located above it. 
 [IMAGE alt='' src='']

See the button descriptions in the following table.

| Button | Tooltip text | Description |
| --- | --- | --- |
|  | Return to Merged Result | Click to open the Differences tree with the appropriate diagram selected within. |
|  | Synchronize Zooming | Click to turn on or off the zooming synchronization in all difference viewers of the same diagram. |
|  | Synchronize Scrolling | Click to turn on or off scrolling synchronization in all difference viewers of the same diagram. |
|  | Print Diagram | Click the button to print a diagram. The diagram will be printed with highlighted areas on it, if the highlighting of the diagram changes is turned on. |
|  | Zooming | Use the buttons to control the diagram size. |

### EXACT CONFLUENCE STORAGE SOURCE

````html
<p>The different diagram versions are displayed in separate <strong>Difference Viewer</strong> tabs in the <strong>Difference Viewer</strong> window next to the <strong>Differences</strong> tree (s<span style="color: rgb(51,51,51);">ee the following figure)</span>.</p><p> </p><p>To open diagram difference viewers of a modified diagram</p><hr /><ul><li>Double-click the modified diagram in the <strong>Differences</strong> tree.</li></ul><p>One, two or three new tabs open in the <strong>Difference Viewer</strong> window. The base tab is opened by default. If a diagram is only available in compared, only this tab is opened in the difference viewer.</p><p>All the diagram difference viewers are used to display the following changes:</p><ul><li>One or more symbols were moved or modified on the diagram.</li><li>A new symbol was added to the diagram.</li><li>A symbol was deleted from the diagram.</li><li>The number of inner model elements has changed.</li><li>A model element has been modified.</li></ul><ac:structured-macro ac:name="warning" ac:schema-version="1" ac:macro-id="377a4e1e-54e0-46ab-b728-1a1b016810a2"><ac:rich-text-body><p>Be advised that symbols with property value changes that actually have no effect on their appearance are highlighted in a diagram difference viewer as well. For example, changing the <strong>Show Stereotypes</strong> property value in the <strong>Symbol Properties</strong> dialog of a class makes the class symbol highlighted as changed in a diagram difference viewer.</p></ac:rich-text-body></ac:structured-macro><p><span style="color: rgb(51,51,51);">As </span>you can see in the following figure, all these changes are marked by color coded lines and flags allowing the quick distinguishing between different types of changes. Numbers on flags indicate the number of changes made to that element. Clicking a flag switches to the <strong>Differences </strong>tab and selects the flagged element in the <strong>Differences</strong> tree.</p><p><ac:inline-comment-marker ac:ref="244e86a6-66d8-4636-86b6-9c6d8998af98">The colors of lines and flags correspond to the color system explained in the difference legend</ac:inline-comment-marker>:</p><ul><li>Blue flags indicate the number of modification/movement changes made to the element, blue lines represent modified/moved relations, and blue borders around symbols mark modified/moved elements in the diagram.</li><li>Green flags indicate the number of addition changes made to the element, green lines represent added relations, and green borders around symbols mark added elements in the diagram.</li><li>Red flags indicate the number of deletion changes made to the element, red lines represent deleted relations, and red borders around symbols mark deleted elements in the diagram.</li></ul><p><ac:image ac:title="Project Compare difference viewer" ac:alt="Project Compare difference viewer"><ri:attachment ri:filename="comparer_difference_viewer.png"><ri:page ri:content-title="(2026x Refresh1) Viewing and analyzing differences of modified diagrams" /></ri:attachment></ac:image></p><p> </p><p>You can customize the types of changes you want to be displayed in the difference viewer by using filtering options available in the legend. Simply select or clear selection from the appropriate check-boxes and view the differences that are relevant to you.<br /> <ac:image ac:border="true" ac:title="Difference viewer legend and change slider" ac:alt="Difference viewer legend and change slider" ac:height="42" ac:width="595"><ri:attachment ri:filename="difference_viewer_legend.png"><ri:page ri:content-title="(2026x Refresh1) Viewing and analyzing differences of modified diagrams" /></ri:attachment></ac:image></p><p> </p><p>If you would like to see the diagram version from other project version without switching between tabs, use the slider available at the bottom of the <strong>Difference Viewer</strong>. Moving the slider makes the current diagram symbols to fade, and the symbols from another diagram version start appearing. When the slider is set to the middle position, different symbols from both diagram.</p><p><ac:image ac:border="true" ac:title="Difference slider" ac:alt="Difference slider" ac:height="44" ac:width="306"><ri:attachment ri:filename="comparer_slider.png"><ri:page ri:content-title="(2026x Refresh1) Viewing and analyzing differences of modified diagrams" /></ri:attachment></ac:image></p><p> </p><p>You can manage the content of a diagram difference viewer using the toolbar that is located above it. <br class="atl-forced-newline" /> <ac:image ac:border="true" ac:title="Toolbar for managing content of diagram difference viewer" ac:alt="Toolbar for managing content of diagram difference viewer" ac:height="24" ac:width="566"><ri:attachment ri:filename="comparer_difference_viewer_toolbar.png"><ri:page ri:content-title="(2026x Refresh1) Viewing and analyzing differences of modified diagrams" /></ri:attachment></ac:image></p><p> </p><p>See the button descriptions in the following table.</p><table><tbody><tr><td><p><br class="atl-forced-newline" /> <strong>Button</strong></p></td><td><p><br class="atl-forced-newline" /> <strong>Tooltip text</strong></p></td><td><p><br class="atl-forced-newline" /> <strong>Description</strong></p></td></tr><tr><td colspan="1"><ac:image ac:height="22" ac:width="144"><ri:attachment ri:filename="return_merge_result_button.png"><ri:page ri:content-title="_buttons" /></ri:attachment></ac:image></td><td colspan="1"><p>Return to Merged Result</p></td><td colspan="1">Click to open the <strong>Differences</strong> tree with the appropriate diagram selected within.</td></tr><tr><td><p><ac:image><ri:attachment ri:filename="synchronize_zooming_button.png"><ri:page ri:content-title="_buttons" /></ri:attachment></ac:image></p></td><td><p>Synchronize Zooming</p></td><td><p>Click to turn on or off the zooming synchronization in all difference viewers of the same diagram.</p></td></tr><tr><td><p><ac:image ac:thumbnail="true" ac:width="23"><ri:attachment ri:filename="synchronize_scrolling_button.png"><ri:page ri:content-title="_buttons" /></ri:attachment></ac:image></p></td><td><p>Synchronize Scrolling</p></td><td><p>Click to turn on or off scrolling synchronization in all difference viewers of the same diagram.</p></td></tr><tr><td><ac:image ac:thumbnail="true" ac:width="23"><ri:attachment ri:filename="print_diagram_button.png"><ri:page ri:content-title="_buttons" /></ri:attachment></ac:image></td><td>Print Diagram</td><td>Click the button to print a diagram. The diagram will be printed with highlighted areas on it, if the highlighting of the diagram changes is turned on.</td></tr><tr><td><ac:image><ri:attachment ri:filename="zooming_options.png"><ri:page ri:content-title="(2026x Refresh1) Viewing and analyzing differences of modified diagrams" /></ri:attachment></ac:image></td><td>Zooming</td><td>Use the buttons to control the diagram size.</td></tr></tbody></table><p class="auto-cursor-target"><br /></p><p> </p>
````

<!--NOMAGIC_PAGE id=303998619 space=MT version=1 -->
## PAGE 02070: (2026x Refresh1) Virtual entities

- page_id: `303998619`
- space_key: `MT`
- source_url: https://docs.nomagic.com/spaces/MT/pages/303998619/2026x+Refresh1+Virtual+entities
- version_number: 1
- version_date: `2026-04-28T10:29:48.468+02:00`
- ancestors: Versions of Modeling Tools Documentation > (2026x Refresh1) Modeling Tools Documentation > (2026x Refresh1) Data-related modeling > (2026x Refresh1) Entity-relationship (ER) modeling and diagrams
- labels: ['msosa']

### NORMALIZED CONTENT

Virtual entities can be derived from information in other entities. They are marked with the keyword «virtual» on diagrams. They can be handled in the same manner as other entities.

Virtual entities roughly correspond to views in databases.

If you need to specify exactly how virtual entities are derived from other entities, you can use Abstraction relationships from UML. You can specify the derivation expression in the Mapping field.

[IMAGE alt='' src='']

###### Example of virtual entity usage in an ER diagram.

### EXACT CONFLUENCE STORAGE SOURCE

````html
<p>Virtual entities can be derived from information in other entities. They are marked with the keyword «virtual» on diagrams. They can be handled in the same manner as other entities.</p><p>Virtual entities roughly correspond to views in databases.</p><p>If you need to specify exactly how virtual entities are derived from other entities, you can use Abstraction relationships from UML. You can specify the derivation expression in the Mapping field.</p><p><ac:image ac:align="center"><ri:attachment ri:filename="Figure 13 Virtual Entity Usage in ER Diagram.png"><ri:page ri:content-title="(2026x Refresh1) Virtual entities" /></ri:attachment></ac:image></p><h6 style="text-align: center;">Example of virtual entity usage in an ER diagram.</h6>
````

## PAGE 02071: (2026x Refresh1) Virtual entity transformation

- page_id: `303999443`
- space_key: `MT`
- source_url: https://docs.nomagic.com/spaces/MT/pages/303999443/2026x+Refresh1+Virtual+entity+transformation
- version_number: 1
- version_date: `2026-04-28T10:30:57.156+02:00`
- ancestors: Versions of Modeling Tools Documentation > (2026x Refresh1) Modeling Tools Documentation > (2026x Refresh1) Data-related modeling > (2026x Refresh1) Transformations > (2026x Refresh1) ER to SQL (generic / Oracle) transformations
- labels: ['msosa']

### NORMALIZED CONTENT

919216430

919216432

919216431

Virtual entities of ER models can be transformed into the following different elements of SQL models.

- Tables (just as ordinary, non-virtual entities).
- SQL views (ER to SQL(Oracle) transformation has an additional choice of simple views or materialized views).

The choice is controlled by the **Virtual Entities Transformed To**transformation property.

919216429

## PAGE 02072: (2026x Refresh1) Vision

- page_id: `303998957`
- space_key: `MT`
- source_url: https://docs.nomagic.com/spaces/MT/pages/303998957/2026x+Refresh1+Vision
- version_number: 2
- version_date: `2026-04-28T10:48:02.070+02:00`
- ancestors: Versions of Modeling Tools Documentation > (2026x Refresh1) Modeling Tools Documentation > (2026x Refresh1) BPMN modeling > (2026x Refresh1) Supportive Diagrams Concepts > (2026x Refresh1) Business Motivation Diagram > (2026x Refresh1) Ends Concepts
- labels: ['msosa']

### NORMALIZED CONTENT

1487106899

1487106899

1487106899

**Description**

A vision is a state where an organization wants to achieve in the future. It is common that a vision is made up of many aspects rather than concentrated on a specific factor of the business problem. It is the final, possibly unattainable, state the organization would like to accomplish. It does not describe how the organization will achieve the state. A Vision is often compound, rather than focused toward one particular aspect of the business problem. It is supported or made operative by Missions and amplified by Goals.

**Notation**

[IMAGE alt='' src='']

1487106898

**Related elements**

- Ends Concepts
- End Concept Relationships

**Related diagram**

- Business Motivation Diagram

<!--NOMAGIC_PAGE id=303997972 space=MT version=1 -->
## PAGE 02073: (2026x Refresh1) Web Publisher 2.0 report

- page_id: `303997972`
- space_key: `MT`
- source_url: https://docs.nomagic.com/spaces/MT/pages/303997972/2026x+Refresh1+Web+Publisher+2.0+report
- version_number: 1
- version_date: `2026-04-28T10:28:51.846+02:00`
- ancestors: Versions of Modeling Tools Documentation > (2026x Refresh1) Modeling Tools Documentation > (2026x Refresh1) General modeling features > (2026x Refresh1) Report Wizard > (2026x Refresh1) Generating Reports from Report Wizard
- labels: ['msosa', 'mcse', 'msa']

### NORMALIZED CONTENT

#### CONTENT-LAYER: Note

347568349

#### CONTENT-COLUMN: Note

347568351

#### CONTENT-BLOCK: Note

347568350

Web Publisher 2.0 is a Java-Doc-like report template with a clickable navigation and an image map for all diagrams and elements. It allowsyou to publish models (including diagrams) in HTML with rich features, and to open them using your browser, e.g., Microsoft Edge, Opera, or Firefox.

This section describes the features of Web Publisher 2.0, illustrates how to generate a Web Publisher 2.0 report template by using the Magic Library.mdzip sample project, and gives you examples on how to work with each feature.

[IMAGE alt='' src='']

###### A Web Publisher 2.0 report.

#### NOTE: Note

Note

Web Publisher 2.0 report is not fully supported by Google Chrome (unless following the steps below), Opera, and Microsoft Edge (Chromium-based); therefore, the data tree structure in the Containment tree may be missing because they do not allow JavaScript to load local files via **XMLHttpRequest**. You can solve this problem by doing one of the following:

- Deploy Web Publisher 2.0 report on web server, or
- Start Google Chrome with " --allow-file-access-from-files " argument by following the steps below:
  1. Close the web browser (Chrome) instances.
  2. Open the web browser with " --allow-file-access-from-files " argument. 2.1 Right click on web browser launcher and select Properties .

[IMAGE alt='' src='']

2.2 Add this argument "**--allow-file-access-from-files**" next to the web browser installation path.

###### [IMAGE alt='' src='']

2.3 Open the web browser instance from the launcher.

2.4 Open the generated Web 2.0 report.

2.5 Start the web browser from the launcher or command line that contains "**--allow-file-access-from-files**" argument before.

#### NOTE: Note

Note

Web Publisher 2.0 report is also supported by Firefox. You can configure Firefox before opening Web Publisher 2.0 report by doing the following:

1. In the Firefox address box, type about:config . [ATTACHMENT filename='config.png']
2. Find the security.fileuri.strict_origin_policy parameter and set its parameter value to false .

#### NOTE: Note

Note

You can also open Web Publisher 2.0 on Safari by doing the following:

1. Open Safari.
2. On the toolbar of the browser, click Safari and select Preferences . The Preference dialog opens.
3. Click the Advanced tab and select the Show Develop menu in menu bar option. Close the dialog. [ATTACHMENT filename='Safari Preference Advanced Dialog.png']
4. On the toolbar of the browser, click Develop and select Disable Local File Restrictions .

Safari is now able to open Web Publisher 2.0.

#### NOTE: Note

Note

In Report Wizard 2021x Refresh 2 and later, **$exporter2 (DOM : Exporter2)** is no longer used, and **$exporter3(XMLStream : ExporterTool3)** is used by default instead.

If you want to continue using Exporter2, use the following line in *index.html* of the Web Publisher 2.0 template:

```text

```

Cameo Collaborator is developed for replacing Web Publisher Collaboration report. This is a web-based solution for publishing and reviewing models online. See more detail at [Cameo Collaborator for Teamwork Cloud Documentation](https://www.3ds.com/products-services/catia/products/no-magic/cameo-collaborator-for-teamwork-cloud/)

347568348

**Related pages**

### EXACT CONFLUENCE STORAGE SOURCE

therefore, the data tree structure in the Containment tree may be missing because they do not allow JavaScript to load local files via <strong>XMLHttpRequest</strong>. You can solve this problem by doing one of the following:</p><ul><li>Deploy Web Publisher 2.0 report on web server, or</li><li>Start Google Chrome with &quot;<strong>--allow-file-access-from-files</strong>&quot; argument by following the steps below:<ol><li>Close the web browser (Chrome) instances.</li><li>Open the web browser with &quot;<strong>--allow-file-access-from-files</strong>&quot; argument.<br /><br />2.1 Right click on web browser launcher and select <strong>Properties</strong>.</li></ol></li></ul><p style="margin-left: 120.0px;"><br /><ac:image><ri:attachment ri:filename="web2.0PropertiesSetting.png"><ri:page ri:content-title="(2026x Refresh1) Web Publisher 2.0 report" /></ri:attachment></ac:image></p><p style="margin-left: 120.0px;"><br /></p><p style="margin-left: 120.0px;">2.2 Add this argument &quot;<strong>--allow-file-access-from-files</strong>&quot; next to the web browser installation path.</p><h6 style="margin-left: 120.0px;"><ac:image><ri:attachment ri:filename="GoogleSettings.png"><ri:page ri:content-title="(2026x Refresh1) Web Publisher 2.0 report" /></ri:attachment></ac:image></h6><p>2.3 Open the web browser instance from the launcher.</p><p>2.4 Open the generated Web 2.0 report.</p><p>2.5 Start the web browser from the launcher or command line that contains &quot;<strong>--allow-file-access-from-files</strong>&quot; argument before.</p></ac:rich-text-body></ac:structured-macro><ac:structured-macro ac:name="note" ac:schema-version="1" ac:macro-id="6d397470-695b-400e-a7b2-c25dd09c32ba"><ac:parameter ac:name="title">Note</ac:parameter><ac:rich-text-body><p>Web Publisher 2.0 report is also supported by Firefox.  You can configure Firefox before opening Web Publisher 2.0 report by doing the following:</p><ol><li>In the Firefox address box, type <strong>about:config</strong>.<br /><br /><ac:image><ri:attachment ri:filename="config.png"><ri:page ri:content-title="(2026x Refresh1) Web Publisher 2.0 report" /></ri:attachment></ac:image><br /><br /></li><li>Find the <strong>security.fileuri.strict_origin_policy</strong> parameter and set its parameter value to <strong>false</strong>.</li></ol></ac:rich-text-body></ac:structured-macro><ac:structured-macro ac:name="note" ac:schema-version="1" ac:macro-id="afebbfc6-1ea8-4b03-8601-7c75a7c65a56"><ac:parameter ac:name="title">Note</ac:parameter><ac:rich-text-body><p>You can also open Web Publisher 2.0 on Safari by doing the following:</p><ol><li>Open Safari.</li><li>On the toolbar of the browser, click <strong>Safari</strong> and select <strong>Preferences</strong>. The Preference dialog opens.</li><li>Click the <strong>Advanced</strong> tab and select the <strong>Show Develop menu in menu bar</strong> option. Close the dialog.<br /><br /><ac:image><ri:attachment ri:filename="Safari Preference Advanced Dialog.png"><ri:page ri:content-title="(2026x Refresh1) Web Publisher 2.0 report" /></ri:attachment></ac:image><br /><br /></li><li>On the toolbar of the browser, click <strong>Develop</strong> and select <strong>Disable Local File Restrictions</strong>.</li></ol><p>Safari is now able to open Web Publisher 2.0.</p></ac:rich-text-body></ac:structured-macro><ac:structured-macro ac:name="note" ac:schema-version="1" ac:macro-id="1f21522f-f183-4810-80ad-7459588add25"><ac:parameter ac:name="title">Note</ac:parameter><ac:rich-text-body><p>In Report Wizard 2021x Refresh 2 and later, <strong>$exporter2 (DOM : Exporter2)</strong> is no longer used, and <strong>$exporter3(XMLStream : ExporterTool3)</strong> is used by default instead.</p><p>If you want to continue using Exporter2, use the following line in <em>index.html</em> of the Web Publisher 2.0 template:</p><ac:structured-macro ac:name="code" ac:schema-version="1" ac:macro-id="bb83dadc-d0df-4ebf-b07e-ba106357622e"><ac:parameter ac:name="language">text</ac:parameter><ac:plain-text-body><![CDATA[XMLRequest.send('$exporter2.exportAll(true, $linkedFile)', renderBrowser);]]></ac:plain-text-body></ac:structured-macro></ac:rich-text-body></ac:structured-macro><p>Cameo Collaborator is developed for replacing Web Publisher Collaboration report. This is a web-based solution for publishing and reviewing models online. See more detail at <a href="https://www.3ds.com/products-services/catia/products/no-magic/cameo-collaborator-for-teamwork-cloud/">Cameo Collaborator for Teamwork Cloud Documentation</a></p></ac:rich-text-body></ac:structured-macro><ac:structured-macro ac:name="content-block" ac:schema-version="1" ac:macro-id="d8fa182a-8527-4cf7-9954-6dc11f237cea"><ac:parameter ac:name="id">347568348</ac:parameter><ac:rich-text-body><p><strong>Related pages</strong></p><p><ac:structured-macro ac:name="children" ac:schema-version="2" ac:macro-id="72500a1f-e65d-4d46-90c8-87b0347b80fd" /></p></ac:rich-text-body></ac:structured-macro></ac:rich-text-body></ac:structured-macro></ac:rich-text-body></ac:structured-macro>

<!--NOMAGIC_PAGE id=304000743 space=MT version=1 -->
## PAGE 02074: (2026x Refresh1) What is a Template?

- page_id: `304000743`
- space_key: `MT`
- source_url: https://docs.nomagic.com/spaces/MT/pages/304000743/2026x+Refresh1+What+is+a+Template
- version_number: 1
- version_date: `2026-04-28T10:32:37.800+02:00`
- ancestors: Versions of Modeling Tools Documentation > (2026x Refresh1) Modeling Tools Documentation > (2026x Refresh1) General modeling features > (2026x Refresh1) Report Wizard > (2026x Refresh1) Template Creation Tutorial > (2026x Refresh1) Introduction
- labels: ['msosa', 'mcse', 'msa']

### NORMALIZED CONTENT

A template, in Velocity, is a text file that tells Velocity what the output should look like. It contains a document page style, layout, header, footer, and static text just like any other template that comes with most of the Word Processor programs. However, a Velocity template also contains specific placeholders for Java objects and Velocity Template Language scripts which will tell the Velocity Engine what and how to print the information in the output.

### EXACT CONFLUENCE STORAGE SOURCE

````html
<p>A template, in Velocity, is a text file that tells Velocity what the output should look like. It contains a document page style, layout, header, footer, and static text just like any other template that comes with most of the Word Processor programs. However, a Velocity template also contains specific placeholders for Java objects and Velocity Template Language scripts which will tell the Velocity Engine what and how to print the information in the output.</p>
````

<!--NOMAGIC_PAGE id=303992551 space=MT version=1 -->
## PAGE 02075: (2026x Refresh1) Whitebox ICD Table

- page_id: `303992551`
- space_key: `MT`
- source_url: https://docs.nomagic.com/spaces/MT/pages/303992551/2026x+Refresh1+Whitebox+ICD+Table
- version_number: 1
- version_date: `2026-04-28T10:22:02.418+02:00`
- ancestors: Versions of Modeling Tools Documentation > (2026x Refresh1) Modeling Tools Documentation > (2026x Refresh1) SysML v1 modeling > (2026x Refresh1) SysML v1 diagram descriptions
- labels: ['mcse', 'msosa']

### NORMALIZED CONTENT

The purpose of Whitebox ICD Table is to show how [CONFLUENCE_PAGE title='Part Property' space='MED'] are connected via ports/interfaces. This table collects all parts of the context element. Those parts are connected through the ports/interfaces using [CONFLUENCE_PAGE title='Connector' space='MED']. You can visually identify what kind of ports/interfaces (Port A and Port B) and flows (Item Flow) are used to connects two Part Properties (Part A and Part B).

In the row 1 of the following figure, the*Controller* Part Property sends the *control* Signal to the *Boiler* Part Property through the ports *b* and *c*. The *Controller* receives the *Status* signal from the *Boiler* through the Ports *c* and *b*.In the row 2, the Part *Controller* sends the flow *Elec Power* to the Part *Boiler* through the Ports *bp* and *p in*. [CONFLUENCE_PAGE title='(2026x Refresh1) Creating Interface Control Document tables' space='']>]]>

###### [IMAGE alt='' src='']An example of Whitebox ICD Table.

In the created table, a row represents the Connector and the columns represent features of that Connector. You can [CONFLUENCE_PAGE title='(2026x Refresh1) Working with columns' space=''] them ifno longer need.

Using **Show Parts**, you can easily filter connectors and choose which ones should be displayed in the table or hidden from it. In the **Show Parts** filter, you can:

- See all the connectors used in a defined context.
- Easier identify and multi-select the needed connectors (they are grouped by parts).
- Scroll through the list with the possibility to expand or collapse groups.
- Use the search field which allows to quickly find needed connectors.

To choose which connectors should be displayed or hidden

1. In the Whitebox ICD Table toolbar, click **Show Parts**. 
[IMAGE alt='' src='']
2. In the **Show Parts** dialog, select the connectors you want to hide or display.
3. Click **OK**.

- If the part is in a partially selected or empty state, clicking it selects all the connectors under that part. If the part is in fully selected state, clicking it cancels all the connector selections.
- The connectors are represented under both end parts, so if you choose the connector under one end, the second end will be selected as well. It works the same way when clearing the selection.

A Whitebox ICD Table consists of the following columns.

| Column name | Description |
| --- | --- |
| # | A row number. |
| Part A | Displays a Part that is connected with Part B. |
| Port A | Displays a Port that is connected with Port B. |
| Port A Features | Displays all the properties of the Port A. |
| Item Flow | Displays the flow kind and its direction which flows through the Connector between two Parts (Part A and Part B). |
| Port B | Displays a Port that is connected with Port A. |
| Port B Features | Displays all the properties of the Port B. |
| Part B | Displays a Part that is connected with Part A. |

### EXACT CONFLUENCE STORAGE SOURCE

````html
<p><span style="color:var(--ds-text,#333333);">The purpose of Whitebox ICD Table is to show how <ac:link><ri:page ri:space-key="MED" ri:content-title="Part Property" /><ac:plain-text-link-body><![CDATA[Part Properties]]></ac:plain-text-link-body></ac:link> are connected via ports/interfaces. This table collects all parts of the context element. Those parts are connected through the ports/interfaces using <ac:link><ri:page ri:space-key="MED" ri:content-title="Connector" /><ac:plain-text-link-body><![CDATA[Connectors]]></ac:plain-text-link-body></ac:link>. You can visually identify what kind of ports/interfaces (Port A and Port B) and flows (Item Flow) are used to connects two Part Properties (Part A and Part B). </span></p><p><span style="color:var(--ds-text,#000000);"><span style="color:var(--ds-text,#333333);">In the row 1 of the following figure, the<span style="color:var(--ds-text,#333333);"> </span><em>Controller</em> <span style="color:var(--ds-text,#000000);"><span style="color:var(--ds-text,#333333);">Part <span style="color:var(--ds-text,#333333);">Property</span></span></span> sends the <em>control</em> Signal to the <span style="color:var(--ds-text,#000000);"><span style="color:var(--ds-text,#333333);"><em>Boiler</em></span></span> Part <span style="color:var(--ds-text,#000000);"><span style="color:var(--ds-text,#333333);">Property</span> </span> through the ports <em>b</em> and <em>c</em>. The <em>Controller</em> receives the <em>Status</em> signal from the <em>Boiler</em> through the Ports <em>c</em> and <em>b</em>.</span><span style="color:var(--ds-text,#000000);"><span style="color:var(--ds-text,#333333);"> In the row 2,  the Part <em>Controller</em> sends the flow <em>Elec Power</em> to the Part <em>Boiler</em> through the Ports <em>bp</em> and <em>p in</em>.</span> <ac:link><ri:page ri:content-title="(2026x Refresh1) Creating Interface Control Document tables" /><ac:plain-text-link-body><![CDATA[Learn how to create and work in the Interface Control Documents >>]]></ac:plain-text-link-body></ac:link></span></span></p><p><span style="color:var(--ds-text,#000000);"> </span></p><h6 style="text-align: center;"><span style="color:var(--ds-text,#000000);"><ac:image ac:align="center" ac:title="Whitebox ICD Table" ac:alt="Whitebox ICD Table"><ri:attachment ri:filename="whitebox_icd_table.png"><ri:page ri:content-title="(2026x Refresh1) Whitebox ICD Table" /></ri:attachment></ac:image>An example of Whitebox ICD Table.</span></h6><p><span style="color:var(--ds-text,#000000);">In the created table, a row represents the Connector and the columns represent <span style="color:var(--ds-text,#333333);">features of that Connector. remove]]></ac:plain-text-link-body></ac:link> them if</span></span><span style="color:var(--ds-text,#333333);"> no longer need.</span></p><p><span style="color:var(--ds-text,#333333);"><span the <strong>Show Parts</strong> filter, you can:</span></p><ul><li><span style="color:var(--ds-text,#333333);">See all the connectors used in a defined context.</span></li><li><p>Easier identify and multi-select the needed connectors (they are grouped by parts).</p></li><li><span style="color:var(--ds-text,#333333);">Scroll through the list with the possibility to expand or collapse If the part is in fully selected state, clicking it cancels all the connector selections.</li><li>The connectors are represented under both end parts, so if you choose the connector under one end, the second end will be selected as well. It works the same way when clearing the selection.</li></ul></ac:rich-text-body></ac:structured-macro><p><span style="color:var(--ds-text,#000000);"> </span></p><p><span style="color:var(--ds-text,#333333);">A Whitebox ICD Table consists of the following columns.</span></p><table class="wrapped"><colgroup><col /><col /></colgroup><tbody><tr><th><span <!--NOMAGIC_PAGE id=303999496 space=MT version=1 -->
````
## PAGE 02076: (2026x Refresh1) WhiteSpace

- page_id: `303999496`
- space_key: `MT`
- source_url: https://docs.nomagic.com/spaces/MT/pages/303999496/2026x+Refresh1+WhiteSpace
- version_number: 1
- version_date: `2026-04-28T10:31:02.060+02:00`
- ancestors: Versions of Modeling Tools Documentation > (2026x Refresh1) Modeling Tools Documentation > (2026x Refresh1) Data-related modeling > (2026x Refresh1) XML schemas > (2026x Refresh1) XML schema mapping to UML elements
- labels: ['msosa']

### NORMALIZED CONTENT

Maps to a UML Attribute with the stereotype XSDwhiteSpace. The name and type of such an attribute do not make sense.

- value – to Attribute initial value.

#### PANEL: whiteSpace XML representation summary

whiteSpace XML representation summary

```text
<whiteSpace
```

```text
fixed = boolean : false
```

```text
id = ID
```

```text
value = (collapse | preserve | replace)
```

```text
{any attributes with non-schema namespace…}>
```

```text
Content: (annotation?)
```

```text
</whiteSpace>
```

#### PANEL: whiteSpace XML code sample

whiteSpace XML code sample

```text
The following example is the datatype definition for the token built-in derived datatype.
```

```text
<simpleType name='token'>
```

```text
<restriction base='normalizedString'>
```

```text
<whiteSpace value='collapse'/>
```

```text
</restriction>
```

```text
</simpleType>
```

### EXACT CONFLUENCE STORAGE SOURCE

````html
<p>Maps to a UML Attribute with the stereotype XSDwhiteSpace. The name and type of such an attribute do not make sense.</p><ul><li style="text-align: left;">value – to Attribute initial value.</li></ul><ac:structured-macro ac:name="panel" ac:schema-version="1" ac:macro-id="744d5a95-b52a-4dd5-9f9f-f43876ef51ff"><ac:parameter ac:name="title">whiteSpace XML representation summary</ac:parameter><ac:rich-text-body><pre>&lt;whiteSpace</pre><pre>	fixed = boolean : false</pre><pre>	id = ID</pre><pre>	value = (collapse | preserve | replace)</pre><pre>	{any attributes with non-schema namespace…}&gt;</pre><pre>	Content: (annotation?)</pre><pre>&lt;/whiteSpace&gt;</pre></ac:rich-text-body></ac:structured-macro><p class="auto-cursor-target"><br /></p><ac:structured-macro ac:name="panel" ac:schema-version="1" ac:macro-id="c4463701-752f-4a2f-88f6-a7ab5ac60fbf"><ac:parameter ac:name="title">whiteSpace XML code sample</ac:parameter><ac:rich-text-body><pre>The following example is the datatype definition for the token built-in derived datatype.</pre><pre>&lt;simpleType name='token'&gt;</pre><pre>	&lt;restriction base='normalizedString'&gt;</pre><pre>		&lt;whiteSpace value='collapse'/&gt;</pre><pre>	&lt;/restriction&gt;</pre><pre>&lt;/simpleType&gt;</pre></ac:rich-text-body></ac:structured-macro><p class="auto-cursor-target"><br /></p>
````
